sections.section_heading: INCREASE OF LIMITATION ON FEE FOR ARCHITECTURAL AND ENGINEERING SERVICES PROCURED BY MILITARY DEPARTMENTS.

manifests.description: Raises the statutory cap on the allowable fee for architectural and engineering services procured by the Department of the Army from 6 percent to 10 percent of the estimated project cost. This enables the Army to pay more competitive fees to external architectural and engineering firms for design work on public works and utility projects.
manifests.subject: Increase fee cap for Army architectural and engineering services
manifests.applies_to: Department of the Army, contracts for architectural and engineering services
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 236.606-70 should be revised to state the new 10 percent fee limitation for the Army.

manifests.description: Raises the statutory cap on the allowable fee for architectural and engineering services procured by the Department of the Navy (including the Marine Corps) from 6 percent to 10 percent of the estimated project cost. This adjustment helps the Navy to procure higher quality design work in a competitive market.
manifests.subject: Increase fee cap for Navy architectural and engineering services
manifests.applies_to: Department of the Navy (including Marine Corps), contracts for architectural and engineering services
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 236.606-70 should be revised to reflect a 10 percent fee limitation for the Navy.

manifests.description: Raises the statutory cap on the allowable fee for architectural and engineering services procured by the Department of the Air Force from 6 percent to 10 percent of the estimated project cost. This increases the Air Force’s flexibility to pay competitive fees to external firms for architectural and engineering projects.
manifests.subject: Increase fee cap for Air Force architectural and engineering services
manifests.applies_to: Department of the Air Force, contracts for architectural and engineering services
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 236.606-70 should be updated to raise the fee cap for the Air Force to 10 percent.

sections.section_heading: EXTENSION OF PILOT PROGRAM TO INCENTIVIZE CONTRACTING WITH EMPLOYEE-OWNED BUSINESSES.

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense not only to establish the pilot program for contracting with employee-owned businesses but also to prescribe implementing regulations. This ensures that formal rules will be set for operating the pilot program.
manifests.subject: Mandate for regulations for pilot program
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and contracting officers implementing the pilot program for employee-owned businesses
manifests.conditions: When implementing and operating the pilot program under section 874
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add regulatory implementation language referencing the pilot program for contracting with employee-owned businesses.

manifests.description: Expands the scope of permissible sole-source follow-on contracts under the pilot program to explicitly include both products and services, and clarifies that either may be procured through noncompetitive procedures when criteria are met.
manifests.subject: Clarification of products and services coverage
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting for follow-on contracts with qualifying employee-owned businesses
manifests.conditions: For follow-on contracts under the pilot program where criteria are met
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Clarify that both products and services may be the subject of noncompetitive contracts under this pilot.

manifests.description: Clarifies the limitation so that each contract held by a qualified employee-owned business is eligible for a sole-source follow-on contract under the pilot, not just one per business. This allows multiple follow-on contracts if there are multiple qualifying prior contracts.
manifests.subject: Contract-by-contract eligibility for sole-source follow-ons
manifests.applies_to: Qualified employee-owned businesses seeking follow-on contracts
manifests.conditions: For each contract previously held under the pilot by a qualifying business
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Specify in DFARS that the sole-source follow-on limitation is applied per contract, not per business.
open_questions: If a business holds multiple qualifying contracts during overlapping periods, does each contract allow a separate opportunity for a sole-source follow-on?

manifests.description: Modifies the rule limiting subcontracting under the program to 50 percent of contract value by adding exceptions: (A) if the excess is subcontracted to other qualifying employee-owned businesses, (B) for product contracts where excess is for materials not available from another qualifying business, and (C) retains allowance for waivers. This makes compliance more flexible and encourages use of employee-owned supply chains.
manifests.subject: Expanded exceptions to subcontracting cap
manifests.applies_to: Qualified employee-owned businesses and their contracts under the pilot
manifests.conditions: Contracts under the pilot program subject to the 50% subcontracting cap
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Amend DFARS to reflect the detailed exceptions to the subcontracting percentage cap for this pilot.
open_questions: How will 'materials not available' be defined and what evidence or process will be required to claim this exception?

manifests.description: Extends the expiration date for the pilot program from five years to eight years after the date of enactment of the Fiscal Year 2022 NDAA, giving the program additional operational time.
manifests.subject: Extended sunset of pilot program
manifests.applies_to: Any pilot program established under Section 874
manifests.conditions: Effective for determining expiration of a pilot program initiated under Section 874
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS to reference the new expiration date for the pilot program.

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: Deadline. Requirements. Contracts. 10 USC 3301 note.>> CONSIDERATION OF THE PAST PERFORMANCE OF AFFILIATE COMPANIES OF SMALL BUSINESS CONCERNS.

manifests.description: The Secretary of Defense must update DFARS section 215.305 (or any successor rule) before July 1, 2024, to require that when small businesses bid on Department of Defense contracts, the evaluation of past performance and source selection process must consider, if relevant, the past performance of the small business's affiliate companies. This ensures that affiliate performance is taken into account when it pertains to the contract, not just the performance of the small business itself.
manifests.subject: Consideration of affiliate companies' past performance for small business DoD contract bids
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers and source selection officials for small business bids
manifests.conditions: Applies to past performance evaluation and source selection for DoD contract bids from small businesses; affiliate company's performance must be relevant to the contract
manifests.effective_date: No later than July 1, 2024
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add language to DFARS 215.305 requiring past performance of relevant affiliate companies of small businesses be considered in past performance evaluations and source selection for DoD contracts. Clarify definition of 'affiliate' and standards for determining relevancy.
open_questions: Should the definition of 'affiliate' follow SBA standards at 13 CFR 121.103?
What documentation is required to establish relevance of an affiliate's past performance?
How should positive vs. negative affiliate past performance be weighted, and what is the applicable time frame?
Does this apply only to new solicitations after the effective date, or also to open ones as of July 1, 2024?

sections.section_heading: MODIFICATION OF EFFECTIVE DATE FOR EXPANSION ON THE PROHIBITION ON ACQUIRING CERTAIN METAL PRODUCTS.

manifests.description: Extends the effective date for when the expansion of the prohibition on acquiring certain metal products by the Department of Defense takes effect, changing the delay from 5 years to 6 years after the enactment of the FY21 NDAA. The purpose is to give suppliers and the government additional time to comply with the new requirement.
manifests.subject: Effective date extension for metal products acquisition prohibition
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense acquisition of covered metal products
manifests.conditions: Amendment applies to the effective date set in section 844(b) of the FY21 NDAA (Public Law 116-283); triggers 6 years from enactment of that Act instead of 5 years.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS should update any references to the compliance effective date for the expanded prohibition on acquiring covered metal products to reflect a 6-year delay instead of 5 years.
open_questions: Are there any existing DFARS clauses that set compliance timelines for 10 U.S.C. 4872/2533c metal product restrictions that need to be updated to match this new effective date?

sections.section_heading: MODIFICATIONS TO THE PROCUREMENT TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE PROGRAM.

manifests.description: Removes the requirement that eligible nonprofit organizations be specifically 'private' in order to participate in the Procurement Technical Assistance Program. Now, any nonprofit organization (public or private) is eligible, broadening the scope of entities that can participate.
manifests.subject: Nonprofit Organization Eligibility
manifests.applies_to: Nonprofit organizations seeking to participate in the Procurement Technical Assistance Program
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise DFARS sections referencing eligibility criteria for nonprofit organizations under the Procurement Technical Assistance Program to eliminate the 'private' requirement.

manifests.description: Defines 'business entity' in statute to explicitly include corporations, associations, partnerships, LLCs, LLPs, consortia, not-for-profits, and other legal entities. This provides clarity on which organizations may receive procurement technical assistance.
manifests.subject: Definition of Business Entity
manifests.applies_to: All references to business entities receiving services or participating in the program
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Reference or incorporate the statutory definition of 'business entity' in DFARS guidance related to this program.

manifests.description: Allows the Secretary of Defense to waive or modify, on a case-by-case basis, the statutory percentage limits for the federal cost share in cooperative agreements under the Procurement Technical Assistance Program, if determined to be in the best interest of the program.
manifests.subject: Cost Share Waiver Authority
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; eligible entities seeking cost share adjustment
manifests.conditions: Determination by Secretary that waiver or modification is in the best interest of the program
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS sections regulating cooperative agreements and federal cost share to allow for case-by-case waiver/modification by the Secretary, including documentation requirements for such determinations.
open_questions: What internal standards or guidelines will be used to determine when a waiver or modification is 'in the best interest of the program'?

manifests.description: Deletes the requirement that at least one Procurement Technical Assistance Program operate in each Department of Defense contract administration services district each fiscal year, thereby removing a specific geographic distribution mandate.
manifests.subject: Removal of District-Level Program Requirement
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense procurement technical assistance program planning
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove references within DFARS to the requirement for at least one program per contract administration services district.

manifests.description: Authorizes the Secretary of Defense to waive the restrictions on the percentage of eligible costs covered by the Procurement Technical Assistance Program as a whole, if it is determined to be in the Federal Government's best interest, with a requirement for the Secretary to submit a written justification to the congressional defense committees when such a waiver is exercised.
manifests.subject: Program-Level Cost Share Waiver and Justification Requirement
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; overall program financial management
manifests.conditions: Written determination that waiver is in the best interest of the Federal Government; written justification submitted to committees
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS coverage should address process and documentation for program-level cost share waivers and committee notification.
open_questions: Format and required detail for justification to congressional committees is unspecified—guidance may be needed.

manifests.description: Expands the types of permissible technical assistance that may be provided by eligible entities to include education and compliance support related to: (1) Department of Defense cybersecurity requirements under DFARS 252.204-7012 (and its successors), and (2) foreign ownership, control, or influence (FOCI) requirements under section 847 of the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2020 and its successors.
manifests.subject: Expanded Technical Assistance Scope—Cybersecurity and FOCI
manifests.applies_to: Eligible entities providing technical assistance; businesses receiving assistance
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure technical assistance programs referenced in DFARS explicitly include DFARS clause 252.204-7012 and NDAA 2020 Section 847 compliance as eligible subject matter.

sections.section_heading: REFORM OF THE STRATEGIC AND CRITICAL MATERIALS STOCK PILING ACT.

manifests.description: Eliminate the Strategic Materials Protection Board and remove all its statutory functions, authorities, and requirements.
manifests.subject: Repeal of Strategic Materials Protection Board
manifests.applies_to: Strategic Materials Protection Board and its associated functions and references
manifests.effective_date: December 23, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove all DFARS references to the Strategic Materials Protection Board and replace with new Board where appropriate.

manifests.description: Establish a new Strategic and Critical Materials Board of Directors responsible for oversight and governance of the National Defense Stockpile, including defining Board structure and membership requirements.
manifests.subject: Establishment of Board of Directors
manifests.applies_to: National Defense Stockpile program governance
manifests.effective_date: December 23, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add Board of Directors authority and structure to DFARS parts regulating strategic materials management (e.g., 225, 237).
open_questions: Should DFARS codify procedures for Board meetings and actions, or just reference the statutory authority?

manifests.description: Specify the Board’s required composition—Senior Defense official as chair, senior representatives from key agencies, designated Congressional Armed Services committee members, and additional experts.
manifests.subject: Board Membership and Expertise
manifests.applies_to: Strategic and Critical Materials Board of Directors
manifests.effective_date: December 23, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Direct DoD and associated agencies to assign appropriate representatives for Board participation. Reference in DFARS for appointment/consultation procedures.
open_questions: Will participating agencies require formal appointment processes or existing staff assignments?

manifests.description: Assign the Board new, non-delegable duties including performance oversight, advising, annual reviews, approving budgets and plans, setting manager compensation, fiscal oversight, and strategic recommendations.
manifests.subject: Board Duties and Powers
manifests.applies_to: Strategic and Critical Materials Board of Directors
manifests.effective_date: December 23, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update relevant DFARS parts to require Board approval or advice for budget, operations plan, and manager evaluations.
open_questions: Will every Stockpile action require Board approval, or only those specified in by-laws? Should this be clarified in DFARS procedures?

manifests.description: Exempt the Board from Section 14 of the Federal Advisory Committee Act, meaning it is not subject to automatic termination or related procedures.
manifests.subject: FACA Section 14 Exemption
manifests.applies_to: Strategic and Critical Materials Board of Directors
manifests.effective_date: December 23, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: State FACA exemptions in DFARS coverage of Board oversight or creation.

manifests.description: Update statutory definitions for 'materials critical to national security', 'military equipment', and 'secure supply' as they apply to the Stock Piling Act.
manifests.subject: Updated Definitions
manifests.applies_to: All programs and policies under Strategic and Critical Materials Stock Piling Act
manifests.effective_date: December 23, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise DFARS definitions section(s) for strategic and critical materials to incorporate these updates.
open_questions: Should DFARS provide explanatory linkages if definitions differ from those used in other supply chain areas?

manifests.description: Impose a requirement that the National Defense Stockpile Manager submits quarterly unaudited financial reports, an annual audited financial statement, and an Annual Materials and Operations Plan to the Board, each on a strict timeline.
manifests.subject: Manager Reports to Board
manifests.applies_to: National Defense Stockpile Manager
manifests.effective_date: December 23, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Implement reporting schedule and manager obligations in relevant DFARS parts for strategic materials inventory management.
open_questions: What enforcement mechanism applies if deadlines are missed?

manifests.description: Require the National Defense Stockpile Manager to report detailed information annually to Congressional defense committees, including procurement, disposal, R&D, finances, and planned future actions, with the Board’s report attached.
manifests.subject: Manager and Board Reports to Congress
manifests.applies_to: National Defense Stockpile Manager and Board
manifests.conditions: No later than 90 days after the fiscal year ends
manifests.effective_date: December 23, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update reporting guidance in DFARS for compliance support regarding required Congressional reports.
open_questions: Must DFARS suppliers or contractors submit data in a specific format for the statutory reports?

manifests.description: Require the Board to prepare an annual written report detailing its activities and the most recent Annual Materials and Operations Plan, to accompany the Manager's report to Congress.
manifests.subject: Board Annual Report to Congress
manifests.applies_to: Strategic and Critical Materials Board of Directors
manifests.conditions: Annually, as part of Congress reporting cycle
manifests.effective_date: December 23, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS reporting references should anticipate this additional required Board comment; update guidance as necessary.
open_questions: How will Board disagreements on strategic recommendations or plan content be resolved in the report?

manifests.description: Make technical and conforming amendments throughout the Stock Piling Act to update outdated terms, ensure coherence with new reporting structures and to align plan/report naming conventions and authorities.
manifests.subject: Technical and Conforming Amendments (Stock Piling Act)
manifests.applies_to: All program sections and procedures in Stock Piling Act
manifests.effective_date: December 23, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update all DFARS cross-references to Stock Piling Act to reflect new agencies, plans, and Board terminology.
open_questions: Are there legacy contracts or plans that use the old terminology or reference old Board authorities?

manifests.description: Update the Defense acquisition statutes (10 U.S.C. 4863 and 4872) to replace references to the repealed Strategic Materials Protection Board with references to the new Board of Directors, making the Board responsible for recommendations and determinations relating to critical materials procurement from U.S. or non-allied sources.
manifests.subject: Conforming Amendments to Title 10 (Acquisition Statutes)
manifests.applies_to: DoD procurement policy and specialty materials acquisition authorities
manifests.effective_date: December 23, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 225.7003, 225.7018, and related parts to refer all relevant Board recommendations, reviews, or exceptions to the new Board of Directors.
open_questions: Should DFARS clarify procedures for pending Board recommendations transitioning from the old to the new Board?

sections.section_heading: CODIFICATION OF THE DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE MENTOR-PROTEGE PROGRAM.

manifests.description: Transfers the Department of Defense Mentor-Protege Program from a temporary, 'pilot' status to a permanent codified program at 10 U.S.C. 4902, establishing clear statutory authority and permanence for the program.
manifests.subject: Permanent Codification of Mentor-Protege Program
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense, current and future Mentor-Protege Program participants
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS Subpart 219.71 and Appendix I references from 'pilot' to 10 U.S.C. 4902; review all references to reflect permanent codification.

manifests.description: Strikes references to 'pilot' in section titles, headings, and in the text, updating the program’s language everywhere to refer to it as the permanent 'Department of Defense Mentor-Protege Program'.
manifests.subject: Terminology Update—Permanent Program Naming
manifests.applies_to: Statutory and regulatory references to the program
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise DFARS 219.71 and Appendix I to use new naming; remove references to 'pilot.'

manifests.description: Lowers the eligibility threshold for a contractor to qualify as a mentor firm from $100,000,000 to $25,000,000 in qualifying Department of Defense contract awards in the prior fiscal year.
manifests.subject: Mentor Firm Eligibility Threshold
manifests.applies_to: Potential mentor firms under the Mentor-Protege Program
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update eligibility thresholds in DFARS 219.71 and Appendix I.

manifests.description: Corrects statutory cross-references associated with eligibility and reimbursement, ensuring they point to the accurate subsections after reorganization.
manifests.subject: Cross-Reference Corrections
manifests.applies_to: Users of 10 U.S.C. 4902
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure DFARS/Appendix I cross-references are updated accordingly.

manifests.description: Extends maximum allowable duration of a mentor-protege agreement from two years to three years by default, with a provision for a five-year period if justified by the Secretary.
manifests.subject: Mentor-Protege Agreement Duration
manifests.applies_to: Mentor and protege firms entering new agreements
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS/Appendix I for new timeline.

manifests.description: Expands permitted forms of assistance by explicitly including 'manufacturing, test and evaluation' within developmental areas mentors can support.
manifests.subject: Expanded Mentor Assistance Areas
manifests.applies_to: Mentor-protege agreements
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Clarify eligibility of these activities in DFARS/Appendix I.
open_questions: Are further clarifying examples or definitions needed for these activities in implementing regulations?

manifests.description: Updates references for procurement technical assistance to chapter 388 of Title 10, reflecting the recodification of authorities.
manifests.subject: Reference Update for Procurement Technical Assistance
manifests.applies_to: All relevant cross-references in program guidance
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS and forms to match current code.

manifests.description: Removes now-obsolete subsections dealing with sunset provisions and duplicate reporting/goal requirements.
manifests.subject: Sunset and Reporting Provisions Removal
manifests.applies_to: Older regulatory and statutory text
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Delete obsolete references in DFARS/Appendix I.

manifests.description: Renumbers several subsections (k–o) in 10 U.S.C. 4902 to match the new organization. Ensures all cross-references within law and regulation remain accurate.
manifests.subject: Subsection Renumbering
manifests.applies_to: Statutory and regulatory cross-references to the program
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update all references/crosswalks in DFARS/Appendix I.

manifests.description: Amends program procedures to clarify both parties—not just mentor firms—can terminate mentor-protege agreements, and removes obsolete requirements for Secretary to publish regulations on a set schedule.
manifests.subject: Agreement Termination and Regulatory Timing
manifests.applies_to: Parties to mentor-protege agreements, DOD
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS should state parties (not just mentors) may initiate termination; remove obsolete rulemaking deadlines.

manifests.description: Requires the Director of the Office of Small Business Programs to annually collect outcome-based performance data (preferably automated) and to conduct an independent review of the Mentor-Protege Program at least once every three years.
manifests.subject: Performance Data Collection and Program Review
manifests.applies_to: Office of Small Business Programs/Administration
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Implement explicit, recurring data/reporting requirement; specify method for independent review.
open_questions: Does 'independent review' require an external (non-DOD) organization? What metrics qualify as 'outcome-based'?

manifests.description: Substantively updates and harmonizes statutory definitions for affiliation, disadvantaged small business concern, historically Black colleges and universities, minority institutions, HUBZone small business concerns, and other related terms, aligning them tightly with the Small Business Act and relevant CFR references.
manifests.subject: Definitions - Modernization and Alignment
manifests.applies_to: All users of Mentor-Protege Program statute and implementing regulations
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure all DFARS/Appendix I definitions match statute.

manifests.description: Conforming and clerical amendments to the Buy Indian Act and Small Business Act, updating references to the Mentor-Protege Program’s location (from NDAA 1991/old Public Law to 10 U.S.C. 4902), and updating cross-references to conform to new structure.
manifests.subject: Conforming Amendments in Cross-Referenced Laws
manifests.applies_to: Statutory cross-references—Buy Indian Act, Small Business Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update cross-references wherever used in DOD acquisition regulation.

manifests.description: Establishes a new five-year 'Protege Technical Reimbursement Pilot Program,' to be set up by July 1, 2023, under which protege firms can receive up to 25% of the otherwise allowable reimbursement for certain technical activities that advance integration of their technologies into DOD systems.
manifests.subject: Protege Technical Reimbursement Pilot Program
manifests.applies_to: Protege firms and mentors under the pilot
manifests.conditions: Covers engineering, software development, or manufacturing customization activities for DOD integration only; up to 25% of eligible reimbursement amount; program sunsets after five years.
manifests.effective_date: To be established by July 1, 2023; runs for five years from establishment date
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS should create procedures and guidance for OSBP and eligible protege firms, including payment mechanics and eligibility for covered activities.
open_questions: How will DOD validate that activities are 'covered' for partial reimbursement?
What evidence/procedure will be used to process the reimbursement and ensure non-duplication with mentor payments?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to issue implementing regulations for 10 U.S.C. 4902, as amended, no later than December 31, 2023.
manifests.subject: Mandatory Rulemaking Timeline
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and rulemaking offices
manifests.effective_date: Regulations due December 31, 2023
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Final rule to be issued by deadline; ensure DFARS/Appendix I are merged/updated as appropriate.

manifests.description: Creates a grandfather clause: all amendments here do NOT apply to agreements entered before the enactment of this section; such agreements stay governed by prior law until completion.
manifests.subject: Grandfathering Old Agreements
manifests.applies_to: All agreements under Mentor-Protege Program entered before enactment date
manifests.conditions: Prior agreements signed under pre-2023 law and rules
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS and contracting guidance must clarify distinction between legacy and new agreements.

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: China>> CODIFICATION OF PROHIBITION ON CERTAIN PROCUREMENTS FROM THE XINJIANG UYGHUR AUTONOMOUS REGION.

manifests.description: Repeals section 848 of the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2022, which had temporarily prohibited Department of Defense procurement of products involving forced labor from the Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region (XUAR). The prior requirements for offeror certification and rulemaking are superseded by the new permanent, codified prohibition.
manifests.subject: Repeal of temporary XUAR procurement prohibition
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense procurement actions previously governed by NDAA FY22 §848
manifests.effective_date: On enactment of this Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove DFARS provisions that implement or refer to NDAA FY22 §848; ensure references point to 10 U.S.C. 4661.

manifests.description: Establishes a permanent, codified prohibition (10 U.S.C. 4661) barring DoD from knowingly using any funds to procure products mined, produced, or manufactured wholly or in part by forced labor from XUAR or from entities participating in certain XUAR labor programs. Definitions of 'forced labor' and 'XUAR' are tied to 10 U.S.C. 2496.
manifests.subject: Permanent prohibition on XUAR procurement with forced labor
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting and procurement of products
manifests.conditions: Applies to products involving forced labor from XUAR, including 'poverty alleviation' or 'pairing assistance' program labor
manifests.effective_date: On enactment of this Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 225.7022, 252.225-7059, and 252.225-7060 already address XUAR forced labor prohibitions. Review and confirm all covered contracts and subcontracts comply with the new codified requirement.

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense, within 180 days, to issue a policy mandating that offerors or awardees of DoD contracts make a good faith effort to determine that forced labor from XUAR will not be used in contract performance, as defined in 10 U.S.C. 4661.
manifests.subject: Policy on contractor certification of good faith effort
manifests.applies_to: Offerors and awardees of Department of Defense contracts
manifests.conditions: Policy to be issued within 180 days of enactment
manifests.effective_date: Policy due within 180 days after enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 252.225-7060 and 252.225-7059 address representation and efforts. Confirm current clauses' language and timing match new policy requirements.
open_questions: Should the 'good faith effort' policy requirements be substantively identical to the repealed FY22 language, or are any elements intentionally altered under the new statute?

manifests.description: Adds section 4661, 'Prohibition on certain procurements from the Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region,' to the table of contents for Chapter 363 of Title 10.
manifests.subject: Clerical codification of new statutory section
manifests.applies_to: Title 10, U.S. Code
manifests.effective_date: On enactment of this Act

sections.section_heading: REQUIREMENTS FOR THE PROCUREMENT OF CERTAIN COMPONENTS FOR CERTAIN NAVAL VESSELS AND AUXILIARY SHIPS.

manifests.description: Relabels the 'Implementation of auxiliary ship component limitation' subsection in 10 U.S.C. 4864 from (l) to (k). This is a technical realignment to accommodate a newly added subsection, with no substantive policy impact.
manifests.subject: Technical amendment: subsection relabeling
manifests.applies_to: Legal cross-references and implementers using 10 U.S.C. 4864
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS only if existing clauses cross-reference the relabeled subsection; otherwise, no substantive impact.

manifests.description: Expands U.S.-manufacturing component requirements previously applicable to T-AO 205 class vessels to also cover T-ARC class vessels. Specifies that the listed components (auxiliary equipment, propulsion components, cranes, and spreaders) for both T-AO 205 and T-ARC ships must be sourced from the national technology and industrial base.
manifests.subject: Expansion of covered auxiliary ship classes
manifests.applies_to: Contracts for procurement of listed components for T-ARC class vessels, in addition to T-AO 205
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 225.7010 and related solicitation clauses (e.g., 252.225-7062) so restrictions apply to both T-AO 205 and T-ARC auxiliary ships.

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to issue regulations by June 1, 2023, implementing the process for analyzing and considering items for required procurement from the national technology and industrial base, as specified in 10 U.S.C. 4864(j). This ensures a transparent, formalized application and review process.
manifests.subject: Deadline for implementing regulations on sourcing process
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense/DoD acquisition policy officials
manifests.effective_date: June 1, 2023
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS/DoD policy must establish procedures for the application/review process described in 10 U.S.C. 4864(j) by the deadline.
open_questions: Has DoD already issued these regulations as of the effective date? If not, what interim processes are in place?

sections.section_heading: MODIFICATION TO THE NATIONAL TECHNOLOGY AND INDUSTRIAL BASE.

manifests.description: This change expands the definition of 'national technology and industrial base' in 10 U.S.C. 4801(1) to include 'New Zealand.' This means that persons and organizations in New Zealand are now officially considered part of the national technology and industrial base for defense purposes, which will allow them to participate in related U.S. defense acquisition programs, research, and technology cooperation similarly to those from the United States, United Kingdom, Australia, and Canada.
manifests.subject: Include New Zealand in the national technology and industrial base definition
manifests.applies_to: Persons and organizations engaged in relevant activities in New Zealand; federal agencies referencing the national technology and industrial base
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS definitions (see 225.003) and any DFARS provisions that implement the 'national technology and industrial base' definition to include New Zealand.
open_questions: Do any specific acquisition programs, waivers, or exceptions that reference the national technology and industrial base require review to prevent unintended consequences from this definition change?
Does the addition of New Zealand require updates or clarifications with respect to ITAR/EAR compliance for technology transfers involving New Zealand?
Are there any country-specific agreements referenced in DFARS that require corresponding updates?

sections.section_heading: MODIFICATION OF AUTHORITY OF THE DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE TO CARRY OUT CERTAIN PROTOTYPE PROJECTS.

manifests.description: Removes the explicit inclusion of follow-on production contracts or transactions (awarded under subsection (f)) from the general approval and notification requirements outlined for high-dollar prototype projects in subsection (a)(2). This clarifies that follow-on production awards are subject to distinct requirements rather than being grouped with initial prototype project transactions.
manifests.subject: Separation of authority for follow-on productions and prototype projects
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense officials exercising authority under 10 U.S.C. 4022(a)(2)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 206.001-70 and PGI to distinguish approval and notification requirements between prototype projects and follow-on production transactions.
open_questions: Should the DFARS be amended to provide more explicit implementation guidance for these distinct authorities and thresholds?

manifests.description: Establishes a new approval and notification process for follow-on production transactions awarded under subsection (f) and expected to cost over $100 million. A 'covered official' must determine in writing that subsection (d) requirements are met and that use is essential for critical national security objectives, and must notify the congressional defense committees in writing at the time the authority is exercised.
manifests.subject: High-value follow-on production approval and notification process
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense when awarding follow-on production transactions over $100M
manifests.conditions: Applies only to follow-on production contracts or transactions over $100M under subsection (f)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add detailed DFARS procedures for 'covered official' written determinations and immediate congressional notification for follow-on productions over $100M.
open_questions: Should DoD use a standard template for these notifications and determinations?

manifests.description: Clarifies and expands definitions in 10 U.S.C. 4022(e), formally defining 'covered official' (including service acquisition executives, Directors of DARPA and MDA, and the Under Secretaries for A&S and R&E) and providing an explicit reference for 'service acquisition executive' by citing 10 U.S.C. 101(a).
manifests.subject: Clarification of covered official and service acquisition executive definitions
manifests.applies_to: Statutory authorizations and delegations under 10 U.S.C. 4022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS parts that reference prototype authority to clearly align with these definitions.
open_questions: Are there components or officials not listed here who might assert or require approval authority under these changes?

manifests.description: Expands the authority for awarding follow-on production contracts or transactions without competition, making clear that these awards are excepted from the requirements of the entirety of chapter 221 of Title 10 (not just section 2304) and are permitted even if the request for proposals did not explicitly include a notification of this authority.
manifests.subject: Broadened authority for follow-on production without competition
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts for follow-on production under 10 U.S.C. 4022(f)(2)
manifests.conditions: When competitive procedures were used for the original prototype selection and the project was successfully completed
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise DFARS 206.001-70 and related procedural text to update statutory citations and clarify policy regarding RFP notification.
open_questions: Is there a DFARS/DoD preference on including or omitting explicit follow-on notification in solicitations, or is this left to contracting officer judgment?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: Deadlines.>> USE OF FIXED-PRICE TYPE CONTRACTS FOR CERTAIN MAJOR DEFENSE ACQUISITION PROGRAMS.

manifests.description: Limits the number of low-rate initial production (LRIP) lots for a major defense acquisition program to one, if at the time of Milestone B approval the milestone decision authority authorizes a fixed-price type contract for both development and LRIP. This aims to prevent excessive dividing of LRIP when using a fixed-price structure and to encourage programs to transition earlier to further production phases.
manifests.subject: Limit on LRIP lots under fixed-price contracts
manifests.applies_to: Major defense acquisition programs using a fixed-price type contract at Milestone B for both development and LRIP.
manifests.conditions: Applies if a fixed-price type contract is authorized at Milestone B and the contract covers both development and LRIP.
amends: Section 818 of the John Warner NDAA for FY2007 (10 U.S.C. note prec. 4231)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Amend or supplement DFARS 234.004 and/or 235.006 to set limitation on number of LRIP lots for covered programs with fixed-price contracts at Milestone B.
open_questions: Is 'lot' universally defined across DoD programs, or is further policy clarification required for enforcement?
Does this require all LRIP items to be structured as a single lot in contract options or CLINs?

manifests.description: Establishes a waiver process for the LRIP lot limitation, providing that the service acquisition executive (or a non-contracting-officer designee) may waive the one-lot rule, but must notify congressional defense committees in writing with rationale within 30 days. This ensures oversight and maintains an exception process for unique program needs.
manifests.subject: Waiver process for LRIP one-lot limit
manifests.applies_to: Service acquisition executives and their designees (excluding contracting officers) responsible for waivers.
manifests.conditions: May only be exercised if not delegated to the contracting officer and notification requirements to Congress are followed.
amends: Section 818 of the John Warner NDAA for FY2007 (10 U.S.C. note prec. 4231)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add explicit waiver process in DFARS for the one-lot restriction, with required notification to congressional defense committees and a record-keeping mechanism.
open_questions: Should the notification format and content be standardized Department-wide?

manifests.description: Mandates use of standardized statutory definitions for 'low-rate initial production,' 'milestone decision authority,' 'major defense acquisition program,' and 'Milestone B approval' (referencing relevant sections in Title 10) for any rules or policy implementing the new LRIP limitation.
manifests.subject: Definitions for LRIP limitation policy
manifests.applies_to: All parties interpreting or applying the LRIP limitation and waiver rules.
amends: Section 818 of the John Warner NDAA for FY2007 (10 U.S.C. note prec. 4231)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Insert explicit references to Title 10 definitions in any DFARS section implementing this limitation.

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to revise the DFARS and any other relevant DoD regulations, within 120 days of enactment, to implement the LRIP limitation, waiver process, and updated definitions regarding use of fixed-price type contracts for major defense acquisition programs.
manifests.subject: DFARS and regulation update for new LRIP requirements
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and DoD acquisition regulatory bodies.
manifests.conditions: Action must be completed within 120 days after the date of enactment.
manifests.effective_date: 120 days after enactment of the Act
amends: Any other relevant DFARS or DoD acquisition regulations
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Issue DFARS case (interim/final rule) and any necessary class deviation. Cascade changes to service supplements as required.
open_questions: Should similar updates be triggered in service-specific or component regulations (e.g., NMCARS, AFARS)?
Are updates to DoDI 5000.85 or other DODIs required as well?

sections.section_heading: DATA REQUIREMENTS FOR COMMERCIAL PRODUCTS FOR MAJOR WEAPON SYSTEMS.

manifests.description: Formalizes detailed data and documentation requirements that offerors must provide when proposing a subsystem of a major weapon system as a 'commercial product' for the first time. The offeror must identify a comparable commercial product sold to the general public or nongovernmental entities, submit a physical and functional comparison to justify the 'of a type' assertion, and provide the National Stock Number for both items, if available. If the offeror does not sell a comparable item for non-governmental use, they must notify the contracting officer and provide the most comparable information available.
manifests.subject: Data requirements for subsystems proposed as commercial
manifests.applies_to: Offerors proposing subsystems of major weapon systems as commercial products
manifests.conditions: Applies if the subsystem has not been previously determined commercial under 10 U.S.C. 3703(d)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 234.7002 and PGI to include mandatory documentation, comparison, and identification requirements for subsystems claimed as commercial.
open_questions: What constitutes the 'most comparable commercial product' when significant differences exist?
What is the offeror's actual duty to investigate comparable products ('to the extent reasonably known')?
Is there a standardized format for the physical/functionality comparison?

manifests.description: Imposes parallel data and documentation requirements on offerors proposing components or spare parts for major weapon systems as 'commercial products'. The offeror must identify a comparable product, submit a comparison supporting the 'of a type' assertion, and provide relevant National Stock Numbers, unless they do not sell a comparable commercial product for non-governmental purposes, in which case they must notify the contracting officer and provide the most similar comparison reasonably known.
manifests.subject: Data requirements for components and spare parts proposed as commercial
manifests.applies_to: Offerors proposing components or spare parts of major weapon systems as commercial products
manifests.conditions: Applies if the component or spare part has not been previously determined commercial under 10 U.S.C. 3703(d)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 234.7002 and 244.303 to address these data and comparison requirements for commercial item claims involving components and spare parts.
open_questions: How should 'most comparable' be interpreted when no close analogy exists?
Does this cover all value-added or reseller tier cases?

manifests.description: Revises procurement data obligations for price reasonableness determinations. The offeror, rather than only when required by the contracting officer, must submit or provide access to a representative sample of prices (and terms and conditions) paid for the same or similar commercial products. If insufficient, a contracting officer with higher-level approval may require additional supporting information. The offeror may redact customer identities from this data, with written certification of customer type. This clarification includes explicit procedural protections and certification requirements.
manifests.subject: Offeror data submission and redaction for price reasonableness in commercial product procurements
manifests.applies_to: Offerors for major weapon system subsystems, components, and spare parts proposed as commercial products
manifests.conditions: Applies to items not covered by exceptions in 10 U.S.C. 3703(a)(1)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 215.408 and relevant PGIs to align data submission triggers, the representative sample requirement, and redaction/certification procedures.
open_questions: Is there a required or default standard for what constitutes a 'representative sample'?
Does the contracting officer now have to formally document rationale for the sample selected?

manifests.description: Adds a new 'Applicability' subsection, clarifying that the commerciality determination and data requirements for subsystems, components, and spare parts only apply to those acquired via a prime contract, a contract modification, or a covered subcontract (where DoD acquires a not-previously-determined-commercial product as commercial).
manifests.subject: Scope and applicability of data requirements for commercial item determinations
manifests.applies_to: All parties involved in procurements of major weapon system subsystems, components, and spare parts as commercial products
manifests.conditions: Applies only to actions via prime contracts, modifications thereto, or certain subcontracts as described
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 234.7000 et seq. to specify the contract types and tiers to which these requirements apply.
open_questions: How does this interact with flow-down obligations and contracts with multi-tier resellers?
Will this affect unresolved prior commerciality claims at the subcontract level?

sections.section_heading: TASK AND DELIVERY ORDER CONTRACTING FOR ARCHITECTURAL AND ENGINEERING SERVICES.

manifests.description: Requires that all task or delivery orders for architectural and engineering (A&E) services issued under 10 U.S.C. 3403 or 3405 must use qualification-based selection procedures described in chapter 11 of title 40 (the Brooks Act). This means that A&E task and delivery orders must be awarded based on the competence and qualifications of the firms, not on price competition. The goal is to ensure consistent application of Brooks Act principles to all A&E orders, not just stand-alone contracts.
manifests.subject: Qualification-based selection for A&E task/delivery orders
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers (and agency heads) issuing task or delivery orders for architectural and engineering services under 10 U.S.C. 3403 or 3405
manifests.conditions: Orders for architectural and engineering services, whether under single or multiple award contracts, under 10 U.S.C. 3403 or 3405
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 236.6 and DFARS 236.602 should be updated to require that orders under A&E IDIQ contracts use Brooks Act qualification-based procedures.
open_questions: Does this requirement apply to both the base contract award and each subsequent order, or just the latter?
Are there task orders for A&E under authorities other than 3403/3405 that would fall outside this rule?
How should existing DFARS pricing mechanisms at IDIQ level interact with qualification-based order selections?

manifests.description: Prohibits agency heads from routinely requesting additional information on qualifications from contractors who already hold a multiple award contract for architectural and engineering services when issuing a task or delivery order under the contract. This is intended to reduce redundant paperwork and streamline task order competition among already qualified A&E firms.
manifests.subject: Prohibition on routine qualification re-evaluation for A&E orders under multiple award contracts
manifests.applies_to: Contracting agencies and officers issuing task or delivery orders for architectural and engineering services under a multiple award contract
manifests.conditions: Order is for A&E services under a multiple award contract
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: A new paragraph in DFARS 236.6 or DFARS 236.602 should prescribe that additional qualification information is generally not to be requested at the order stage for multiple award A&E contracts.
open_questions: What is considered a 'routine' request for qualifications, and in what circumstances may additional information be properly requested?
For unique or specialized task orders, can updated or supplemental qualifications be requested on a non-routine basis?
How does this prohibition interact with requirements for fair opportunity and unique order requirements among multiple-award contract holders?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 3204 note.>> PILOT PROGRAM TO INCENTIVIZE CONTRACTING WITH EMPLOYEE- OWNED BUSINESSES.

manifests.description: Authorizes the Secretary of Defense to create a pilot program that allows certain follow-on contracts with businesses wholly owned through an Employee Stock Ownership Plan (ESOP) structured as S-corporations to be awarded using noncompetitive contracting procedures, provided the business performed satisfactorily on a prior similar contract.
manifests.subject: Pilot program for sole-source follow-on awards to ESOP-owned S-corps
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and qualified ESOP-owned S-corporations
manifests.conditions: Applicable only for follow-on contracts for products or services the same as or substantially similar to prior contracts, and if past contract performance was rated satisfactory or better.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Create a new DFARS subpart or section implementing this pilot authority and clarifying when noncompetitive awards to qualifying ESOP businesses are permitted.
open_questions: Should implementation use a DFARS subpart devoted to pilot programs or integrate into existing sole-source/competitive procedures guidance?
How should the transition from 10 U.S.C. 2304 to 10 U.S.C. 3204 be precisely referenced in regulatory text?

manifests.description: Limits each qualified ESOP-owned business to a single sole-source follow-on contract award under this pilot, unless a senior contracting official approves a waiver.
manifests.subject: Limit to one sole-source award per ESOP-owned business (waivable)
manifests.applies_to: Qualified ESOP-owned S-corporations
manifests.conditions: Waiver may be approved by a senior contracting official as defined in 10 U.S.C. 1737.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add to pilot program rules in DFARS a cap of one award per qualifying business, with a documented waiver process.
open_questions: Should there be recordkeeping or public reporting about the waivers granted?
Does the waiver process need DFARS-level forms or documentation templates?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to establish procedures for businesses to verify their qualifying ESOP-owned S-corporation status using existing federal reporting mechanisms.
manifests.subject: Verification procedures for ESOP-owned S-corp status
manifests.applies_to: Businesses seeking to participate in the pilot
manifests.conditions: Verification must use existing Federal mechanisms.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Create verification procedures in DFARS cross-referenced for pilot eligibility.
open_questions: Will this require modifications to SAM.gov or other government systems to flag ESOP-owned S-corps?
What specific documents or database checks will suffice as verification?

manifests.description: Requires qualifying businesses to certify that no more than 50 percent of the contract payments will be used for subcontracts, unless a necessary and reasonable waiver is granted by the Secretary.
manifests.subject: Limit on subcontracting under pilot contracts (50% cap, waivable)
manifests.applies_to: Qualified ESOP-owned S-corporation awardees
manifests.conditions: Certification required; Secretary may issue waiver.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add a new DFARS contract clause or pilot rule imposing this certification/waiver requirement.
open_questions: What is the definition of the base for calculating 'amount paid under the contract' (e.g., total price, obligated amount)?
Is this certification required pre-award, post-award, or periodically?

manifests.description: Requires the collection and maintenance of key information about each follow-on contract awarded under the pilot and the recipient ESOP-owned business, and directs this information to be provided to the Comptroller General.
manifests.subject: Reporting on pilot contracts awarded and participating ESOP businesses
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense
manifests.conditions: For each pilot contract awarded.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Establish reporting obligations in DFARS for contract and business data transmission to a central point.
open_questions: Should this reporting use or extend FPDS mechanisms, or will a separate system be needed?
What contract and business details specifically must be included?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense, if establishing the pilot, to create and submit to Congress a data collection and analysis strategy and plan, and prohibits starting the pilot until this plan is completed and submitted.
manifests.subject: Data collection and reporting strategy requirement before pilot start
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense
manifests.conditions: Plan must be submitted to Congress before starting the pilot.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add a prerequisite in DFARS for validated data strategy/plan before any pilot awards.
open_questions: Does the strategy and plan require renewal or update over the pilot’s lifetime?
How is 'submission' to the committees documented/verified at the contracting level?

manifests.description: Sets a five-year sunset for any pilot program established under this section, expiring five years after enactment.
manifests.subject: Pilot program sunset after five years
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense pilot program under this section
manifests.conditions: Five years since NDAA 2022 enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS provision or DFARS PGI note explaining the sunset; include closeout procedure(s).
open_questions: Must existing contracts be completed or can they run to normal completion past the sunset date?
How will active awards under the program be tracked relative to the sunset?

manifests.description: Requires the Comptroller General to submit a report to Congress, within three years of enactment, covering all uses of the pilot program’s authority, including assessments of its frequency, impact on national defense strategy, participation incentives, and related issues.
manifests.subject: Comptroller General report on pilot program within 3 years
manifests.applies_to: Comptroller General; requires support from DoD for data
manifests.conditions: Report due three years after enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS may note requirement to retain and support provision of needed data to the GAO.
open_questions: What process will ensure that necessary information for this report is collected and retained appropriately by DoD contracting offices?
Are there template report elements for this type of pilot program?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: Contracts. 10 USC 4651 note prec.>> EMPLOYMENT TRANSPARENCY REGARDING INDIVIDUALS WHO PERFORM WORK IN THE PEOPLE'S REPUBLIC OF CHINA.

manifests.description: Requires any entity submitting a bid or proposal for a Department of Defense contract or subcontract over $5 million (excluding commercial products/services) to disclose if they employ one or more individuals who will perform work in the People’s Republic of China on that contract, unless the Secretary of Defense determines that such disclosure would not be in the interest of national security.
manifests.subject: Initial disclosure of employment in China
manifests.applies_to: All entities bidding on DoD contracts/subcontracts over $5M (non-commercial) who employ individuals in China for that contract
manifests.conditions: At the time of bid or proposal submission, and subject to a national security exception
manifests.effective_date: July 1, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: New DFARS solicitation provision and contract clause needed for initial disclosure, with a mechanism for national security exceptions.
open_questions: How should DFARS operationalize the national security exception — does DoD expect offerors to request waivers, or will this be predetermined?
Does this requirement overlap with other disclosure requirements for foreign labor or supply chain risk?

manifests.description: Requires for each of fiscal years 2023 and 2024 that all entities holding one or more covered contracts must make annual recurring disclosures if they employ individuals performing work in China on those contracts.
manifests.subject: Recurring annual disclosure of China-based employment
manifests.applies_to: Entities with active covered DoD contracts during FY23 and FY24
manifests.conditions: Applies to covered entities in FY2023 and FY2024
manifests.effective_date: July 1, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS clause should require annual (or more frequent) reporting by all current contractors for FY23 and FY24.
open_questions: Do recurring disclosures cease after FY2024, or is there an intent to extend further?

manifests.description: Specifies that any required disclosure (initial or recurring) must include (A) the total number of such individuals performing work in China on covered contracts funded by DoD and (B) a description of the physical presence in China where work will be performed.
manifests.subject: Content of China-related employment disclosures
manifests.applies_to: Covered entities making required disclosures
manifests.conditions: Applies whenever initial or recurring disclosure is required
manifests.effective_date: July 1, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS clause should clarify the specific data required in disclosures (number of employees and location details).
open_questions: What level of detail constitutes a sufficient 'description of the physical presence' in China—address, facility ownership, or further details?

manifests.description: Bars the Department of Defense from awarding or renewing any covered contract unless the covered entity has satisfied all disclosure requirements established by this section.
manifests.subject: Contract award/renewal contingent on disclosure compliance
manifests.applies_to: Contracting officers awarding or renewing DoD contracts/subcontracts
manifests.conditions: Contract award/renewal decision points after effective date
manifests.effective_date: July 1, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS should make compliance with these disclosures a condition precedent for award or renewal.
open_questions: What is the remedy for nondisclosure or false reporting discovered after award?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to brief the congressional defense committees every six months, starting January 1, 2023, summarizing all covered employment disclosures received in the past 180 days. Briefings may be classified.
manifests.subject: Semi-annual congressional briefings on disclosures
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.conditions: Semi-annual, starting January 1, 2023
manifests.effective_date: January 1, 2023
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS may require reporting systems or workflows to aggregate disclosure information for staff compilation of congressional briefings.

manifests.description: Defines 'covered contract' as any DoD contract or subcontract over $5,000,000, not including those for commercial products or services, and defines 'covered entity' as essentially any business entity (including subsidiaries) performing work in China (including by leasing or owning relevant real property).
manifests.subject: Definitions for covered contracts/entities
manifests.applies_to: All section requirements
manifests.effective_date: July 1, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Definitions need to be incorporated into the DFARS clause/explanation.
open_questions: Does ownership/lease of real property only trigger the requirement when directly used on the covered contract, or might broader property interests inadvertently create coverage?

manifests.description: Establishes July 1, 2022, as the effective date for all requirements in this section.
manifests.subject: Section effective date
manifests.applies_to: All entities and actions under section 855
manifests.effective_date: July 1, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS clause should specify applicability to solicitations/contracts on or after July 1, 2022.

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 3344 note prec.>> ASSURING INTEGRITY OF OVERSEAS FUEL SUPPLIES.

manifests.description: Requires, before awarding a contract for fuel for any overseas contingency operation, that the Secretary of Defense ensure, to the maximum extent practicable, that no otherwise responsible offeror is disqualified solely because of an unsupported denial of access to a facility or equipment by the host nation government. This aims to prevent unfair disqualification of responsible offerors due to arbitrary or unjustified host nation actions.
manifests.subject: Pre-award protection against unsupported host nation denial
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and contracting officers awarding overseas contingency operation fuel contracts
manifests.conditions: Prior to contract award for fuel supply for any overseas contingency operation
manifests.effective_date: Contracts entered into on or after the enactment date of the Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: New DFARS requirement or clause detailing procedures for documenting and challenging unsupported host nation denials in overseas fuel contracting.
open_questions: What constitutes an 'unsupported denial' and how should it be documented or appealed?

manifests.description: Requires that, before contract award, the Secretary of Defense obtain assurances from the offeror that they will comply with the certification and record submission requirements regarding fuel sourcing and anticorruption compliance.
manifests.subject: Assurances of compliance with sourcing and anticorruption certification and record requirements
manifests.applies_to: Offerors for fuel supply contracts for any overseas contingency operation
manifests.conditions: Before contract award
manifests.effective_date: Contracts entered into on or after the enactment date of the Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Modify pre-award DFARS processes for overseas fuel contracts to require written assurance of compliance with subsections (b) and (c).
open_questions: What form or language is sufficient for such assurance of compliance?

manifests.description: Requires all offerors for overseas contingency operation fuel contracts to certify that the fuel, or its derivatives, is not sourced, in whole or in part, from any nation or region prohibited from selling petroleum to the United States. This enhances fuel supply chain integrity and compliance with U.S. sanctions.
manifests.subject: Certification of prohibited source compliance for fuel and derivatives
manifests.applies_to: Offerors for fuel supply contracts for any overseas contingency operation
manifests.conditions: Offer submission for contracts entered into on or after the enactment date
manifests.effective_date: Contracts entered into on or after the enactment date of the Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Expand/modify DFARS representations and certifications clauses for overseas fuel contracts to require origin certification covering all prohibited nations or regions.
open_questions: Who determines which nations or regions are prohibited, and how is this communicated to offerors?
Does this expand, supersede, or parallel the Russia-specific energy sourcing ban (DFARS 225.7019/252.225-7053,7054)?

manifests.description: Requires offerors for overseas contingency operation fuel contracts to furnish all records necessary to verify compliance with anticorruption laws and regulations, as determined by the Secretary, including specifically the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act, ITAR, EAR, and OFAC regulations.
manifests.subject: Records submission for anticorruption statute and regulation compliance
manifests.applies_to: Offerors for fuel supply contracts for any overseas contingency operation
manifests.conditions: Upon request by the Secretary of Defense or contracting officials
manifests.effective_date: Contracts entered into on or after the enactment date of the Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add or modify a DFARS clause requiring record submission for anticorruption compliance for all overseas contingency operation fuel contracts.
open_questions: What specific records are required to demonstrate compliance?
How will these requirements be updated as new statutes or regulations are issued?

manifests.description: Clarifies that the requirements in subsections (a) and (b)—host nation denial, assurance, certification, and record requirements—apply only to contracts entered into on or after the enactment of this Act.
manifests.subject: Applicability date for new overseas fuel contracting integrity requirements
manifests.applies_to: All relevant contracting actions
manifests.conditions: Applies to contracts entered into on or after the date of enactment
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment of the Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure effective date is clear in DFARS guidance and any implementing clauses.

manifests.description: Requires that for overseas contingency operation fuel contracts, the contracting officer must consider use of 'tradeoff processes' (as described in FAR Subpart 15), including evaluation of past performance, cost, anticorruption training, and compliance. If tradeoff is not used, the contracting officer must submit a written justification for this decision to the Secretary of Defense before the solicitation is issued. This reduces reliance on lowest-price technically acceptable contracting in high-risk contexts.
manifests.subject: Mandatory consideration (and justification if not used) of tradeoff processes for contingency fuel contracts
manifests.applies_to: Contracting officers handling overseas contingency operation fuel contracts
manifests.conditions: When awarding contracts for fuel for overseas contingency operations
manifests.effective_date: Contracts entered into on or after the enactment date of the Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Require in DFARS that for overseas contingency fuel procurements, tradeoff evaluation be considered and documented, and require submission of written justification to the Secretary if not used.
open_questions: What is the standard for an acceptable written justification?
To whom exactly is the justification to be submitted and at what organizational level?

sections.section_heading: MODIFICATION OF PRIZE AUTHORITY FOR ADVANCED TECHNOLOGY ACHIEVEMENTS.

manifests.description: Clarifies that the Department of Defense can award procurement contracts and other types of agreements as prize awards for advanced technology achievements, not just cash or other tangible items. This expands the outcomes DoD can use as incentives in prize competitions.
manifests.subject: Clarifies prize authority to include contracts and agreements
manifests.applies_to: DoD agencies awarding technology achievement prizes
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Clarify in DFARS 235.016 or relevant sections that procurement contracts and other agreements are valid prize types under this authority.
open_questions: Does 'other agreements' explicitly include Other Transactions, and is further clarification needed for contracting officers?

manifests.description: Requires that the competitive process used for prize selection also applies to the selection of recipients for procurement contracts and other agreements awarded as prizes, ensuring all recipients (not only cash prize winners) are selected through a competitive process.
manifests.subject: Mandates competition for contract/agreement recipients
manifests.applies_to: DoD programs awarding contracts or agreements as prizes
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Reference in DFARS 235.016 and 235.006-71 that competitive selection is required for all recipients, including contracts and agreements awarded under prize authority.
open_questions: Should DFARS more precisely define 'competitive selection' for other agreements in this prize context?

manifests.description: Specifies that no prize competition may result in an award (prize, contract, or agreement) exceeding $10 million in fair market value without approval by the Under Secretary of Defense for Research and Engineering, reinforcing oversight on high-value awards.
manifests.subject: Requires approval for high-value awards
manifests.applies_to: DoD agencies conducting prize competitions under this authority
manifests.conditions: Applies when the prize, contract, or agreement exceeds $10,000,000 in fair market value
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Explicitly link this threshold and approval requirement in DFARS guidance for advanced technology prize authority programs.
open_questions: Are there established DoD processes for seeking/recording this approval, and should DFARS specify them?

manifests.description: Creates a new requirement for the Secretary of Defense to notify congressional defense committees within 15 days when a procurement contract or other agreement resulting from a prize competition exceeds $10 million. Notice must include the contract/agreement value, a description of what was awarded, and a statement of military benefit.
manifests.subject: Congressional notification for large awards
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense (and relevant DoD staff administering prize contracts/agreements)
manifests.conditions: When an award resulting from the prize authority exceeds $10,000,000 in fair market value
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add a reporting workflow in DFARS 235.016 for tracking and congressional notification of qualifying awards.
open_questions: Is there an existing workflow for these notifications, or is a new tracking/reporting process required in DFARS?

sections.section_heading: REPEAL OF PREFERENCE FOR FIXED-PRICE CONTRACTS.

manifests.description: Repeals the requirement for the Department of Defense to prefer fixed-price contracts (including fixed-price incentive fee contracts) and eliminates the requirement for high-level approval before entering into certain large cost-type contracts. The goal is to remove the mandatory preference for fixed-price contract structures and associated approval thresholds for cost-type contracts, giving more flexibility in contract type selection.
manifests.subject: Repeal of preference for fixed-price contracts and associated approval for cost-type contracts
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense acquisition personnel and contracting officers
source_authorities: Section 829 of the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2017
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove or revise DFARS rules derived from the repealed Section 829, including any established preference for fixed-price contracts and approval requirements for large cost-type contracts; review Subpart 216.2 and related sections.
open_questions: Are there other DFARS or DoD policies outside those implemented directly from Section 829 that need to be reviewed or rescinded to ensure the preference for fixed-price contracts is fully removed?

sections.section_heading: LIMITATION ON PROCUREMENT OF WELDED SHIPBOARD ANCHOR AND MOORING CHAIN FOR NAVAL VESSELS.

manifests.description: Adds welded shipboard anchor and mooring chain for naval vessels to the statutory list of naval vessel components subject to sourcing limitations, meaning these items can only be procured from qualified manufacturers. This change strengthens supply chain protections by ensuring these components are sourced from within the national technology and industrial base.
manifests.subject: Adds welded shipboard anchor and mooring chain as a covered item
manifests.applies_to: Procurement of welded shipboard anchor and mooring chain for naval vessels by the Department of Defense
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 225.7007 to explicitly include welded shipboard anchor and mooring chain as a covered item and ensure related contract clauses (e.g., 252.225-7019) reflect this statutory expansion.
open_questions: Does this require changes to DFARS 225.7007 beyond what current appropriations statute coverage already provides?
Should supply chain data/reporting be updated to specifically track welded anchor and chain as distinct covered items?

manifests.description: Specifies that a manufacturer of welded shipboard anchor and mooring chain for naval vessels qualifies for DoD procurement if it is part of the national technology and industrial base, clarifying which manufacturers are eligible and possibly broadening the pool to include all entities within this definition.
manifests.subject: Clarifies manufacturer qualification for welded shipboard anchor and mooring chain
manifests.applies_to: Manufacturers of welded shipboard anchor and mooring chain for naval vessels
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 225.7007 and 252.225-7019 to state that manufacturers of welded shipboard anchor and mooring chain qualify if they are part of the national technology and industrial base. Clarify and harmonize DFARS coverage with this statutory definition.
open_questions: Does this change override stricter requirements in appropriations law, or do stricter rules still apply when present?
How is membership in the national technology and industrial base validated by contracting officers—are there established DoD processes for verification?

sections.section_heading: MODIFIED CONDITION FOR PROMPT CONTRACT PAYMENT ELIGIBILITY.

manifests.description: This change raises the standard for when a prime contractor can qualify for prompt payment based on payments to a subcontractor. Instead of qualifying if the contractor 'agrees or proposes' to make payments, only those who actually 'agree' to make payments (have a firm agreement) will be eligible, preventing eligibility based solely on a proposal.
manifests.subject: Tightening eligibility for prompt payment to prime contractors
manifests.applies_to: Prime contractors seeking prompt payment eligibility under Department of Defense contracts
manifests.conditions: The change applies when prompt payment eligibility is determined based on payments to subcontractors under 10 U.S.C. 2307(a)(2)(B).
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Review and revise DFARS Subpart 232, particularly 232.906 and any clauses referencing prompt payment eligibility for primes based on subcontractor payments, to ensure only those with an actual agreement—not merely a proposal—qualify.
open_questions: Does this require new guidance on what constitutes an 'agreement' (e.g., is a purchase order sufficient)?
Does this apply only to contracts awarded after enactment, or retroactively to existing contracts?

sections.section_heading: AUTHORITY TO ACQUIRE INNOVATIVE COMMERCIAL PRODUCTS AND COMMERCIAL SERVICES USING GENERAL SOLICITATION COMPETITIVE PROCEDURES.
