manifests.description: Establishes a permanent authority allowing the Secretary of Defense and the Secretaries of the military departments to acquire innovative commercial products and commercial services using a competitive selection process through general solicitation and peer review, instead of traditional acquisition methods.
manifests.subject: Permanent authority for acquiring innovative commercial products and services using general solicitation
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and Secretaries of the military departments
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: New DFARS subpart (likely in Part 212 or new section 212.7) describing the general solicitation peer review process.

manifests.description: Specifies that the general solicitation competitive procedure described in the new authority counts as use of competitive procedures for statutory and regulatory requirements regarding competition.
manifests.subject: General solicitation deemed competitive procedures
manifests.applies_to: Acquisitions conducted under the new authority
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Explicit regulatory provision stating this process satisfies FAR/DFARS competition requirements.

manifests.description: Requires a written determination by the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment or relevant service acquisition executive for any contract or agreement exceeding $100 million, confirming the effort's efficacy to meet mission needs before proceeding under this authority.
manifests.subject: High-dollar award written determination requirement
manifests.applies_to: Contracting under this authority for amounts over $100 million
manifests.conditions: Award amount exceeds $100 million
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add a procedural step in DFARS requiring this approval before award.
open_questions: Should DFARS specify a standard template or required content for the determination?

manifests.description: Restricts contracts or agreements using this new authority to fixed-price or fixed-price incentive fee types only, prohibiting cost-reimbursement and other contract types.
manifests.subject: Contract type limitation: fixed-price only
manifests.applies_to: Contracts and agreements awarded under this authority
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Prohibit use of cost-type and other non-fixed-price contracts under this authority in DFARS.
open_questions: Are time-and-materials or labor-hour contracts considered permitted or prohibited under this limitation?

manifests.description: Requires that all products and services acquired under this new authority must be treated as commercial products and commercial services for acquisition purposes, even if they do not fit traditional definitions.
manifests.subject: Mandatory commercial treatment for items/services acquired
manifests.applies_to: Products and services acquired under this authority
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Amend DFARS definitions and relevant clauses to explicitly classify such acquisitions as commercial.
open_questions: Will this override other laws or regulations requiring a separate commercial item/service determination?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to notify the congressional defense committees within 45 days after awarding any contract over $100 million under this authority, including detailed justification and information about the acquisition.
manifests.subject: Congressional notification for high-dollar awards
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.conditions: Contract exceeds $100 million under this authority
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Mirror reporting requirements and timelines in DFARS as used for high-dollar UCAs or major programs.
open_questions: Who is responsible for transmitting the notification—the local contracting activity or a centralized DoD office?

manifests.description: Defines 'innovative' for this authority to mean new technologies, processes, or methods (including R&D) or new applications of existing ones as of the proposal submission date.
manifests.subject: Definition of 'innovative'
manifests.applies_to: Any use of this authority and related reporting/data collection
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add the definition to the DFARS subpart implementing the new section.
open_questions: Can this include innovative business models as well as technical concepts?

manifests.description: Prohibits the use of the new acquisition authority from October 1, 2022, until the Secretary of Defense submits to Congress a completed plan for required data collection and analysis regarding the authority's implementation.
manifests.subject: Moratorium on use until data collection plan is submitted
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and military departments
manifests.conditions: From October 1, 2022, until data collection plan submitted
manifests.effective_date: Restriction in effect beginning October 1, 2022, until submission of plan
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Implement as a pre-condition/procurement block noted in PGI or required reviews.
open_questions: Who certifies when the plan is 'completed' and made available?
How is status communicated to contracting offices?

manifests.description: Transfers the new statutory authority from chapter 140, section 2380c, to chapter 247, section 3458, and updates all internal statutory cross-references (e.g., to the competition chapter and definition sections) accordingly.
manifests.subject: Recodification and cross-reference update
manifests.applies_to: 10 U.S.C. sections referencing this authority
manifests.effective_date: As if included in NDAA FY21 title XVIII
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS cross-references to cite 3458 and new chapter numbers as necessary.
open_questions: Are there any existing DFARS references to the repealed pilot authority that also need updating?

manifests.description: Repeals Section 879 of the NDAA for FY2017, thus ending the prior pilot authority to acquire innovative commercial items and services by general solicitation, as it is superseded by the new permanent authority.
manifests.subject: Repeal of obsolete pilot program
manifests.applies_to: 10 U.S.C. 2302 note; DoD
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment of this section
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove any remaining DFARS or PGI references to the repealed pilot program.
open_questions: Are current contracts under the repealed pilot authority still valid until completion?

sections.section_heading: PROHIBITION ON ACQUISITION OF PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT FROM NON-ALLIED FOREIGN NATIONS.

manifests.description: Establishes a prohibition preventing the Department of Defense from acquiring certain personal protective equipment (PPE) and related sanitary items from specified non-allied foreign nations (North Korea, China, Russia, and Iran). This applies to all prime contracts and subcontracts, unless a listed exception applies. The purpose is to strengthen supply chain security and reduce dependence on potential adversaries for critical health protection items.
manifests.subject: Prohibition on acquisition of specified PPE from certain nations
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense procurement officers, prime contractors, and subcontractors at all tiers
manifests.conditions: Unless an exception applies (as defined in subsection (c))
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 225.7023 and clause 252.225-7061 already substantially implement this requirement but should be crosschecked for full alignment and updated references.
open_questions: Are further waivers, reporting, or exception-handling procedures needed, or do existing DFARS provisions suffice?

manifests.description: Defines the exact types of items subject to the procurement prohibition (PPE for disease prevention, sanitary items, and specific exclusions such as sensors or electronics not normally associated with PPE). Also defines 'covered nation' as North Korea, China, Russia, and Iran. This standardizes what is restricted under this policy.
manifests.subject: Definition of covered items and nations
manifests.applies_to: All entities subject to the Section 802 prohibition
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Check and update DFARS 252.225-7061 definitions for parity with statute.
open_questions: Should future updates to the covered items or covered nations list be statutory only or could DoD/DFARS make updates administratively?

manifests.description: Details exceptions to the prohibition: (1) If the Secretary of Defense determines that required quality or quantity, in proper form, can't be met from other nations at a reasonable price; (2) items procured for use outside the U.S.; (3) purchases not exceeding $150,000, with a prohibition on splitting larger buys to evade this threshold. This provides limited flexibility for DoD sourcing needs.
manifests.subject: Exceptions to PPE procurement prohibition
manifests.applies_to: DoD, contractors, subcontractors
manifests.conditions: When a listed exception in subsection (c) applies; structuring to avoid threshold is prohibited.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure DFARS 225.7023-3, -4, and 252.225-7061 exceptions match statute in scope and application, particularly threshold/structuring clauses.
open_questions: Are additional internal DoD policies required to support the Secretary's determination exception (e.g., documentation, approval procedures)?

manifests.description: Transfers and redesignates the substantive prohibition, definitions, and exceptions from section 2533e to section 4875 under the new legal structure created by the Title 10 reorganization. Updates corresponding tables of sections to reflect new statute location.
manifests.subject: Code section redesignation and updating
manifests.applies_to: Statutory authorities and all referencing regulations, including DFARS
manifests.conditions: Effective immediately after amendments of NDAA FY21 Title XVIII take effect
amends: 10 U.S.C. chapter 385, subchapter III table of sections
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS citations and cross-references should be updated from 2533e to 4875 when effective. Ensure contract clauses and regulatory background reflect updated section.
open_questions: Should legacy contracts reference the old or new Code section?
What is the exact date the DFARS/citation change takes effect in relation to NDAA FY21 Title XVIII implementation?

manifests.description: Confirms that general savings, reference, and rule-of-construction provisions apply: regulations, actions, and violations under the previous statute (2533e) are deemed to carry over to the new section (4875), and the reorganization does not modify substantive law or its effect.
manifests.subject: Savings and reference provisions
manifests.applies_to: DoD, contractors, regulatory authorities
manifests.conditions: Actions, offenses, or interpretations under prior statute are treated as under redesignated law
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS may note continuity and reference carryover in the background or regulatory history of the implementing rule, but no new compliance action is needed.
open_questions: Does DoD plan to issue additional guidance to clarify the statute/DFARS update for acquisition professionals and contractors?

sections.section_heading: TRANSFER AND REORGANIZATION OF DEFENSE ACQUISITION STATUTES.

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to establish a process for comprehensive review and stakeholder engagement—drawing on both public and private sector experts—to assess Title XVIII of the FY21 NDAA and the amendments made by it. The goal is to ensure the restructuring and transfer of defense acquisition statutes is evaluated with broad input, surfacing potential issues and needed adjustments before full implementation.
manifests.subject: Statute review and stakeholder engagement process
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; interested parties and experts from public and private sectors
manifests.conditions: Must be established by February 1, 2021
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: A new requirement for a comprehensive multi-stakeholder review process of statutory acquisition reorganization; DFARS should reference this engagement and review requirement as a precondition to the title’s regulatory update.
open_questions: What is the scope of ‘interested parties and experts’? Does DoD internally define or solicit public comment?

manifests.description: Mandates the Secretary of Defense submit a report to Congress (by March 15, 2021) evaluating Title XVIII and its amendments. The report must include: recommendations for legislative modifications; a summary and assessment of review activities; a detailed implementation plan (including scheduling, role assignments, resources, and review criteria); and any other items deemed appropriate. This is aimed at consolidating lessons learned and planning how to operationalize the statutory reorganization in Department regulations like the DFARS.
manifests.subject: Congressional report on review, assessment, and implementation plan
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.conditions: Must be submitted by March 15, 2021
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS should include cross-reference to the resultant implementation plan as a guiding document for any conforming changes, and clarify that regulatory adjustments must align with the staged implementation process outlined in the report.
open_questions: How binding will the recommendations and implementation plan be on future regulatory updates?

manifests.description: Delays the enactment of Title XVIII and its amendments until January 1, 2022 (unless otherwise specified), giving time for review, planning, and necessary regulatory changes. This is meant to manage transition and avoid regulatory confusion.
manifests.subject: Delayed effective date for Title XVIII and amendments
manifests.applies_to: All authorities subject to this title and its amendments
manifests.conditions: Except as specifically provided, delayed until January 1, 2022
manifests.effective_date: January 1, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS must not implement or reflect the reorganized statutes before January 1, 2022, except where specifically authorized.

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to revise or modify the DFARS and any other affected authorities by not later than January 1, 2023, to conform to Title XVIII and its amendments. This ensures the regulatory system catches up with the statutory changes within a year of their effective date.
manifests.subject: Required DFARS and regulatory update to reflect Title XVIII
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; DFARS and other affected authorities
manifests.conditions: Action must be completed not later than January 1, 2023
manifests.effective_date: January 1, 2023
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS must be comprehensively revised, with relevant authorities and text updated, by the January 1, 2023, deadline to align with the reorganized statutes.
open_questions: What happens if the DFARS is not updated before the deadline? (Unclear enforcement or fallback.)

manifests.description: Requires that for contracts entered during the 'covered period' (from January 1, 2022, until the DFARS and authorities are revised, but no later than January 1, 2023), the Department of Defense must continue to apply acquisition law as in effect on December 31, 2021. This transition provision prevents mid-contract uncertainty over which statutory authority governs.
manifests.subject: Transition application of pre-existing acquisition law for new contracts
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; contracts entered into during transition ('covered period')
manifests.conditions: Contracts entered between January 1, 2022 and the earlier of DFARS update or January 1, 2023
manifests.effective_date: January 1, 2022 - January 1, 2023 (or update)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add explicit language to DFARS to ensure legacy statutes are used for applicable contracts during the transition period, and procedures for identifying and administering such contracts.
open_questions: What guidance will DoD issue to ensure contracting officers are clear on which set of laws apply for actions initiated in the covered period?

sections.section_heading: REQUIREMENT TO BUY CERTAIN SATELLITE COMPONENT FROM NATIONAL TECHNOLOGY AND INDUSTRIAL BASE.

manifests.description: Adds a requirement that any 'star tracker' used in a satellite weighing more than 400 pounds, whose principal purpose is to support U.S. national security, defense, or intelligence needs, must be procured from the National Technology and Industrial Base. This change is meant to strengthen supply chain security for critical satellite components by requiring domestic sourcing.
manifests.subject: NTIB procurement requirement for star trackers
manifests.applies_to: Procurements of satellites or satellite subsystems for U.S. national security, defense, or intelligence with a mass greater than 400 pounds
manifests.conditions: Satellite must weigh more than 400 pounds and be intended primarily for U.S. national security, defense, or intelligence use
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add new entry to DFARS 225.7010-1 listing star trackers for covered satellites as subject to NTIB sourcing requirement.
open_questions: Should 'star tracker' be further defined in DFARS to avoid ambiguity?
Is a new section needed for satellite components, or should this be added as a new paragraph?

manifests.description: Exempts programs that received Milestone A approval before October 1, 2021, from the new NTIB procurement requirement for star trackers. This means existing or significantly advanced satellite programs are not forced to comply retroactively, minimizing program disruption.
manifests.subject: Exemption for prior-approved programs
manifests.applies_to: Satellite programs with Milestone A approval before October 1, 2021
manifests.conditions: Milestone A approval before October 1, 2021 (as defined in 10 U.S.C. 4211)
manifests.effective_date: October 1, 2021
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: State in the new DFARS paragraph that programs with Milestone A approved before 10/1/2021 are exempt from this requirement.
open_questions: Should DFARS specify a method for certifying compliance with the Milestone A date exemption?

manifests.description: Allows the Secretary of Defense to delegate the authority to approve waivers for the star tracker NTIB sourcing requirement to service acquisition executives (e.g., Secretaries of the military departments), subject to certain oversight conditions in existing law. This streamlines the waiver process for affected acquisitions.
manifests.subject: Delegation of waiver authority
manifests.applies_to: Waiver process for the star tracker procurement requirement
manifests.conditions: Delegation is subject to 10 U.S.C. 2534(i) oversight
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Reference in DFARS the possibility to delegate waiver approval for the star tracker rule to service acquisition executives, consistent with other DFARS delegation clauses under 225.7010-3.
open_questions: Should the DFARS specify formal documentation or notification mechanisms for such delegation?

sections.section_heading: RESTRICTIONS ON OVERHAUL, REPAIR, ETC. OF NAVAL VESSELS IN FOREIGN SHIPYARDS.

manifests.description: Expands the permitted exceptions for repairing U.S. naval vessels in foreign shipyards to explicitly include repairs required due to damage from hostile actions or interventions, in addition to voyage repairs. Provides clear legal authority for such repairs to ensure naval readiness after unexpected adverse events outside the United States or Guam.
manifests.subject: Exception for foreign shipyard repairs after hostile action
manifests.applies_to: Naval vessels homeported in the United States or Guam
manifests.conditions: Repairs must be voyage repairs or necessary to correct damage due to hostile actions or interventions
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 225.7013(b) to reference both 'voyage repairs' and the new 'hostile actions or interventions' exception for foreign shipyard repairs.
open_questions: Should 'hostile actions or interventions' be defined further for regulatory clarity?
What is the legal definition of 'interventions' for these purposes?

manifests.description: Creates a new threshold and process for using foreign workers to perform corrective or preventive maintenance or repair on Littoral Combat Ships in foreign shipyards. The Secretary of the Navy (not delegable) must determine that U.S. personnel travel is not advisable for health or safety reasons. Written notification including reasoning, location, and duration must be sent to Congressional defense committees within 30 days.
manifests.subject: Threshold and reporting for use of foreign workers in repairs/maintenance
manifests.applies_to: Corrective and preventive maintenance or repair on Littoral Combat Ships in foreign shipyards
manifests.conditions: Only if Secretary of the Navy determines travel by U.S. personnel is not advisable for health or safety reasons; notification sent within 30 days of determination
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add new detailed exception and reporting requirement under DFARS 225.7013 for health/safety-based use of foreign workers for deployed maintenance/repair.
open_questions: Should this be implemented as an explicit DFARS reporting requirement or contract clause?
Is further guidance needed for what constitutes 'health or safety reasons'?

manifests.description: Makes a technical correction by removing an unnecessary period after 'means--' in the definition of 'facilities maintenance' in 10 U.S.C. 8680(a)(2)(C)(ii). This is a formatting fix that does not affect meaning or implementation.
manifests.subject: Technical correction to statute formatting
manifests.applies_to: Definition of 'facilities maintenance' in 10 U.S.C. 8680

sections.section_heading: PREFERENCE FOR UNITED STATES VESSELS IN TRANSPORTING SUPPLIES BY SEA.

manifests.description: Completely rewrites 10 U.S.C. 2631 to strengthen and clarify the preference for using United States vessels to transport Department of Defense supplies by sea. Specifies eligible vessel types, narrowly defines waiver authority, sets forth annual congressional notification, and establishes detailed compliance monitoring and enforcement mechanisms.
manifests.subject: Preference for U.S. vessels in DoD sea transportation
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and its contractors transporting supplies by sea
manifests.conditions: Applies unless a U.S. vessel is unavailable at a fair and reasonable rate, or otherwise unavailable, or unless a waiver is granted according to national security needs.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 247.572, 252.247-7023, 252.247-7025, and 252.247-7026; oversee that DoD vessel transportation requirements and waiver/notification/enforcement mechanisms are consistent with the amended law.

manifests.description: Requires that any reflagging, repair, or corrective/preventive maintenance performed on vessels under DoD time-charter contracts must occur in the United States or its territories as much as practicable, with waiver authority only for critical national security reasons and immediate reporting of all waivers to Congress.
manifests.subject: Location of reflagging and repair work for chartered vessels
manifests.applies_to: Vessels chartered under DoD contracts for supply transportation
manifests.conditions: Waiver permitted only if the Secretary of Defense determines necessity for national security and notifies Congress in writing.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 247.572 and 252.247-7025; ensure contract language and enforcement mechanisms reflect requirement for U.S. location for work and clear waiver notification obligations.
open_questions: What evidence is needed to demonstrate that maintenance or repair in the U.S. has been achieved 'to the greatest extent practicable'?

manifests.description: Mandates at least annual written notification to specified congressional committees about any waivers granted to the U.S.-vessel preference requirement or to the U.S.-based maintenance/repair requirement, including the reasons for each waiver.
manifests.subject: Congressional notification of waivers
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and relevant DoD authorities
manifests.conditions: Triggered whenever a waiver is granted under 10 U.S.C. 2631 subsections (a) or (c)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS coverage should reference reporting requirements for granted waivers by the Secretary of Defense.
open_questions: Should DFARS specify who within DoD is responsible for consolidating and transmitting these reports to Congress?

manifests.description: Requires DoD contracting officers to monitor and enforce contractor compliance with the U.S.-vessel preference and associated requirements, including entering performance data in relevant databases and exercising remedies (such as contract termination, ineligibility, suspension, or debarment) for violations, including violations as determined by the Secretary of Transportation for associated statutes.
manifests.subject: Contractor compliance and enforcement
manifests.applies_to: DoD contracting officers and contractors transporting supplies by sea
manifests.conditions: When a contractor violates the requirements in 10 U.S.C. 2631 or relevant sections of Title 46
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Expand DFARS guidance for contractor performance tracking and enforcement actions for noncompliance related to the cargo preference requirements.
open_questions: Will additional guidance be issued to agencies to clarify 'responsible offeror' determinations for compliance purposes?

manifests.description: Transfers and renumbers 46 U.S.C. 55301 (priority loading for coal) to 46 U.S.C. 55123, and updates the tables of contents for chapters 551 and 553 accordingly. No substantive change to underlying requirements.
manifests.subject: Technical amendments to Title 46, priority loading for coal
manifests.applies_to: United States Code (Title 46), agencies referencing these statutes
amends: Table of sections for ch. 553 subch. I
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update any DFARS references to the affected sections to match new citations.

manifests.description: Changes the heading of subchapter I, chapter 553 of Title 46 from 'Passenger and Cargo Preferences' to 'Government Impelled Transportation' for accuracy and clarity.
manifests.subject: Technical amendment to Title 46 subchapter heading
manifests.applies_to: United States Code (Title 46), agencies referencing this subchapter
amends: Heading of 46 U.S.C. ch. 553, subch. I
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure any DFARS citation to the subchapter heading uses the updated title as appropriate.

sections.section_heading: REVISION TO REQUIREMENT TO USE FIRM FIXED-PRICE CONTRACTS FOR FOREIGN MILITARY SALES.

manifests.description: Repeals the statutory requirement that the Department of Defense must generally use firm fixed-price contracts for Foreign Military Sales (FMS), unless the foreign country requests otherwise or waivers are granted. This restores discretion to DoD officials to select the contract type for FMS transactions as appropriate, removing the default fixed-price mandate.
manifests.subject: Repeal of fixed-price contract requirement for FMS
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and FMS contracting officials
amends: Section 830 of the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2017 (22 U.S.C. 2762 note)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove or revise DFARS 225.7301-1, which currently requires firm-fixed-price contracts for FMS pursuant to FY17 section 830. Update related DFARS sections to reflect the repeal.
open_questions: Should an alternative default contract type be specified in place of firm fixed-price, or is the intent to revert entirely to the pre-2017 state of discretion?
Are there any ongoing waivers or existing FMS contracts negotiated under the repealed requirement that require special transition provisions?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 4701 note.>> PROHIBITION ON AWARDING OF CONTRACTS TO CONTRACTORS THAT REQUIRE NONDISCLOSURE AGREEMENTS RELATING TO WASTE, FRAUD, OR ABUSE.

manifests.description: Prohibits the Department of Defense (DoD) from awarding contracts for goods or services to contractors who require their employees to sign internal confidentiality agreements or statements that would prohibit or otherwise restrict lawful reporting of waste, fraud, or abuse related to DoD contract performance to a designated DoD investigative or law enforcement representative.
manifests.subject: Prohibition on restrictive NDAs for DoD contract reporting
manifests.applies_to: DoD contract awardees and applicants
manifests.conditions: Award of any DoD contract for goods or services
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add new DFARS requirement: prior to contract award, contractors must represent they do not require such restrictive confidentiality agreements (analogous to 203.903 but specific to this prohibition).
open_questions: Should the contractor representation be a new FAR/DFARS certification or added to an existing representation clause?

manifests.description: Requires that contractors inform their employees that internal confidentiality agreements or statements cannot prohibit or restrict lawful reporting of waste, fraud, or abuse regarding DoD contracts to designated DoD authorities.
manifests.subject: Contractor obligation to inform employees about reporting rights
manifests.applies_to: DoD contractors and their employees
manifests.conditions: Applies to all contractors awarded contracts by DoD
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Mandate contractor notification requirement in contracts; require representation of compliance before award.
open_questions: Is a specific method or timing for providing this notification to employees required or left to contractor discretion?

manifests.description: Allows DoD contracting officers to rely on the contractor’s representation regarding compliance with the above requirements unless the officer has reason to question its accuracy.
manifests.subject: Reliance by contracting officer on contractor representation
manifests.applies_to: DoD contracting officers and contractors
manifests.conditions: Unless the contracting officer has reason to question the accuracy of contractor's representation
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Authorize reliance in DFARS on contractor’s certification, with procedures for addressing concerns about representation accuracy.
open_questions: What constitutes 'reason to question' the accuracy, and what process should be followed if concerns arise?

sections.section_heading: MISCELLANEOUS LIMITATIONS ON THE PROCUREMENT OF GOODS OTHER THAN UNITED STATES GOODS.

manifests.description: Updates and clarifies the list of naval vessel components that are subject to domestic source restrictions. The new list includes: gyrocompasses, electronic navigation chart systems, steering controls, propulsion and machinery control systems, and totally enclosed lifeboats, to the extent they are unique to marine applications. This removes older items such as chemical weapons antidotes, valves, and machine tools.
manifests.subject: Naval vessel component domestic source requirements
manifests.applies_to: DoD procurement of naval vessel components
manifests.conditions: Applies to components unique to marine applications
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 225.7010-1(a) to reflect the revised and narrowed/expanded list of vessel components.
open_questions: Is there an official DoD definition or guidance to determine what is 'unique to marine applications'?

manifests.description: Creates a new, explicit category of components for T-AO 205 class vessels that must be procured from within the national technology and industrial base: (A) auxiliary equipment (including pumps) for all shipboard services, (B) propulsion system components (including engines, reduction gears, and propellers), (C) shipboard cranes, (D) spreaders for shipboard cranes.
manifests.subject: Domestic source requirement for T-AO 205 class vessel components
manifests.applies_to: DoD procurement of listed T-AO 205 class vessel components
manifests.conditions: Specific to T-AO 205 class vessels
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Expand DFARS 225.7010 to include explicit requirements for T-AO 205 class vessel components.
open_questions: Is this interpreted as a new restriction beyond prior categories, or is it confirming an existing intent for T-AO 205 class vessels?

manifests.description: Simplifies and clarifies the eligibility criteria for manufacturers: a manufacturer now meets requirements purely by being part of the national technology and industrial base, removing other descriptive language.
manifests.subject: Definition of eligible manufacturer
manifests.applies_to: All manufacturers under 10 U.S.C. 2534
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update eligibility in DFARS references to match new, simplified definition.
open_questions: Does this language close possible loopholes previously used for multinational/joint ventures?

manifests.description: Removes paragraphs in subsection (c) that had provided exceptions or clarifying details for restricted items, such as valves, machine tools, and other components no longer covered by the law.
manifests.subject: Elimination of outdated exceptions for certain components
manifests.applies_to: All DoD contracting activities subject to 10 U.S.C. 2534
manifests.conditions: Applies to valves, machine tools, ball bearings, vessel propellers, and chemical weapon antidotes previously covered
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove DFARS cross-references to deleted exceptions.
open_questions: Are current exception-based contracts grandfathered or will they require modification?

manifests.description: Makes technical corrections throughout the statute, including renumbering provisions, correcting cross-references, and updating the title 'Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics' to 'Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment.'
manifests.subject: Technical/statutory housekeeping amendments
manifests.applies_to: All DoD acquisition practitioners referencing 10 U.S.C. 2534
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Review all DFARS references for title and cross-reference consistency.

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to expedite the review process under the domestic sourcing application process (now 2534(j)) to no more than 60 days for certain auxiliary ship components: auxiliary equipment (including pumps), propulsion system components, shipboard cranes, and spreaders for shipboard cranes.
manifests.subject: Expedited review of sourcing applications for specified auxiliary ship components
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and relevant acquisition/executive offices
manifests.conditions: Applications under 10 U.S.C. 2534(j) for these ship components
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add procedural guidance in DFARS for 60-day review requirement for these items.
open_questions: Does the 60-day review requirement apply indefinitely, or only temporarily?

sections.section_heading: EXPANSION ON THE PROHIBITION ON ACQUIRING CERTAIN METAL PRODUCTS.

manifests.description: Expands the prohibition on acquiring certain metal products for DoD so that, instead of only banning covered materials (such as certain magnets and tungsten) that are 'melted' in covered countries, it now also bans those that are 'mined, refined, or separated' in those countries. This closes loopholes and broadens controls to earlier stages in the supply chain.
manifests.subject: Broader prohibition on covered materials sourced from covered countries
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and its contractors and suppliers
manifests.conditions: Acquisition of covered materials (neodymium iron-boron magnets, samarium-cobalt magnets, tungsten, or their alloys) where material is mined, refined, separated, or melted in a covered country (e.g., North Korea, China, Russia, Iran).
manifests.effective_date: January 1, 2026
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 225.7018-2 and related clauses to prohibit covered materials that are mined, refined, or separated (in addition to melted) in a covered country.
open_questions: Should the DFARS define 'mined,' 'refined,' and 'separated' for enforcement and compliance purposes?
Will suppliers be required to provide new certifications covering mining, refining, and separation stages?

manifests.description: Replaces a reference to 'tungsten' with 'covered material' in an exception or special case provision, so that the clause now applies to all covered materials specified under the statute (neodymium magnets, samarium-cobalt magnets, and tungsten), not just tungsten.
manifests.subject: Exception/reporting provision broadened from tungsten to all covered materials
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense acquisition exception or waiver processes for covered materials
manifests.conditions: Wherever the exception/reporting provision referenced 'tungsten', it now covers all 'covered materials'.
manifests.effective_date: January 1, 2026
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS references relating to tungsten exceptions/waivers to apply to all covered materials.
open_questions: Does this change affect any existing exceptions or waivers that were limited to tungsten, requiring process or documentation updates?

manifests.description: Delays the effective date of the expanded prohibitions and broadened exception/waiver scope for five years from the date of enactment (i.e., they become enforceable on January 1, 2026). This gives contractors and suppliers time to adjust their supply chains.
manifests.subject: Effective date delay for new supply chain restrictions
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense supply chain/acquisition processes and contractor compliance schedules
manifests.conditions: Expanded prohibitions and broadened exception/waiver do not apply before January 1, 2026.
manifests.effective_date: January 1, 2026
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS guidance and clauses should clarify that these expanded prohibitions and broadened exception/waiver do not apply before January 1, 2026, and should prepare contractors for the upcoming change.
open_questions: Should the DoD or DFARS issue guidance or notice to contractors and suppliers to inform them of the upcoming compliance deadline?

sections.section_heading: CONTRACT CLOSEOUT AUTHORITY FOR SERVICES CONTRACTS.

manifests.description: Expands the contracts eligible for special contract closeout by revising the time threshold: for most contracts, the contract must have been entered into at least 7 fiscal years before the current fiscal year (instead of the prior static fiscal year 2000 requirement). This ensures the authority applies to a wider and continually updating group of contracts.
manifests.subject: Rolling eligibility for special contract closeout authority
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts (except military construction or shipbuilding)
manifests.conditions: Contract or group of contracts was entered into at least 7 fiscal years before the current fiscal year; other statutory conditions remain unchanged.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 204.804(3)(i)(A) - update rolling threshold to 7 fiscal years before current fiscal year

manifests.description: Creates a longer eligibility period for special contract closeout regarding military construction or shipbuilding contracts, setting the threshold at at least 10 fiscal years before the current fiscal year. This is a narrower standard than for other contracts.
manifests.subject: Eligibility interval for contract closeout—military construction and shipbuilding
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts for military construction (as defined in 10 U.S.C. 2801) or shipbuilding
manifests.conditions: Contract or group of contracts was entered into at least 10 fiscal years before the current fiscal year
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 204.804(3)(i)(A) - add special 10-year rule for military construction and shipbuilding

manifests.description: Standardizes the time after delivery or performance that must have elapsed before special contract closeout authority can be used, setting the period at at least 4 years before the current fiscal year for all covered contracts.
manifests.subject: Elapsed time since performance or delivery for special contract closeout
manifests.applies_to: All Department of Defense contracts eligible for special closeout authority
manifests.conditions: Performance or delivery completed at least 4 years before the current fiscal year
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 204.804(3)(i) - specify that performance or delivery must have been completed at least 4 years before the current fiscal year

sections.section_heading: MODIFICATION TO SMALL PURCHASE THRESHOLD EXCEPTION TO SOURCING REQUIREMENTS FOR CERTAIN ARTICLES.

manifests.description: Explicitly replaces the reference to the 'simplified acquisition threshold' in the Berry Amendment small purchase exception with a fixed threshold of $150,000. Purchases at or below this amount are exempt from the domestic sourcing requirements for certain articles.
manifests.subject: Small purchase threshold for Berry Amendment exception
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers and contract actions for covered items
manifests.conditions: Applies to acquisitions for covered items where the total purchase amount does not exceed $150,000.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 225.7002-2(a) and relevant clauses to substitute the fixed $150,000 amount for the previous reference to the simplified acquisition threshold.
open_questions: Is the intent to have this threshold float with or diverge from the simplified acquisition threshold in the FAR?

manifests.description: Adds an explicit prohibition against dividing a larger procurement into multiple smaller actions to qualify for the small purchase exception. Purchases exceeding $150,000 cannot be split into parts in order to evade the sourcing requirements.
manifests.subject: Anti-splitting rule for small purchase threshold
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers and contract actions for covered items
manifests.conditions: Procurements where the total requirement exceeds $150,000 but are being divided into smaller purchases or contracts
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add an anti-splitting provision to DFARS 225.7002-2 and/or 252.225-7012 reflecting the statutory restriction.
open_questions: Should DCAA/IG issue guidance for oversight or enforcement regarding artificial splitting of purchases?

manifests.description: Authorizes the Secretary of Defense, every five years (years divisible by five), to adjust the $150,000 small purchase threshold based on changes in the Consumer Price Index. Any adjustment becomes effective upon publication in the Federal Register.
manifests.subject: CPI-based adjustment of small purchase threshold
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and Department of Defense acquisition workforce
manifests.conditions: Every five years, on October 1, in years evenly divisible by five
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add a DFARS rule (225.7002/x) mandating periodic review and potential CPI-based adjustment of the threshold, with corresponding notice requirements.
open_questions: Is additional implementation guidance needed on the methodology for CPI adjustment?
Should this process have a standing delegation or require action each cycle?

sections.section_heading: PROMPT PAYMENT OF CONTRACTORS.

manifests.description: Removes the limitation that prompt payment standards only apply if a specific payment date is not established by contract. Now, the statutory prompt payment rules apply regardless of whether a contract includes a specific payment date, meaning contractors may receive payments sooner and the agency cannot circumvent prompt payment rules by negotiating a specific date in the contract.
manifests.subject: Prompt payment rule applicability
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers and contractors (primes and subs)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Review DFARS 232.906 and 232.007 to ensure clauses do not restrict statutory prompt payment rules based on whether the contract specifies a payment date.
open_questions: Does this override any commonly-used DFARS clauses that incorporate contractually-negotiated payment schedules for special projects?

manifests.description: Authorizes prompt or accelerated payment to subcontractors not only if the prime contractor agrees, but also if the prime contractor proposes such payments. This increases the ability of prime contractors to enable faster payments to their subcontractors.
manifests.subject: Authority for accelerated payments to subcontractors
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers, prime contractors, and subcontractors
manifests.conditions: If the prime contractor agrees or proposes accelerated payments to its subcontractors
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 232.009 to allow accelerated payments when proposed by the prime contractor, not just when the prime contractor agrees.
open_questions: Should there be a defined process in DFARS for how a prime contractor can propose accelerated payment to ensure proper implementation?

sections.section_heading: DEFINITION OF MATERIAL WEAKNESS FOR CONTRACTOR BUSINESS SYSTEMS.

manifests.description: Raises the threshold for what triggers remedies or corrective actions for contractor business systems: all references to 'significant deficiency' in the DoD program for oversight of contractor business systems are replaced with 'material weakness.' This means only more serious issues—those likely to result in material misstatements in system information—will trigger system disapproval, payment withholds, or enhanced scrutiny.
manifests.subject: Threshold for deficiencies in contractor business systems
manifests.applies_to: Contractor business systems subject to DoD review and oversight
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update all instances of 'significant deficiency' in DFARS Subparts 215.407-5-70, 242.70X, clause 252.215-7002, and related contractor business system provisions to 'material weakness.'
open_questions: Should all DFARS clauses that use 'significant deficiency' as a standard for system review and remedy (e.g., withholding payments) update the term to 'material weakness,' or only those for DoD business systems?
Are contracts already in process before the law’s effective date subject to the new 'material weakness' standard or the old 'significant deficiency' standard?

manifests.description: Establishes a new statutory definition for 'material weakness' in contractor business systems: any deficiency, or combination of deficiencies, in internal controls over information such that there is a reasonable possibility of failing to prevent, or detect and correct, a material misstatement on a timely basis. 'Reasonable possibility' is defined as an event that is probable or more than remote but less than likely. This provides a more objective and precise standard for evaluating contractor business systems.
manifests.subject: Definition of 'material weakness' in contractor business systems
manifests.applies_to: Oversight and review of contractor business systems and determinations of system adequacy
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Insert the statutory definition of 'material weakness' in the relevant DFARS subparts and clauses (such as 252.215-7002 and 242.70X-series) regarding contractor business systems. Remove or replace prior definitions of 'significant deficiency.'
open_questions: Does DFARS need transitional guidance to clarify how ongoing business system reviews are handled when the new definition takes effect?

sections.section_heading: IMPROVED ENERGY SECURITY FOR MAIN OPERATING BASES IN EUROPE.

manifests.description: Prohibits Department of Defense contracts for supplying energy to main operating bases in Europe from using energy sourced from inside the Russian Federation. This aims to enhance energy security and decrease reliance on Russian energy sources at critical U.S. military locations in Europe.
manifests.subject: Prohibition on Russian-sourced energy for European bases
manifests.applies_to: All DoD contracts for the acquisition of energy for covered U.S. military installations in Europe (main operating bases)
manifests.conditions: Covers energy contracts where energy is furnished to a covered installation; subject to possible waiver as described in section.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add a new clause to DFARS part 241 (or a subpart) addressing sourcing requirements for energy contracts supporting covered U.S. military installations in Europe, prohibiting use of Russian-origin energy.
open_questions: Does this apply to all forms of energy contracting, including third party and energy savings performance contracts?
What process determines whether an installation is a 'main operating base'?

manifests.description: Authorizes the Secretary of Defense to waive the prohibition on Russian-sourced energy for a specific contract if necessary to ensure an adequate energy supply, but only with a certification to Congress that national security requirements have been balanced against the risks of Russian reliance.
manifests.subject: Waiver of prohibition for national security needs
manifests.applies_to: The Secretary of Defense for covered energy contracts
manifests.conditions: Waiver only if adequate supply cannot otherwise be ensured and the Secretary justifies the balance of national security requirements versus risks.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS waiver procedures, modeled after other waivers, with specific certification and Congressional notification requirements.
open_questions: What internal review is required before a waiver is approved?
Who else in DoD must participate in the certification or review process?

manifests.description: Requires that, at least 14 days prior to executing an energy contract under a waiver, the Secretary of Defense must notify the congressional defense committees, including the rationale, the basis for the waiver, an assessment of the impact on European energy resiliency, and an explanation of risk mitigation measures for using Russian energy.
manifests.subject: Advance notice and explanation of waivers to Congress
manifests.applies_to: The Secretary of Defense for any waived contract subject to the prohibition
manifests.conditions: Notice must be provided 14 days prior to contract execution for contracts where waiver is used.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add a reporting and submission step in the DFARS process for covered European energy contracts where waivers are used.
open_questions: What level of detail is required in the risk assessment and mitigation explanations?
What qualifies as adequate mitigation measures or an acceptable rationale?

manifests.description: Defines 'covered military installation' as a military installation in Europe identified by DoD as a main operating base, and 'furnished energy' as energy provided in any form or for any purpose, including heating, cooling, and electricity.
manifests.subject: Definitions used in new prohibition and waiver regime
manifests.applies_to: All parts of Section 2821 and related DFARS implementations
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Include these definitions in DFARS 241 or as part of a new specific section for European energy security.
open_questions: Is there a formal or published list of 'main operating bases' in Europe for use by contracting officers?
Should this be interpreted to cover all kinds of energy supply, regardless of business model or delivery method?

manifests.description: Repeals Section 2811 of the Military Construction Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2019, which had imposed similar but more limited restrictions and preferences for sourcing energy for the new Army Medical Center in Germany. The repeal aims to eliminate conflicting or duplicative requirements in favor of the new, broader regime.
manifests.subject: Repeal of prior specific Russian energy limitation for Germany medical center
manifests.applies_to: Prior requirements for the Army Medical Center energy source (Military Construction Authorization Act for FY2019, Sec. 2811)
amends: Military Construction Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2019, Section 2811 (Public Law 115-232; 132 Stat. 2266)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove any DFARS policies or procedures implementing the repealed 2019 provision, and supersede with new section.
open_questions: Are there any outstanding energy contracts or waivers under the 2019 section that need special handling or transition?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: Venezuela. 10 USC 2302 note.>> PROHIBITION ON CONTRACTING WITH PERSONS THAT HAVE BUSINESS OPERATIONS WITH THE MADURO REGIME.

manifests.description: The Department of Defense (DoD) is newly prohibited from entering into contracts for goods or services with any person or entity that conducts any form of business operations with an authority of the Government of Venezuela that is not recognized as legitimate by the United States government. The purpose is to restrict DoD resources from supporting or legitimizing the Maduro regime.
manifests.subject: Prohibition on contracting related to unrecognized Venezuelan authorities
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts for procurement of goods or services
manifests.conditions: Unless an exception under subsections (b), (c), or (d) applies; applies to business with Venezuelan authorities not recognized by the U.S. as the legitimate government
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment (December 20, 2019)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Implement as a new subpart (e.g., DFARS 225.7XX), modeling existing prohibitions such as 225.771 or 225.701, establishing screening and certification requirements for Venezuelan relationships.
open_questions: How should DoD verify which Venezuelan entities are not recognized by the U.S.? Is there an authoritative list?
What processes should the DoD use to determine ongoing business operations at the time of contract award and throughout contract performance?

manifests.description: Allows senior officials (the Secretary of Defense and the Secretary of State, jointly) to except contracts from the prohibition if necessary for humanitarian assistance, disaster relief, noncombatant evacuations, or if vital to U.S. national security interests. This provides flexibility for emergencies or overriding national interests.
manifests.subject: Executive waiver for humanitarian, disaster, evacuation, or national security needs
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts that would otherwise be prohibited under subsection (a)
manifests.conditions: Requires joint determination from Secretary of Defense and Secretary of State regarding the necessity of the contract for specified purposes
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment (December 20, 2019)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Establish a formal waiver process within the new DFARS coverage, similar to 225.770-5, requiring joint DoD/State approval, with documentation requirements.
open_questions: What is the required process and timeframe for making and recording these joint determinations?
Is there a required documentation or reporting form?

manifests.description: Requires that whenever the above executive waiver is exercised, the Secretary of Defense must notify the congressional defense committees, the House Foreign Affairs Committee, and the Senate Foreign Relations Committee. The aim is robust oversight of any exception granted.
manifests.subject: Congressional notification of exceptions/waivers
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.conditions: Applies to every contract entered into by DoD pursuant to a waiver under subsection (b)(1)
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment (December 20, 2019)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Require notification to relevant Congressional committees, modeled on notification requirements in DFARS 225.770-5 or similar reporting obligations.
open_questions: Must notification be prior to, concurrent with, or immediately after contract award?
What specific content is required in the notification?

manifests.description: Entities or persons holding a valid OFAC (Office of Foreign Assets Control) license to operate in Venezuela are exempt from the DoD contracting prohibition. This exception aligns with general U.S. sanctions policy and reflects existing Department of the Treasury vetting.
manifests.subject: Exception for contractors with valid OFAC license
manifests.applies_to: Persons/entities with a valid OFAC license to operate in Venezuela
manifests.conditions: Must possess a valid license issued by OFAC (U.S. Treasury)
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment (December 20, 2019)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add a cross-reference in the DFARS implementation (cf. 225.701-70) to recognize the OFAC licensing exception.
open_questions: How will DoD contracting officials validate the current validity of OFAC licenses?

manifests.description: Contracts relating to the operation and maintenance of U.S. Government consular offices and diplomatic posts in Venezuela are excluded from the prohibition. This ensures essential diplomatic functions can continue.
manifests.subject: Exception for support of U.S. diplomatic mission in Venezuela
manifests.applies_to: Contracts for operating and maintaining U.S. diplomatic and consular posts in Venezuela
manifests.conditions: Limited to activities related to those posts
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment (December 20, 2019)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Include this as a specific exception within the new DFARS section.
open_questions: Does this exception extend to contractors supporting allied or international missions at U.S. diplomatic facilities?

manifests.description: This language sets explicit definitions for terms in the section: 'business operations,' 'Government of Venezuela' (including political subdivisions and agencies as defined under 28 U.S.C. 1603(b)), and 'person' (covering entities, organizations, multilateral institutions under 22 U.S.C. 262r(c)(3), and all affiliates, parents, and subsidiaries). The broad definition aims to prevent circumvention through complex organizational structures.
manifests.subject: Definitions of business operations, Government of Venezuela, and person
manifests.applies_to: All determinations under this prohibition
manifests.conditions: Definitions are controlling for section 890 implementation
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment (December 20, 2019)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Include these definitions directly or by cross-reference in the new DFARS section to ensure comprehensive coverage.
open_questions: What types of documentation or diligence will suffice to establish 'common ownership or control'?
How should contracting officers address ambiguous affiliate/control arrangements?

manifests.description: Specifies that the prohibition and its exceptions apply only to contracts entered into on or after the date the law is enacted (i.e., prospectively). Existing contracts are not affected.
manifests.subject: Applicability to future contracts
manifests.applies_to: All Department of Defense contracts for goods and services
manifests.conditions: Applies only to contracts entered into on or after enactment date
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment (December 20, 2019)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add an effective date/applicability clause in the implementing DFARS section clarifying coverage of future awards.
open_questions: Do new options or exercised extensions on existing contracts count as 'entered into' post-enactment and are thus covered?

sections.section_heading: REAUTHORIZATION AND IMPROVEMENT OF DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE MENTOR-PROTEGE PROGRAM.

manifests.description: Extends the period during which the Department of Defense Mentor-Protege Program may enter into new agreements from September 30, 2018, to September 30, 2024. Allows agreements entered before October 1, 2024, to continue until September 30, 2026. These changes take effect once the Secretary of Defense submits the small business strategy required by 10 U.S.C. 2283 to Congress.
manifests.subject: Extension of Mentor-Protege Program authorization
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense Mentor-Protege Program
manifests.conditions: Takes effect on the date the Secretary of Defense submits the small business strategy under 10 U.S.C. 2283 to Congress.
manifests.effective_date: Upon submission of DoD small business strategy as required by 10 U.S.C. 2283
amends: 10 U.S.C. 2302 note (section 831(j) of NDAA FY91, as amended)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 219.71 and Appendix I to reflect new program end dates for agreement entry/continuation.

manifests.description: Reduces the maximum program participation term for Mentor-Protege agreements from three years to two years for new agreements, thereby shortening the period a protege may receive assistance.
manifests.subject: Maximum participation term
manifests.applies_to: New participants in the DoD Mentor-Protege Program
manifests.conditions: Applies upon effective date determined by mentor-protege program amendments (see above).
manifests.effective_date: Upon submission of DoD small business strategy as required by 10 U.S.C. 2283
amends: 10 U.S.C. 2302 note (section 831(e)(2) of NDAA FY91, as amended)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS and Appendix I to set maximum agreement participation to two years.
open_questions: Does the change apply retroactively to agreements already in force, or only to those entered after the effective date?

manifests.description: Requires the Office of Small Business Programs to establish performance goals and outcome-based metrics for the Mentor-Protege Program, review procedures for approving agreements, and submit a report on progress to Congress by February 1, 2020.
manifests.subject: Program goals, metrics, and periodic review/reporting
manifests.applies_to: Office of Small Business Programs (Department of Defense)
manifests.effective_date: Immediately on enactment; report due February 1, 2020
amends: 10 U.S.C. 2302 note (section 831, adds new subsection (n) as redesignated)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add explicit metric and reporting duties for Office of Small Business Programs to DFARS 219.71/Appendix I.
open_questions: Is ongoing reporting expected, or only the initial report required by February 1, 2020?

manifests.description: Expands eligibility for the Mentor-Protege Program by modifying the definition of 'disadvantaged small business concern': eligible firms may now be any that are 'not more than' the size standard for their industry, rather than only those 'less than half' the size standard.
manifests.subject: Definition of disadvantaged small business concern
manifests.applies_to: Small businesses seeking to participate as protégés in the DoD Mentor-Protege Program
manifests.effective_date: Immediately on enactment
amends: 10 U.S.C. 2302 note (section 831(o)(2) of NDAA FY91, as redesignated)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update all DFARS/Appendix I definitions to correspond with the revised eligibility standard.
open_questions: Are any other DFARS or internal DoD policy documents using the old 'less than half' standard that require coordination or updates?

manifests.description: Directs the Defense Business Board to independently evaluate the Mentor-Protege Program's effectiveness and recommend improvements. The Board must submit a report to Congress by March 31, 2022.
manifests.subject: Independent assessment by Defense Business Board
manifests.applies_to: Defense Business Board; Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: Report due by March 31, 2022
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure program office provides required information; no direct DFARS language change required.
open_questions: Will recommendations from the assessment prompt further statutory or regulatory change?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to submit an annual report to Congress, starting within 180 days of enactment and annually through September 30, 2024. The report must detail each mentor-protege agreement, the types of disadvantaged firms assisted, the nature of assistance, benefits to mentors, and protégés' progress in federal contracting.
manifests.subject: Annual program reporting to Congress
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; Congress
manifests.conditions: Annually, until September 30, 2024
manifests.effective_date: First report due within 180 days after enactment; annually thereafter until Sept. 30, 2024
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 219.7105 and Appendix I reporting requirements to ensure all required data are collected and organized for the new annual reports.
open_questions: Does this requirement supplement or replace existing reporting instructions in DFARS Appendix I?

sections.section_heading: DEFENSE ACQUISITION WORKFORCE CERTIFICATION, EDUCATION, AND CAREER FIELDS.

manifests.description: Requires all Department of Defense acquisition workforce members to obtain professional certification. Certifications should be based on third-party accredited programs (nationally or internationally recognized), but if they do not exist, the Secretary of Defense must establish comparable requirements.
manifests.subject: Universal professional certification requirement
manifests.applies_to: All DoD acquisition workforce members
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Create a new DFARS subpart requiring minimum professional certification standards for all acquisition workforce career fields, reference third-party standards, and specify Secretary's authority if a standard doesn't exist.
open_questions: What process will DoD use to determine the existence and adequacy of third-party certification for each career field?
How will the Secretary establish and implement internal requirements?

manifests.description: Makes continuing education and training a mandatory requirement (previously only encouraged) for the acquisition workforce.
manifests.subject: Mandatory continuing education and training
manifests.applies_to: All DoD acquisition workforce members
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise existing DFARS/PGI direction to mandate continuing education and training as a job duty, include in appointments and performance plans.

manifests.description: Requires explicit consequences, not just warnings, be included in the performance evaluation process for acquisition workforce members who consistently fail to meet standards.
manifests.subject: Consequences for underperformance
manifests.applies_to: All DoD acquisition workforce members
manifests.conditions: Applies to repeated or consistent failure to meet performance standards.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS/PGI performance management processes to expressly outline actions for ongoing underperformance.
open_questions: What types of consequences (e.g., retraining, demotion, removal) are intended?

manifests.description: Authorizes members of the acquisition workforce to participate in professional associations to gain leadership and management skills, as long as it's consistent with their performance plans.
manifests.subject: Professional association participation
manifests.applies_to: All DoD acquisition workforce members
manifests.conditions: Participation must align with the individual's performance plan.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Allow and possibly encourage participation in professional associations for career development, with credit where relevant.
open_questions: Should further guidance clarify conflict of interest or security concerns?

manifests.description: Eliminates the requirement that contracting officers and GS-1102/1102-like military positions complete at least 24 semester credit hours in specified business areas. The baccalaureate degree requirement remains.
manifests.subject: Removal of 24-credit education requirement
manifests.applies_to: Contracting officers and GS-1102 or equivalent DoD personnel
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove DFARS and PGI requirements for 24-credit coursework from contracting officer qualification.
open_questions: Should transitional provisions be created for individuals currently taking courses?

manifests.description: Simplifies selection criteria for acquisition positions by striking references to removed educational requirements. Conforming amendments throughout related subsections.
manifests.subject: Streamlining acquisition position selection criteria
manifests.applies_to: DoD acquisition workforce position candidates
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update all DFARS selection procedures to match the new single-degree educational requirement.
open_questions: How will experience and equivalency/waiver policies change, if at all?

manifests.description: Requires Defense Acquisition University to hire a minimum number of full-time visiting professors from civilian universities: at least five by September 1, 2021 and at least ten by September 1, 2022.
manifests.subject: DAU civilian faculty hiring requirements
manifests.applies_to: Defense Acquisition University faculty composition
manifests.conditions: Deadlines: Sept. 1, 2021 (5 professors); Sept. 1, 2022 (10 professors)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DAU faculty planning and contracting should specify quotas for external faculty and monitor deadlines.
open_questions: What is the full role of these faculty—teaching, curriculum development, advisory, or combination?

manifests.description: Expands curriculum development at DAU to require inclusion of commercial training providers in addition to military departments.
manifests.subject: Curriculum cooperation with commercial providers
manifests.applies_to: Defense Acquisition University curriculum and instructional development
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Refer to commercial providers as required stakeholders in any DAU curriculum DFARS/PGI text.

manifests.description: Expands list of designated acquisition workforce areas to include security cooperation and allows Secretary to designate additional positions as necessary.
manifests.subject: Security cooperation as acquisition position
manifests.applies_to: DoD acquisition workforce structure
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update definitions/lists in DFARS for workforce categories and ensure integration in workforce planning.

manifests.description: Mandates that each acquisition workforce career field must have an explicit career path developed and implemented in compliance with section 1722(a). Deadline for implementation is two years from enactment.
manifests.subject: Career path for each acquisition career field
manifests.applies_to: All acquisition workforce career fields
manifests.conditions: Must be developed and implemented within two years after enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Require all DoD components to formally publish and maintain career paths for each acquisition field.
open_questions: What oversight or standards govern adequacy of career paths?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to designate, by regulation within six months, the career fields in DoD that are acquisition workforce career fields.
manifests.subject: Designation of acquisition workforce career fields
manifests.applies_to: All DoD career fields relevant to acquisition
manifests.conditions: Deadline: six months from enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Publish updated lists of career fields classified as acquisition in regulation and DFARS/PGI.
open_questions: How often will this list be reviewed or updated in the future?

manifests.description: Mandates development and implementation of 'key work experiences' (such as multidisciplinary experience) for each acquisition career field. Requires the Secretary to send a plan to Congress within a year, specifying progress and resource commitment.
manifests.subject: Key work experiences for each career field
manifests.applies_to: All acquisition workforce career fields
manifests.conditions: Plan to Congress due one year after enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS/PGI to require and report multidisciplinary/key experience opportunities per career field.
open_questions: How should the quality and impact of these experiences be assessed?

manifests.description: Extends career path requirements to all acquisition workforce personnel, not just those in 'critical acquisition-related' positions.
manifests.subject: Career path requirement extension
manifests.applies_to: All DoD acquisition workforce
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure all workforce members, not just critical, are included in applicable career path policies.

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to establish, for each acquisition career field, defined proficiency standards and technical/non-technical competencies for civilian acquisition personnel, and assign resources to meet them. Implementation deadline is two years.
manifests.subject: Acquisition workforce competency development
manifests.applies_to: Civilian acquisition workforce in every acquisition career field
manifests.conditions: Implementation required by two years after enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Require inclusion of career field competency models and resource planning in workforce training policy.

manifests.description: Abolishes the Defense Acquisition Corps, repealing 10 U.S.C. 1731 and 1732. All references to the Corps are replaced or conformed throughout DoD acquisition statutes. Related statutory cleanup is implemented, renumbering and updating related sections and references.
manifests.subject: Termination of Defense Acquisition Corps and statutory conforming amendments
manifests.applies_to: All statutes referencing the Defense Acquisition Corps or its requirements
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Delete all DFARS/PGI references to the Defense Acquisition Corps. Update cross-references, forms, and requirements to refer to acquisition workforce/critical acquisition positions as now defined.
open_questions: Are there transition or grandfathering policies for former Corps members in place?

sections.section_heading: REQUIREMENT THAT CERTAIN SHIP COMPONENTS BE MANUFACTURED IN THE NATIONAL TECHNOLOGY AND INDUSTRIAL BASE.

manifests.description: Creates a new procurement limitation that requires large medium-speed diesel engines for auxiliary ships, when procured for new construction contracts by the Department of Defense, to be manufactured within the national technology and industrial base. This expands domestic manufacturing requirements to include these specific ship components for auxiliary ships, strengthening the U.S. defense industrial base and reducing reliance on foreign-made engines for these vessels.
manifests.subject: Domestic manufacturing requirement for auxiliary ship diesel engines
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense military departments awarding contracts for new auxiliary ships
manifests.conditions: Applies only to contracts for new construction of auxiliary ships (as later defined) using Sealift Fund or Shipbuilding/Conversion, Navy funds, awarded after December 20, 2019
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add a new requirement to DFARS Part 225 and subpart 225.71 regarding procurement of auxiliary ship engines, similar to existing coverage for other vessel components.
open_questions: Should waivers and exceptions follow procedures used for other NTIB procurement limitations in DFARS?
Is a regulatory or technical definition of 'large medium-speed diesel engines' required for consistent enforcement across contracts?

manifests.description: Defines the scope and applicability of the new procurement limitation: it applies only to contracts awarded after December 20, 2019, for new construction of auxiliary ships (excluding icebreakers and special mission ships), and only when funding is provided by the National Defense Sealift Fund or Shipbuilding and Conversion, Navy. This ensures the requirement is targeted to future shipbuilding actions and that certain classes of ships are excluded.
manifests.subject: Applicability and scope of auxiliary ship engine procurement limitation
manifests.applies_to: Contracts for new auxiliary ships (excluding icebreakers and special mission ships) by Department of Defense military departments, using specified funds
manifests.conditions: Only for contracts awarded after enactment date (December 20, 2019), using National Defense Sealift Fund or Shipbuilding and Conversion, Navy funds
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Clarify scope and exclusions in DFARS 225.7010 or related supplementary regulations; ensure the definitions and applicability are reflected for contracting officers.
open_questions: Should the exclusions for icebreakers and special mission ships be added to DFARS definitions or referenced in relevant clauses?
Do terms like 'National Defense Sealift Fund' and 'Shipbuilding and Conversion, Navy' need further regulatory definition for program assignment?

sections.section_heading: AUTHORIZED OFFICIAL TO CARRY OUT THE PROCUREMENT TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE COOPERATIVE AGREEMENT PROGRAM.

manifests.description: Transfers responsibility for carrying out the Procurement Technical Assistance Cooperative Agreement Program under chapter 142 of Title 10 from the Director of the Defense Logistics Agency to the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment, effective October 1, 2021. This makes the Under Secretary the official acting for the Secretary of Defense in all matters related to this program.
manifests.subject: Change of responsible official for procurement technical assistance program
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense, specifically roles associated with oversight and execution of the Procurement Technical Assistance Cooperative Agreement Program
manifests.conditions: Effective October 1, 2021
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS and PGI language to reflect Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment as the responsible official instead of the Director of Defense Logistics Agency for the program.
open_questions: Are there additional legacy tasks, delegations, or contracts requiring specific transition planning?
Are further clarifying instructions required for the Under Secretary’s new role?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to provide, by November 1, 2020, a written report and briefing to the congressional defense committees concerning activities undertaken to prepare for the transition of responsibilities for the Procurement Technical Assistance Cooperative Agreement Program from DLA to the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment.
manifests.subject: Report to Congress on program transition preparations
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.conditions: Not later than November 1, 2020
