manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: No DFARS implementation required; ensure internal DoD reporting offices are aware for compliance.
open_questions: Is there a preferred or required format or template for this report and briefing?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense, starting with the FY2021 defense budget and for each FY thereafter, to include in the defense budget materials a budget justification display for the Procurement Technical Assistance Cooperative Agreement Program as part of the Operation and Maintenance, Defense-wide appropriation for the Office of the Secretary of Defense.
manifests.subject: Recurring annual budget justification display
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense (DoD budget offices responsible for O&M, Defense-wide)
manifests.conditions: Beginning with the FY2021 budget and each year after
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure DoD budget guidance and DFARS/PGI reference the requirement for an annual display for the program within O&M, Defense-wide submissions.
open_questions: Are there required contents or data fields for this justification display beyond what is standard for OSD or similar programs?

sections.section_heading: MODIFICATION OF PROHIBITION ON ACQUISITION OF SENSITIVE MATERIALS FROM NON-ALLIED FOREIGN NATIONS.

manifests.description: Broadens the restriction on sales from the National Defense Stockpile to adversary nations so that it applies to all 'material', instead of only 'covered material'. This makes the scope of restricted sales slightly broader.
manifests.subject: Expansion of Stockpile Sales Restriction Terminology
manifests.applies_to: National Defense Stockpile Manager and sales involving North Korea, China, Russia, or Iran
manifests.conditions: Sale is to a 'covered nation' or an entity/third party acting on behalf of a covered nation and is not determined by the Stockpile Manager to be in the national interests of the United States.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Review DFARS 225.7018 restrictions and definitions concerning stockpile sales; ensure the broader term 'material' is reflected.
open_questions: Does the use of 'material' rather than 'covered material' require further regulatory clarification on what materials are intended to be included?

manifests.description: Adds tantalum metals and alloys to the definition of 'covered material', making these subject to the DoD procurement and stockpile sale prohibitions that apply to sourcing from North Korea, China, Russia, or Iran.
manifests.subject: Tantalum Added as Covered Material
manifests.applies_to: DoD procurement and National Defense Stockpile activities involving tantalum metals and alloys
manifests.conditions: Material is tantalum metals or alloys; must not be procured from, or sold to, a covered nation or its agents unless an exception applies.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Verify DFARS 225.7018 and contract clause 252.225-7052 explicitly incorporate tantalum metals and alloys as covered material.
open_questions: Are transition provisions or grandfathering needed for existing DoD contracts involving tantalum?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2304 note.>> MODIFICATION OF JUSTIFICATION AND APPROVAL REQUIREMENT FOR CERTAIN DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE CONTRACTS.

manifests.description: Raises the threshold below which a justification and approval (J&A) is required for Department of Defense sole-source contracts in a covered procurement from $20 million to $100 million. After this change, no J&A is required for DoD sole-source contracts in covered procurements not exceeding $100 million.
manifests.subject: Sole-source contract J&A threshold increase
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers for covered sole-source procurements
manifests.conditions: Sole-source covered procurements not exceeding $100,000,000
amends: Section 811 of NDAA FY2010 (Public Law 111-84; 123 Stat. 2405)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 206.302 and associated J&A thresholds for DoD sole-source covered procurements.
open_questions: What is the precise definition of 'covered procurement' as referenced here, and does it require additional DFARS clarification?

manifests.description: Establishes that for sole-source contracts in a covered procurement exceeding $100 million, the justification approval must be made by the official designated in 10 U.S.C. 2304(f)(1)(B)(ii), i.e., the head of the procuring activity or their senor-level designee.
manifests.subject: Approval authority for J&A over $100M
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers and approving officials for sole-source contracts above $100M
manifests.conditions: Sole-source covered procurement contracts exceeding $100,000,000
amends: Section 811 of NDAA FY2010 (Public Law 111-84; 123 Stat. 2405)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 206.304 — update approval authorities for DoD sole-source covered procurements above $100 million.
open_questions: Should this approval threshold change harmonize with any non-DoD federal sole-source J&A requirements?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to issue guidance implementing the new sole-source justification authority and thresholds within 90 days of enactment.
manifests.subject: Implementation guidance for new J&A thresholds
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; all DoD contracting activities
manifests.conditions: Guidance must be issued within 90 days after enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Temporary class deviation and subsequent DFARS update; may also require PGI or DoD-wide contracting guidance.
open_questions: Is specific form (Interim rule, PGI, class deviation) for initial guidance dictated?

manifests.description: Mandates that the Department of Defense track all uses of the revised authority (i.e., the $100 million threshold for J&A waivers on covered procurements) and make these data available to the Comptroller General for oversight and reporting.
manifests.subject: Data tracking and transparency for sole-source J&A waivers
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense procurement and data management offices
manifests.conditions: Covers all sole-source contracts using the changed threshold/requirement
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add data tracking instructions to DFARS PGI or other internal DoD contracting systems guidance.
open_questions: Which IT system or process will be formally used for this tracking? Is a new reporting form required?

manifests.description: Requires the Comptroller General to submit a report to congressional defense committees by March 1, 2022, assessing the financial effect of the change on native corporations, businesses, and communities, the types and extents of contracts excluded from J&A by this change, and any other matters the Comptroller General considers appropriate.
manifests.subject: GAO report on effects of J&A threshold change
manifests.applies_to: Comptroller General; indirectly, all contractors and businesses affected by J&A waivers
manifests.conditions: Report due March 1, 2022; covers changes through end of FY21
manifests.effective_date: March 1, 2022 (for report delivery)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: No direct DFARS action; ensure DoD is prepared to provide all tracked data to the Comptroller General as required.
open_questions: Will additional analysis be required by DoD to support GAO's review, or is it strictly a data provision obligation?

sections.section_heading: MODIFICATION OF WRITTEN APPROVAL REQUIREMENT FOR TASK AND DELIVERY ORDER SINGLE CONTRACT AWARDS.

manifests.description: Clarifies and restructures the requirement that, except as provided under a new exception, no task or delivery order contract with an estimated value exceeding $100,000,000 (including options) may be awarded to a single source unless the head of the agency determines in writing that one of four specific conditions justifies the award. The amendment reorganizes the statutory language for clarity and updates the labeling of referenced subparagraphs and clauses.
manifests.subject: Written determination required for single-award task/delivery orders over $100M
manifests.applies_to: Heads of defense agencies awarding large task or delivery order contracts
manifests.conditions: Contracts expected to exceed $100,000,000 (including all options); unless otherwise excepted
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 216.504; ensure the requirement for a written determination by the agency head for single-award contracts over $100M is maintained and references updated.
open_questions: Are there subordinate regulations or internal agency instructions that should be checked for parallel cross-referencing updates due to the change in clause structure?

manifests.description: Creates a new exception allowing a task or delivery order contract estimated to exceed $100,000,000 (including all options) to be awarded to a single source without the standard written determination, if the head of the agency has made a written determination under 10 U.S.C. 2304(c) that justifies the use of noncompetitive procedures for the award.
manifests.subject: Exception to written determination requirement for single-source contracts over $100M
manifests.applies_to: Heads of defense agencies awarding task or delivery order contracts over $100M to a single source
manifests.conditions: The head of agency has made a written 2304(c) determination that use of procedures other than competitive procedures may be used
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 216.504 and related coverage; add cross-references and guidance reflecting that the written determination requirement is excepted if 2304(c) noncompetitive justification is documented by the agency head.
open_questions: Are there places in DFARS or agency practices where the new exception should be highlighted to prevent unnecessary duplicative justifications?

manifests.description: Reorganizes statutory language by relabeling certain subparagraphs and clauses for improved clarity and internal consistency. This technical update enables more accurate referencing but does not have independent substantive effect.
manifests.subject: Technical correction of cross-references and structure
manifests.applies_to: Statutory text and all users referencing 10 U.S.C. 2304a(d)(3)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Check DFARS and PGI for any cross-references to clause or subparagraph citations and update as needed.

sections.section_heading: DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE USE OF FIXED-PRICE CONTRACTS.

manifests.description: Requires the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment to conduct a review of how the Department of Defense makes decisions to use fixed-price contracts, including various types of such contracts like fixed-price incentive contracts. The goal is to ensure these decisions are made strategically and consistently department-wide to better support acquisition objectives.
manifests.subject: Review of Fixed-Price Contract Decision-Making
manifests.applies_to: Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Potential updates to PGI or guidance for DFARS 216.403, 235.006, or 232.702 to reflect the findings on fixed-price contract selection.
open_questions: What aspects of current fixed-price decision processes will require change following the review?

manifests.description: Requires the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment to brief the congressional defense committees on the findings of the fixed-price contracts review no later than February 1, 2020.
manifests.subject: Report on Fixed-Price Contract Review
manifests.applies_to: Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment

manifests.description: Requires the Comptroller General to submit a report to the congressional defense committees by February 1, 2021, detailing the Department of Defense's use of fixed-price contracts. The report must include how often these contracts are used, the situations where they are used, the effects of these decisions on costs or efficiencies, and how different types of fixed-price contracts affect contract closeout.
manifests.subject: Comptroller General Report on Fixed-Price Contracts
manifests.applies_to: Comptroller General (GAO)

manifests.description: Delays the effective date of regulations requiring firm fixed-price contracts for Foreign Military Sales (FMS), as prescribed by FY17 NDAA §830, until December 31, 2020. The regulations, when they take effect, must also take into account the findings from the new review of DoD fixed-price contract use mandated in this section.
manifests.subject: Delayed Implementation for FMS Fixed-Price Contract Regulations
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense regulations on Foreign Military Sales contracting
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS Subpart 225.73 (Foreign Military Sales) and policy or PGI implementing FMS contract type requirements.
open_questions: How will the findings from the review affect the content of the final regulations for FMS contracts?

sections.section_heading: FAILURE TO PROVIDE OTHER THAN CERTIFIED COST OR PRICING DATA UPON REQUEST.

manifests.description: Prohibits contracting officers from determining a contract or subcontract price to be fair and reasonable based solely on the prices historically paid by the Government. This requires contracting officers to obtain and use additional data or context when conducting price reasonableness determinations.
manifests.subject: Prohibition on sole reliance on historical Government prices for fair/reasonable price determinations
manifests.applies_to: DoD contracting officers determining price reasonableness for contracts and subcontracts
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise DFARS 215.403-3 and relevant PGI or procedures to prohibit sole reliance on historical Government prices in price analysis.
open_questions: What qualifies as sufficient 'additional' information beyond historical prices?
Are there exceptions for certain procurements (e.g., commercial items) allowed under this prohibition?

manifests.description: Establishes that if an offeror fails to make a good faith effort to comply with a reasonable request to submit data (other than certified cost or pricing data) and the contracting officer cannot determine price reasonableness by other means, the offeror is ineligible for award. The head of the contracting activity may override this ineligibility if awarding is in the best interest of the Government, considering specified factors such as urgency, risk, and efforts made.
manifests.subject: Ineligibility for award for failure to provide requested pricing data
manifests.applies_to: Offerors on contracts or subcontracts subject to 10 U.S.C. 2306a(d) data requirements
manifests.conditions: Contracting officer cannot determine price reasonableness by other means and offeror fails to make a good faith effort to comply with a reasonable data request
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add explicit ineligibility criteria in DFARS 215.403-3 for non-compliance, and processes for head of contracting activity override with documentation of the required factors.
open_questions: What constitutes a 'good faith effort' to provide data?
What documentation is required for an override decision?

manifests.description: Requires heads of contracting activities (or their designees) to report, on a quarterly basis, each instance in which a determination is made to override the ineligibility provision and make an award to an offeror who failed to provide requested data.
manifests.subject: Reporting on overrides of ineligibility for award
manifests.applies_to: Heads of contracting activities or their designees
manifests.conditions: When an override determination is made under the new authority
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add DFARS reporting provision requiring quarterly submissions to the Principal Director, Defense Pricing and Contracting on these cases.
open_questions: Is there a standardized reporting format?
Are these reports integrated with any other price reasonableness or procurement reporting requirements?

manifests.description: Directs the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment (or a designee) to produce an annual report identifying offerors who denied multiple requests for uncertified cost or pricing data over the prior three years but still received awards. The report must identify products or services from those offerors that should undergo should-cost analysis.
manifests.subject: Annual reporting on recurring non-compliant offerors and identification of products/services for should-cost analysis
manifests.applies_to: USD(A&S); offerors who denied multiple data requests but won awards
manifests.conditions: Denial of multiple government data requests over three years and receipt of contract award
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Require a DFARS subpart or PGI supplement to direct the annual report’s preparation and use relevant DoD data systems for compliance tracking.
open_questions: How many 'denials' qualify as 'multiple' for inclusion in the report?
What is the standard for identifying products/services for should-cost analysis?

manifests.description: Authorizes the Secretary of Defense to notate the past performance system used by the federal government to include information about offerors who repeatedly deny data requests but still receive contract awards.
manifests.subject: Notation in past performance systems for repeatedly non-compliant offerors
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; past performance tracking systems
manifests.conditions: When an offeror is identified in the annual USD(A&S) report as having denied multiple data requests yet received contract awards
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Modify DFARS/PGI and CPARS or other relevant system guidance to permit/require notations for this scenario.
open_questions: Is the notation process mandatory or discretionary?
Which past performance systems (e.g., CPARS, SAM) are affected and how does notation affect future evaluations?

manifests.description: Requires the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment to assess whether identified non-compliant offerors are sole source providers and to develop strategies to incentivize entry of new suppliers to increase alternative sources for the affected products or services.
manifests.subject: Assessment of sole source status and strategy for increasing sources in the industrial base
manifests.applies_to: USD(A&S)
manifests.conditions: After identification of offerors repeatedly denying government data requests
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Publish assessment and strategies as part of PGI, DFARS procedures, and market research requirements at DFARS Subpart 207.1.
open_questions: What methods/tools will be used for the industrial base assessment?
What specific incentives will be developed and how will potential new entrants be supported and tracked?

sections.section_heading: EXPANSION AND ENHANCEMENT OF AUTHORITIES ON TRANSFER AND ADOPTION OF MILITARY ANIMALS.

manifests.description: Expands and clarifies the authorities for military animal 'transfer' in addition to 'adoption' throughout 10 U.S.C. § 2583, so that military animals may be either transferred or adopted, and associated procedures and liability limitations apply to both.
manifests.subject: Transfer and Adoption Authority Expansion
manifests.applies_to: Military departments managing military animals
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure DFARS text, guidance, and definitions refer to both 'transfer' and 'adoption' for military animal disposition.

manifests.description: Requires the relevant military department to transport military working dogs to the Veterinary Treatment Facility at Lackland AFB, TX for veterinary screening and necessary care (including surgery for mental, dental, or stress-related illness) prior to further disposition when the dog is to be retired, unless medical circumstances make this not in the dog’s best interest.
manifests.subject: Retirement Veterinary Screening and Care
manifests.applies_to: Military working dogs to be retired
manifests.conditions: Dog is not outside the United States or, if so, Secretary determines transport is medically inadvisable
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add requirement for transport to Lackland and pre-disposition veterinary screening/care for retiring military working dogs in DFARS.
open_questions: What specific documentation or process is required to invoke the exception for not transporting a dog outside the United States for medical reasons?

manifests.description: Revises procedures for transporting retired military working dogs to ensure that after veterinary screening and care, dogs are moved either to overseas adopting U.S. citizens/service members, or within the U.S. to specified locations for adoption or transfer.
manifests.subject: Transportation Procedure After Veterinary Care
manifests.applies_to: Military working dogs being retired
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS logistics policies should align transport destinations for adopted dogs according to post-vet care requirements.

manifests.description: Amends the policy statement to clarify that preference for transfer of military working dogs to law enforcement agencies applies before the end of the dogs’ useful working lives, instead of just 'adoption.'
manifests.subject: Law Enforcement Agency Transfer Policy
manifests.applies_to: Military departments handling military working dog disposition
manifests.conditions: Dog is still within useful working life
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS adoption/transfer prioritization language to accord with clarified statutory order.

manifests.description: Clarifies that all horses, mules, or donkeys (equids) owned by DoD qualify as 'military animals' for purposes of transfer or adoption authority, not just horses.
manifests.subject: Definition of Military Animals (Equids)
manifests.applies_to: All equids owned by DoD
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Broaden DFARS definitions to ensure all applicable equids are covered.

manifests.description: Requires all contracts for provision of contract working dogs to explicitly include terms for post-service transfer of the dog to the 341st Training Squadron for veterinary screening and care per 10 U.S.C. § 2583, and updates cross-reference language accordingly.
manifests.subject: Contract Working Dog Transfer Terms
manifests.applies_to: All DoD contracts for contract working dogs
manifests.conditions: At the conclusion of the dog's service life
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Contracting clauses for contract working dog services must mandate post-service transfer to 341st Training Squadron and link to updated veterinary screening and care requirement.

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2661 note prec.>> REPLACEMENT OF FLUORINATED AQUEOUS FILM-FORMING FOAM WITH FLUORINE-FREE FIRE-FIGHTING AGENT.

manifests.description: Mandates that the Secretary of the Navy publish a military specification for a fluorine-free fire-fighting agent for all military installations by January 31, 2023, and ensure the agent is available for use by October 1, 2023. The intent is to provide a standardized, approved alternative to fluorinated foam before the prohibition deadline.
manifests.subject: Military specification for fluorine-free fire-fighting agent
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of the Navy; all military installations
manifests.conditions: Not later than January 31, 2023 (spec published); agent available by October 1, 2023
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS Procedures, Guidance, and Information (PGI) on equipment spec and contracting language for fire-fighting agents.
open_questions: How is 'available for use' defined (Does it require presence on-hand at all installations or only that the agent can be procured)?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to submit a detailed report to the congressional defense committees, concurrent with the spec publication, with a plan to implement the transition to a fluorine-free fire-fighting agent by October 1, 2023. The report must cover progress, technology/equipment needs, funding, and a timeline.
manifests.subject: Report to Congress on transition to fluorine-free foam
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.conditions: Concurrent with military spec publication; by Jan 31, 2023
manifests.effective_date: By Jan 31, 2023
open_questions: Is the report intended for public release, or only to Congress?
What are the consequences if Congress finds the implementation plan inadequate?

manifests.description: Prohibits the obligation or expenditure of DoD funds after October 1, 2023, to procure fire-fighting foam containing more than one part per billion of perfluoroalkyl substances or polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), with exceptions for shipboard use. This enforces the chemical standard for foam acquisitions.
manifests.subject: Procurement prohibition for legacy fluorinated fire-fighting foam
manifests.applies_to: All DoD procurement personnel and contractors
manifests.conditions: Applies after October 1, 2023; threshold is 1 ppb PFAS/PFOS
manifests.effective_date: October 1, 2023
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Implemented at DFARS 223.7402 and 252.223-7009 for procurement contracts
open_questions: Does this retroactively affect existing contracts or only new obligations after this date?

manifests.description: Prohibits, after October 1, 2024 (or earlier if feasible), the use of fluorinated aqueous film-forming foam at all military installations, not just its procurement. This ensures an operational phase-out, compelling transition even if some legacy stock exists.
manifests.subject: Ban on use of fluorinated fire-fighting foam
manifests.applies_to: All military installations
manifests.conditions: Effective on the earlier of October 1, 2024 or the date on which the Secretary determines full compliance is possible
manifests.effective_date: The earlier of October 1, 2024 or date determined by Secretary
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS should address not just procurement but 'use' by contractors/personnel; recommend policy guidance to installations.
open_questions: How will DoD enforce prohibition of use at the installation level? What are the penalties for non-compliance?

manifests.description: Exempts fire-fighting foam intended for use solely onboard ocean-going vessels from the procurement and use bans. This acknowledges unique shipboard firefighting requirements.
manifests.subject: Exemption for shipboard foam use/procurement
manifests.applies_to: Procurement/use for ocean-going vessels
manifests.conditions: Foam for use solely onboard ocean-going vessels
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 223.7402 shipboard exemption already implemented
open_questions: Does this apply to all classes of military vessels (e.g., Coast Guard, MSC, Navy support ships)?

manifests.description: Authorizes the Secretary of Defense (but not subordinates) to grant a waiver to the use prohibition under very strict conditions, including required notification, justification, one-year limit (plus one-year extension), and public notice. Waivers are only allowed if necessary for life/safety and if no alternative solutions are available.
manifests.subject: Waiver authority for foam use ban
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; military installations
manifests.conditions: Requires congressional briefing, written certification, and public notice at least 60 days before waiver; limited to up to two years max (with extension) under strict findings
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Recommend implementing waiver procedures in DFARS or PGI, with explicit conditions and reporting requirements
open_questions: What constitutes sufficient documentation for certification and briefing?
How is public notice to be accomplished?

manifests.description: Defines 'perfluoroalkyl substances' and 'polyfluoroalkyl substances' for purposes of this section, ensuring clarity in what chemicals are regulated under the foam bans.
manifests.subject: Definitions of PFAS/PFOS
manifests.applies_to: All military foam contracting, procurement, and use actions
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 252.223-7009 incorporates these definitions by reference; ensure definitions control in related clauses
open_questions: How are future changes in chemical science/definitions for PFAS handled?
Do these definitions override EPA/OSHA or other agency definitions?

sections.section_heading: SMALL BUSINESS SET-ASIDE FOR CONTRACTS FOR ARCHITECTURAL AND ENGINEERING SERVICES AND CONSTRUCTION DESIGN.

manifests.description: Raises the monetary threshold for mandatory small business set-aside of contracts for architectural and engineering services and construction design related to military construction or family housing projects from $300,000 to $1,000,000. Contracts under $1 million must now be set aside for small businesses, which increases opportunities for small business participation.
manifests.subject: Raise threshold for small business set-aside
manifests.applies_to: DoD contracting for architect-engineer services and construction design on military construction or family housing projects
manifests.conditions: If estimated award amount is less than $1,000,000
manifests.effective_date: Fiscal year 2019 and each succeeding fiscal year
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 219.502-1 and 219.502-2 to raise the dollar threshold for small business set-asides for architect-engineer services to $1,000,000.
open_questions: Should application for ID/IQ contracts or multi-year awards be clarified under the new threshold?
Was the $400,000 previously in DFARS explicitly authorized, and how does it interact with the new statutory threshold?

manifests.description: Makes it mandatory that contracts for architect-engineer services under the $1,000,000 threshold must be awarded as a small business set-aside in accordance with the Small Business Act. Removes prior language that prohibited set-aside at or above the threshold, clarifying that below-threshold contracts are required to be set aside for small businesses.
manifests.subject: Mandatory small business set-aside below threshold
manifests.applies_to: DoD contracting officers awarding architect-engineer services and construction design contracts
manifests.conditions: If estimated award amount is less than $1,000,000
manifests.effective_date: Fiscal year 2019 and each succeeding fiscal year
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure DFARS 219.502-1, 219.502-2, and related subparts specify that contracts under $1,000,000 must be set aside for small businesses.
open_questions: Do any exceptions or agency waiver authorities under the Small Business Act affect this statutory mandate?

manifests.description: Removes the authority of the Secretary of Defense to administratively revise the set-aside threshold for these contracts, so that only Congress can change the threshold in the future.
manifests.subject: Elimination of administrative threshold adjustment
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; DoD contracting for architect-engineer services
manifests.conditions: N/A
manifests.effective_date: Fiscal year 2019 and each succeeding fiscal year
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove any DFARS provision referencing DoD discretion to revise the set-aside threshold for architect-engineer services.
open_questions: Does DFARS currently reference any discretionary authority for DoD to adjust this threshold that must be repealed?

manifests.description: Applies the amendments beginning with fiscal year 2019 and for all subsequent fiscal years, clarifying that agencies must follow the new rules for contracts awarded from FY2019 onward.
manifests.subject: Effective date of amendments
manifests.applies_to: DoD and all applicable contracting activities
manifests.conditions: Contracts awarded for fiscal year 2019 and subsequent fiscal years
manifests.effective_date: Fiscal year 2019
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS guidance and communications to ensure implementation for all contracts solicited or awarded in FY2019 or after.

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 240d note.>> TRANSPARENCY OF ACCOUNTING FIRMS USED TO SUPPORT DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE AUDIT.

manifests.description: Requires accounting firms that provide financial statement auditing or audit remediation services to the Department of Defense, in support of audits required under 31 U.S.C. 3521, to submit a statement detailing any disciplinary proceedings involving the firm or its associated persons before any entity authorized to enforce rules or laws applicable to audit services. This requirement applies to all contract actions (including awards, renewals, and amendments) occurring more than 180 days after the enactment of the Act. The purpose is to improve DoD's transparency regarding the professional standing and compliance history of accounting firms conducting or supporting its audits.
manifests.subject: Disclosure of disciplinary proceedings by accounting firms serving DoD audits
manifests.applies_to: Any accounting firm providing audit or audit remediation services to DoD in support of audits under 31 U.S.C. 3521
manifests.conditions: All contract actions (including awards, renewals, and amendments) more than 180 days after enactment of NDAA FY19
manifests.effective_date: Contract actions occurring more than 180 days after the date of enactment of NDAA FY19
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add a new DFARS subpart or clause requiring accounting firms providing financial statement audit or remediation services to DoD to disclose details of disciplinary proceedings involving the firm or associated persons for all covered contract actions.
open_questions: Is the disclosure statement required only at the time of the contract action, or must it be updated during contract performance if new disciplinary proceedings arise?
How should 'associated persons' be defined for the purpose of this requirement?
Which entities are considered 'entities with authority to enforce compliance'—does it include state licensing boards, SEC, PCAOB, international regulators?
What level of detail is required in the disclosure (e.g., findings, pending investigations, outcomes, sanctions)?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2302 note.>> INSTRUCTION ON PILOT PROGRAM REGARDING EMPLOYMENT OF PERSONS WITH DISABILITIES.

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to update the Defense Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement (DFARS) to provide instruction about the pilot program—authorized by section 853 of the FY2004 NDAA—that promotes awarding contracts to eligible contractors employing at least 33% severely disabled individuals, with specific requirements for fair compensation and benefits. This ensures contracting officers are informed and able to implement the demonstration program, with the instruction to be issued within 180 days after enactment of the FY2019 NDAA.
manifests.subject: DFARS instruction on pilot program for employment of persons with disabilities
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and Department of Defense contracting officials
manifests.conditions: Instruction must be included in DFARS within 180 days after enactment of the FY2019 NDAA
manifests.effective_date: Within 180 days after enactment of FY2019 NDAA
source_authorities: Section 853, NDAA FY2004 (10 U.S.C. 2302 note; Public Law 108-136)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: A new section or instruction to be added to DFARS—potentially within or referenced from Subpart 222.14 (Employment of Workers with Disabilities) or 208.7 (Acquisition from Nonprofit Agencies Employing People Who Are Blind or Severely Disabled)—specifically describing the demonstration project, contractor eligibility (33% severely disabled employees, minimum wage, comparable benefits), evaluation criteria, and implementation procedures for contracting officials.
open_questions: Should the instruction be issued as a standalone DFARS subpart, an appendix, or integrated into existing subparts (222.14 or 208.7)?
Should specific procedures be included for verifying eligibility and benefit requirements?
How should this instruction interact with, or be distinguished from, existing DFARS provisions on employment of people with disabilities, which are not limited to the demonstration project?

sections.section_heading: PERMANENT SUPPLY CHAIN RISK MANAGEMENT AUTHORITY.

manifests.description: Creates a permanent statutory authority for the Secretary of Defense and the Secretaries of the Army, Navy, and Air Force to (1) exclude sources from defense procurement actions or (2) limit disclosure of information, when justified by significant supply chain risk to national security systems or covered information technology, following a rigorous risk assessment and notification procedure. This authority formalizes and makes permanent the previously temporary authority to mitigate supply chain risk related to covered systems and certain IT procurements.
manifests.subject: Permanent supply chain risk management authority
manifests.applies_to: Heads of covered DoD agencies (Secretary of Defense and Secretaries of the Army, Navy, and Air Force), and related DoD procurement actions for covered systems or IT
manifests.conditions: May be exercised only after a joint recommendation by senior DoD officials based on a risk assessment, concurrence of appropriate officials, and notification to the appropriate congressional committees as specified in the statute.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 239.73 and related contract clauses should be updated to reference 10 U.S.C. 2339a instead of the now-repealed section 806 authority, making the risk mitigation and non-disclosure authority permanent.
open_questions: Should DFARS conform only regarding nomenclature, or are procedural clarifications needed for updated titles/responsibilities (e.g., Under Secretary roles)?
Is further guidance needed to clarify the new statutory definitions of 'covered procurement action' beyond what is provided?

manifests.description: Repeals the time-limited supply chain risk management authority granted in section 806 of the FY11 NDAA, since that authority is now replaced by a permanent statutory provision.
manifests.subject: Repeal of obsolete supply chain risk authority
manifests.applies_to: All DoD supply chain risk management actions previously authorized solely by section 806 of the FY11 NDAA.
manifests.conditions: Becomes effective upon enactment of this section, with all new actions governed by 10 U.S.C. 2339a.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove references to section 806 authority in DFARS and replace with references to 10 U.S.C. 2339a.
open_questions: Are there legacy/procedural situations initiated under section 806 that require grandfathering or continued recognition, or does all authority transfer to 2339a immediately?

manifests.description: Makes a conforming clerical amendment by adding a new item to the table of sections for chapter 137 of title 10, reflecting the creation of section 2339a.
manifests.subject: Clerical update to table of sections
manifests.applies_to: Table of sections at start of 10 U.S.C. Chapter 137

sections.section_heading: PROMOTION OF THE USE OF GOVERNMENT-WIDE AND OTHER INTERAGENCY CONTRACTS.

manifests.description: Clarifies that the requirement for a written agreement assigning administration and management responsibility between agencies applies specifically to all interagency assisted acquisitions, focusing compliance on these types of acquisitions rather than all interagency acquisitions in general.
manifests.subject: Written agreement requirement for interagency assisted acquisitions
manifests.applies_to: All interagency assisted acquisitions involving Federal executive agencies
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 217.502-1 and related PGI sections—update to reference and align with the clarified scope for assisted acquisitions.
open_questions: Does this clarification mean other forms of interagency acquisition (such as 'direct acquisitions') are now exempt from this requirement?

manifests.description: Eliminates the requirement for a determination that an interagency acquisition is the best procurement alternative, removing this mandate from compliance requirements for these transactions.
manifests.subject: Best procurement alternative determination removal
manifests.applies_to: All interagency acquisitions governed by 31 U.S.C. 1535 note
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove or revise DFARS language referencing best procurement alternative determination for interagency acquisitions.
open_questions: Does any other existing regulation (e.g., FAR) still require such a determination, potentially resulting in regulatory inconsistency?

manifests.description: Clarifies that the requirement for sufficient documentation to ensure an adequate audit applies specifically to all interagency assisted acquisitions, narrowing previous requirements that applied to all interagency acquisitions.
manifests.subject: Audit documentation for interagency assisted acquisitions
manifests.applies_to: All interagency assisted acquisitions involving Federal executive agencies
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 217.502-1 and PGI for audit documentation procedures to specify scope is 'assisted acquisitions.'
open_questions: Does this mean 'direct acquisitions' are excluded from audit documentation requirements?
Should the DFARS further delineate requirements between types of interagency acquisitions?

sections.section_heading: PROHIBITION ON ACQUISITION OF SENSITIVE MATERIALS FROM NON-ALLIED FOREIGN NATIONS.

manifests.description: Establishes a prohibition on Department of Defense procurement of certain sensitive materials—including samarium-cobalt magnets, neodymium-iron-boron magnets, tungsten metal powder, and tungsten heavy alloys—if those materials are melted or produced in North Korea, China, Russia, or Iran. The prohibition also extends to the procurement of end items containing such materials and to certain sales of these materials from the National Defense Stockpile to these countries or their agents.
manifests.subject: Prohibition on DoD acquisition and sale of specific magnets and tungsten from designated non-allied countries
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and contractors and subcontractors at all tiers
manifests.conditions: Except where otherwise exempted under stated exceptions

manifests.description: Specifies that the above prohibition applies to all Department of Defense prime contracts and to all subcontract tiers, ensuring broad supply chain coverage.
manifests.subject: Applicability to contracts and subcontracts
manifests.applies_to: All DoD prime contracts and subcontracts at any tier
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 225.7018, flowdown requirements

manifests.description: Establishes specific exceptions to the prohibition: when compliant materials cannot be obtained in sufficient quality or quantity at a reasonable price; for procurement or sales for use outside the United States; for certain commercially available off-the-shelf (COTS) items and electronic devices (unless DoD determines them as critical); and for certain neodymium-iron-boron magnets from recycled material processed in the US.
manifests.subject: Statutory exceptions to prohibition
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and contractors (see main prohibition for scope)
manifests.conditions: Secretary of Defense determination, item type, geographic use, or recycled status
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 225.7018-3 and -4 (exceptions and waivers)
open_questions: How will DoD operationalize the 'reasonable price' and quality/quantity determination for exemptions — will guidance to contracting officers be required?

manifests.description: Prohibits the sale of covered materials from the National Defense Stockpile to covered nations or to brokers/agents acting for them, unless the Stockpile Manager determines that such a sale is in the national interest of the United States.
manifests.subject: Restrictions on National Defense Stockpile sales
manifests.applies_to: National Defense Stockpile Manager and DoD
manifests.conditions: Unless Stockpile Manager issues a national interest determination
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: May require a DFARS cross-reference to Defense National Stockpile Center procedures or development of a formal determination process
open_questions: What mechanisms will ensure proper documentation and rationale for 'national interest' sales?

manifests.description: Provides statutory definitions for 'covered material', 'covered nation,' and 'end item' for this prohibition to ensure clarity and alignment with existing acquisition rules and DFARS usage.
manifests.subject: Definitions for sensitive material procurement prohibition
manifests.applies_to: All implementations and interpretations of §2533c
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure definitions harmonize with DFARS 225.7018-1 and corresponding DFARS clauses

manifests.description: Updates the table of contents for 10 U.S.C. Chapter 148, Subchapter V, to include the new section 2533c, making the prohibition discoverable in statutory references.
manifests.subject: Clerical amendment: update table of contents
manifests.applies_to: Statutory chapter table of contents

sections.section_heading: VALIDATION OF PROPRIETARY AND TECHNICAL DATA.

manifests.description: Removes the special validation rule for technical data restrictions related to major weapon systems, subsystems, or components developed exclusively at private expense. Now, all challenges to use or release restrictions for technical data presumed to be developed exclusively at private expense (including for commercial items) are handled through a single standardized process.
manifests.subject: Standardization of validation process for technical data restriction challenges
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers and contractors/subcontractors asserting use or release restrictions on technical data
manifests.conditions: When the technical data is delivered under any Department of Defense contract, including those related to major weapon systems and commercial items
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 227.7103-13 and related coverage of validation of restrictive markings on technical data (for both commercial and noncommercial items); remove any special validation rules for major weapon systems.
open_questions: Should any existing DFARS sub-regulatory guidance or clauses specific to technical data for major weapon systems be deleted or otherwise updated as a result of this change?

sections.section_heading: PROMPT PAYMENTS OF SMALL BUSINESS CONTRACTORS.

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to set an accelerated payment date with a goal of 15 days after receipt of a proper invoice for prime contractors that are small business concerns, where no specific payment date is established by contract. This is intended to help small business primes with cash flow and reduce financial stress, by ensuring payments are made as quickly as possible.
manifests.subject: Accelerated payments to small business prime contractors
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; prime contractors that are small business concerns
manifests.conditions: Only when a specific payment date is not established by contract; applies to proper invoices for the amount due
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 232.906, 232.903, and related payment timing policy to require the 15-day payment goal for small business primes.
open_questions: What constitutes 'to the fullest extent permitted by law' for the purposes of implementation?
Should 'goal' be interpreted as imposing a hard requirement if practicable, or as an aspirational target?
How will ‘proper invoice’ be defined for this specific provision (should use standard FAR/DFARS definitions)?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to set an accelerated payment date with a goal of 15 days after receipt of a proper invoice for prime contractors (of any size) that subcontract with small business concerns, where (1) no specific payment date is set in the contract, and (2) the prime agrees to pay its small business subcontractor(s) on the same accelerated basis, to the maximum extent practicable, without additional consideration or fees charged to the small business. This ensures early payment flows to small business subs as well.
manifests.subject: Accelerated payments to primes for small business subcontracting
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; prime contractors who subcontract with small business concerns
manifests.conditions: No specific payment date in contract; prime agrees to pay small business sub on the same accelerated basis with no additional consideration or fees
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Expand DFARS 232.906 and 232.903 to require accelerated payment where the prime certifies/agrees to pay its small business sub on the same basis, without additional charges.
open_questions: What form should the prime contractor’s agreement to pay the sub on the accelerated basis take (representation, certification, contract clause)?
What are the enforcement mechanisms or remedies if primes do not pay subs as agreed?
Is there a required process to document the small business status of the subcontractor?

manifests.description: Makes technical and conforming modifications to reorganize 10 U.S.C. 2307(a) into subparagraphs and insert new paragraph identifiers to accommodate the substantive additions for accelerated payment. This clarifies organization but does not change contract policy.
manifests.subject: Conforming/structural amendments to enable payment provisions
manifests.applies_to: Statutory and regulatory text referencing 10 U.S.C. 2307(a)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: No substantive DFARS impact, but cross-references may need to be updated if they point to specific paragraphs.

sections.section_heading: LIMITATION ON APPLICABILITY TO DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE COMMERCIAL CONTRACTS OF CERTAIN PROVISIONS OF LAW.

manifests.description: Changes the cutoff date for the automatic exemption of defense-unique statutory provisions or contract clause requirements from DoD commercial item procurements. Previously, only provisions enacted after January 1, 2015, were automatically inapplicable unless specifically applied by DOD determination; now, the exemption covers all provisions enacted after October 13, 1994. This broadens the range of laws inapplicable to DoD contracts for commercial products and services.
manifests.subject: Expansion of automatic exemption date for commercial contracts
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts and subcontracts for commercial items/products/services
manifests.conditions: Applies to defense-unique statutory provisions enacted after October 13, 1994, unless specifically determined otherwise by the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 212.503; ensure the DFARS list of inapplicable defense-unique laws reflects the October 13, 1994 date.
open_questions: Are there specific statutes enacted between 1994 and 2015 currently being enforced that now require review for continued applicability or waiver process documentation?

manifests.description: Updates terminology in the Berry Amendment (section 2533a) to change references from 'commercial items' to 'commercial products' both in the subsection heading and within the text. This aligns coverage with the modern DoD definitions and could narrow or clarify which types of procurements are subject to Berry Amendment domestic source requirements.
manifests.subject: Terminology update: commercial items to commercial products (Berry Amendment)
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts and subcontracts for commercial products
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 225.7002; update all references for domestic source restrictions to match statutory usage of 'commercial products'.
open_questions: Does this change narrow the coverage to completely exclude commercial services, or are there edge cases where coverage persists?

manifests.description: Updates terminology in the domestic specialty metals restrictions (section 2533b) to change references from 'commercial items' to 'commercial products' in the heading and throughout subsection (h). This clarifies that the statutory requirements apply specifically to commercial products as now defined by DoD, rather than the broader 'commercial items' category.
manifests.subject: Terminology update: commercial items to commercial products (Specialty Metals)
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts and subcontracts for commercial products
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 225.7003; update provisions and clause flowdown instructions to use 'commercial products' in alignment with the statute.
open_questions: Are there other DFARS sections or clauses still using 'commercial items' that should be reviewed for consistency?
Are there contracts for commercial services or mixed items that require review due to this terminology change?

sections.section_heading: REVISION OF DEFINITION OF COMMERCIAL ITEM FOR PURPOSES OF FEDERAL ACQUISITION STATUTES.

manifests.description: Replace the statutory definition of 'commercial item' in 41 U.S.C. 103 with two new definitions — 'commercial product' and 'commercial service'. This change separates acquisition policies for products and services, aiming for greater clarity and improved alignment to modern business practices.
manifests.subject: Definition split: 'commercial item' to 'commercial product' and 'commercial service'
manifests.applies_to: All federal executive agencies subject to Title 41 procurement statutes
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 202, 212; update all references to 'commercial item', add/define 'commercial product' and 'commercial service'
open_questions: For items or solutions that have attributes of both product and service, how are ambiguous cases to be classified post-amendment?

manifests.description: Make conforming amendments to numerous sections in Title 41 to replace 'commercial item(s)' with 'commercial product(s)' and/or 'commercial service(s)' as appropriate. Ensures consistency in terminology throughout procurement statutes.
manifests.subject: Conforming amendments throughout Title 41
manifests.applies_to: Federal executive agencies, procurement officials
manifests.conditions: As per statutory cross-references listed in section text
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Review all DFARS parts referencing commercial item(s) for language and definition consistency, especially 202, 212, and parts implementing Title 41 procedures.
open_questions: Are additional notices or guidance needed for contracting personnel about interpreting old references crossing the effective date?

manifests.description: Update all Title 10 statutes relating to defense procurement by replacing or supplementing references to 'commercial item(s)' with 'commercial product(s)' and/or 'commercial service(s)' as now defined in Title 41. This ensures statutory consistency for Department of Defense acquisition.
manifests.subject: Title 10 conforming amendments: definitions, threshold, preference, and procedures
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and related agencies
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update all corresponding DFARS parts/clauses referencing 10 U.S.C. provisions for definitions and usage of 'commercial product/service'. Key places include DFARS 212, 227, 237, 252.
open_questions: In procurement practices, are all 'commercial item' legacy procedures to simply bifurcate, or will certain policy preferences (eg, simplicity for commercial items) be reallocated to only products, only services, or both?

manifests.description: Make parallel conforming amendments in previously-enacted NDAAs, and a broad range of federal statutes (including Titles 6, 20, 31, 33, 40, 42, 49, 51 U.S.C.) to align all acquisition policy, definitions, and procedures to use the new 'commercial product' and 'commercial service' terminology. This ensures unified, government-wide application.
manifests.subject: Cross-statute conforming changes, including prior NDAAs
manifests.applies_to: Executive branch subject to affected statutes/regulations
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS and DoD implementation guidance to follow terminology and cross-references for all statutory requirements that flow into procurement regulations, especially regarding exceptions, preferences, and reporting.
open_questions: For non-DoD or governmentwide statutes, which agency will issue operational guidance/revisions to FAR/DFARS?

manifests.description: Require the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment to develop and submit by April 1, 2019, an implementation plan for these statutory changes—including schedule, further statutory or regulatory changes, and analysis of similarities/differences in acquisition policy between commercial products and services.
manifests.subject: Implementation plan for transition to separate product/service terms
manifests.applies_to: Office of the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment
manifests.conditions: By April 1, 2019
manifests.effective_date: 2019-04-01 (due date for submission); ongoing for reviews
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS should be updated to reflect (and follow, where appropriate) the recommendations and planned sequencing from the official implementation plan.
open_questions: Will the implementation plan’s recommendations mandate further staged changes in DFARS publication and procurement policies after January 1, 2020?

manifests.description: Establish a consistent effective date of January 1, 2020 for all the above amendments, and specify a legal savings provision to ensure that regulations and procedures referencing now-replaced 'commercial item' coverage are grandfathered or continued under the updated 'commercial product' and 'commercial service' framework.
manifests.subject: Effective date and legal transition rules
manifests.applies_to: Regulations, contracts, and acquisition procedures subject to these statutes
manifests.conditions: Transition as described in Sec. 836(h)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS/federal policy notice should clearly specify the conversion rules for legacy contracts and regulations.
open_questions: Should contracting officers treat references to 'commercial item' in legacy contracts/programs as intending the new bifurcation, or leave alone? What if cross-references are material to contract scope or pricing?

sections.section_heading: INCREASE IN MICRO-PURCHASE THRESHOLD APPLICABLE TO DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE.

manifests.description: Raises the micro-purchase threshold for the Department of Defense from $5,000 to $10,000. This streamlines the process for small-value acquisitions by allowing DoD to use simplified competitive procedures for purchases up to $10,000, reducing administrative burden.
manifests.subject: Increase DoD micro-purchase threshold
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers and acquisition personnel
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update all references to the DoD micro-purchase threshold (e.g., DFARS 213.2, 213.306, PGI 213.201) to $10,000.

manifests.description: Removes the reference to sections 2338 and 2339 of title 10 from the general definition of micro-purchase threshold in federal procurement law to reflect that DoD's threshold is now directly specified as $10,000.
manifests.subject: Conforming amendment to definition of micro-purchase threshold
manifests.applies_to: All federal agencies interpreting micro-purchase thresholds, especially DoD
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Review DFARS references to 41 U.S.C. 1902 for appropriateness in light of the unified threshold for DoD.

manifests.description: Repeals 10 U.S.C. 2339, which had authorized a special micro-purchase threshold for basic research programs and activities at DoD science and technology reinvention laboratories. All such research activities are now subject to the standard $10,000 DoD threshold.
manifests.subject: Repeal of special basic research micro-purchase authority
manifests.applies_to: DoD science and technology reinvention laboratories, contracting officers, and acquisition personnel
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Delete DFARS provisions that reference a separate threshold for DoD S&T reinvention labs.

manifests.description: Removes the entry for section 2339 from the chapter table of sections in Title 10. This is a conforming amendment reflecting the repeal of section 2339.
manifests.subject: Cleanup of statutory table of sections
manifests.applies_to: Statutory index and legal code
amends: 10 U.S.C. Chapter 137 Table of Sections (2301 prec.)

sections.section_heading: DATA COLLECTION AND INVENTORY FOR SERVICES CONTRACTS.

manifests.description: Expands the Department of Defense's annual service contract inventory and data collection to include not only staff augmentation contracts, but also contracts closely associated with inherently governmental functions. This ensures that DoD tracks and reports on services contracts that may impact core government responsibilities.
manifests.subject: Inclusion of contracts closely associated with inherently governmental functions in annual inventory
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense (services contract inventories and reports to Congress)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS Subpart 207.5, particularly 207.503, may need clarification to reflect this expanded inventory requirement.
open_questions: Are new data collection processes or definitions needed, or is this primarily a clarification and expansion of existing practice?

manifests.description: Updates the title of the DoD official responsible for guidance on service contract inventories and data collection. Replaces references to the 'Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics' with the 'Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment' to match DoD organizational changes.
manifests.subject: Update of responsible DoD official's title for inventory guidance
manifests.applies_to: Internal DoD guidance and reporting chain for services acquisition inventories
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update all DFARS/PGI references (including in implementing procedures under Subpart 207.5) to reflect the new title 'Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment.'
open_questions: Are there any related DFARS or internal DoD documents still using the superseded title that must also be updated?

sections.section_heading: MODIFICATION OF LIMITATIONS ON SINGLE SOURCE TASK OR DELIVERY ORDER CONTRACTS.

manifests.description: Tightens the standard for awarding a single-source task or delivery order contract exceeding $100 million by requiring the head of an agency to determine in writing that only a single source can efficiently (rather than just reasonably) perform the work. This aims to limit such awards to cases where only one contractor can do the work in an efficient manner, not merely in a reasonable fashion.
manifests.subject: Threshold for single-source task or delivery order contracts
manifests.applies_to: Heads of contracting agencies awarding single-source task or delivery order contracts exceeding $100 million
manifests.conditions: Applies when awarding a single-source task or delivery order contract exceeding $100 million, unless an exception for noncompetitive procedures applies
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Amend DFARS Subpart 216.5 guidance, associated PGI, and templates for single-award determination to require justification that only a single source can efficiently perform the work, not just reasonably. Ensure corresponding language in clauses, such as in approval documentation or checklists for 10 U.S.C. 2304a determinations.
open_questions: Is there existing DoD or OMB guidance clarifying the difference between 'efficiently' and 'reasonably' in this acquisition context?
Should additional training, definitions, or examples be provided to contracting officers to ensure consistent application of the new 'efficiently perform the work' standard?

sections.section_heading: REPEAL OF CERTAIN DEFENSE ACQUISITION LAWS.

manifests.description: Repeals 10 U.S.C. 167a, which delegated limited acquisition authority to the commander of unified combatant command for joint warfighting experimentation. Removes that special authority.
manifests.subject: Repeal of special acquisition authority for unified command
manifests.applies_to: DoD, unified combatant command leadership
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove references to 10 U.S.C. 167a authority throughout DFARS—especially delegation language.

manifests.description: Deletes the table of sections listing for section 167a in chapter 6 of title 10, as a conforming amendment.
manifests.subject: Technical update – table of sections
manifests.applies_to: Statute tables

manifests.description: Removes reference to 167a in section 905(a)(1) of the John Warner NDAA for FY2007 to reflect its repeal.
manifests.subject: Update to cross-reference for combatant command acquisition authority
manifests.applies_to: Under Secretary of Defense (Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics) oversight responsibilities

manifests.description: Repeals 10 U.S.C. 2323, ending the statutory program granting Department of Defense and other agencies authority for specific set-aside and preference contracting programs for small disadvantaged businesses, historically Black colleges and universities, and minority institutions. Such preferences revert to government-wide authority under the Small Business Act.
manifests.subject: Repeal DoD small disadvantaged business preference program
manifests.applies_to: All DoD contracting officers and affected contractors
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove any DFARS subparts implementing 10 U.S.C. 2323. Rely on 15 U.S.C. 637/644 Small Business Act programs.

manifests.description: Deletes the table of sections listing for 2323 in chapter 137 of title 10, as a technical conforming amendment.
manifests.subject: Technical update – table of sections
manifests.applies_to: Statute tables

manifests.description: Eliminates or adjusts references to 10 U.S.C. 2323 throughout various statutes, NDAA sections, and cross-references—removing obsolete program requirements, updating 'small business' program language, clarifying scope, or striking now-inapplicable instructions. This includes technical revisions and clarifications (e.g., 10 U.S.C. 2304, 2304e, 2323a; 15 U.S.C. 644; 41 U.S.C. 1902).
manifests.subject: Conforming repeal/amendments of 2323 references in related acquisition statutes
manifests.applies_to: All authorities that referenced the old 2323 SDB program
amends: Federal Acquisition Streamlining Act of 1994 §8304(1) (10 U.S.C. 2375 note)
Federal Acquisition Streamlining Act of 1994 §10004(a)(1) (41 U.S.C. 1122 note)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Strike or update all references to 2323 or related programmatic language in DFARS. Focus on ensuring only Small Business Act authorities are referenced.
open_questions: Are there remaining DFARS provisions with implied 2323 language or logic?
Are there regulations or forms that need updating alongside statutory text?

manifests.description: Repeals 10 U.S.C. 2332, which authorized DoD share-in-savings contracts for information technology projects.
manifests.subject: Repeal share-in-savings contract authority
manifests.applies_to: DoD contracting for IT
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove any language in DFARS authorizing or referencing 10 U.S.C. 2332 share-in-savings contracts.
open_questions: Do any standing contracts need grand-fathering based on this authority?

manifests.description: Deletes the table of sections listing for 2332 in chapter 137 of title 10, a technical conforming amendment.
manifests.subject: Technical update – table of sections
manifests.applies_to: Statute tables

manifests.description: Repeals numerous (60+) specific pilot programs, statutory notes, expired reporting requirements, and dormant authorities in prior NDAAs, appropriations acts, and other laws, thereby eliminating obsolete or expired requirements within the DoD acquisition legal framework.
manifests.subject: Sunset/repeal of legacy acquisition laws and pilot programs
manifests.applies_to: DoD acquisition community, affected offices, compliance function
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Sweep DFARS and related handbooks for references to repealed pilot or expired programs and delete or archive as appropriate.
open_questions: Any cross-reference mapping required for components with cross-cutting or legacy program responsibilities?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 491 note.>> SECURITY OF NUCLEAR COMMAND, CONTROL, AND COMMUNICATIONS SYSTEM FROM COMMERCIAL DEPENDENCIES.

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to certify to the congressional defense committees, within 180 days of enactment, whether covered telecommunications equipment or services (from Huawei, ZTE, or other Chinese or Russian government-linked entities) are used as a substantial or essential component or as critical technology in any system used for nuclear deterrence or homeland defense missions, including nuclear command, control, communications, tactical warning and attack assessment, or continuity of government and ballistic missile defense.
manifests.subject: Certification of use of covered telecommunications equipment in critical mission systems
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; Department of Defense
manifests.conditions: Certification due within 180 days after enactment of the Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Implement via a reporting/certification rule, e.g. in DFARS Part 204 or PGI 204, requiring a formal certification to the congressional defense committees on the use of covered equipment/services in specified systems.
open_questions: Is ongoing or recurring certification required after the initial report?
What format/process should DoD use for certification reports?

manifests.description: Prohibits, effective one year after enactment, the Department of Defense from procuring, obtaining, or extending/renewing contracts for any equipment, system, or service to carry out nuclear deterrence or homeland defense missions if such items use covered telecommunications equipment or services as a substantial or essential component or as critical technology.
manifests.subject: Prohibition on procurement of systems using covered telecommunications equipment
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense (procurement officials, contractors, related supply chains)
manifests.conditions: Applies beginning one year after enactment, unless a waiver is granted under the statute
manifests.effective_date: One year after enactment of this Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 204.2102 and 252.204-7018 already implement this prohibition; ensure all relevant contracts/solicitations reference the prohibition and adhere to the definitions in this statute.
open_questions: Are any grandfathered contracts or existing obligations exempt?
How are edge cases (e.g., dual-use items) adjudicated?

manifests.description: Authorizes the Secretary of Defense to waive the prohibition for a single one-year period, on a case-by-case basis, if the Secretary determines the waiver is in the national security interests of the United States, and certifies to congressional defense committees that sufficient mitigations are in place and that steps are being taken to remove covered equipment or services from the relevant systems.
manifests.subject: Waiver process for covered equipment prohibition
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.conditions: Waiver may be issued for one year, on a case-by-case basis, with required determinations and certification to congressional committees
manifests.effective_date: Beginning one year after enactment (when prohibition takes effect)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 204.2104 implements the waiver process; ensure policy and contract language require findings and certifications as specified.
open_questions: Is there a mandatory form or reporting process for such certifications?
Can waivers be renewed under new facts or continued justification?

manifests.description: Restricts delegation of waiver authority: Secretary of Defense may only delegate this power to the Deputy Secretary of Defense or the co-chairs of the Council on Oversight of the National Leadership Command, Control, and Communications System (as established in 10 U.S.C. 171a), and to no other officials.
manifests.subject: Restriction on delegation of waiver authority
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; DoD leadership/officials
manifests.conditions: Waivers under this section may only be approved by Deputy Secretary or named Council co-chairs
manifests.effective_date: One year after enactment of this Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS waiver/approval processes (e.g. 204.2104, PGI 204) should spell out who may issue waivers for this prohibition, aligning with this statutory restriction.
open_questions: How should DFARS language be updated if Council co-chairs or their titles change under law?
Is there a notification requirement if delegation is exercised?

manifests.description: Defines critical statutory terms for implementation, including: 'congressional defense committees' (as in 10 U.S.C. 101(a)(16)); 'covered foreign country' (China, Russia); and 'covered telecommunications equipment or services' (telecom equipment/services from Huawei, ZTE or their affiliates; or any entity linked to those governments, or providing services using such equipment).
manifests.subject: Definitions of key statutory terms
manifests.applies_to: DoD procurement, contracting, and compliance officials
manifests.effective_date: Immediate, at enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: These definitions align with those used in DFARS 204.2101 and 252.204-7018; maintain consistency and incorporate any updates as supply chain threats evolve.
open_questions: What evidentiary standard or process establishes DoD's 'reasonable belief' about an entity's connection to a covered foreign country?
Is there a formal process for updating this defined list in the FAR/DFARS as new threats emerge?

sections.section_heading: FOREIGN COMMERCIAL SATELLITE SERVICES: CYBERSECURITY THREATS AND LAUNCHES.

manifests.description: Adds an explicit prohibition on DoD contracts for satellite services with a foreign entity if entering into the contract would create an unacceptable cybersecurity risk for the Department of Defense, in addition to the existing prohibitions related to covered foreign country government involvement and service provision.
manifests.subject: Addition of cybersecurity risk prohibition for satellite service contracts
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts for satellite services with foreign entities
manifests.conditions: Applies when the Secretary reasonably believes the contract would create an unacceptable cybersecurity risk
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment of this Act (December 12, 2017)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 225.772-2(a)(1)(iii) and 252.225-7051; ensure cybersecurity risk test matches statutory and regulatory language.

manifests.description: Prohibits DoD from entering into contracts for satellite services if the Department reasonably believes the satellite will be designed, manufactured, or launched in a covered foreign country, or by an entity associated with such a country, extending beyond the previous focus on satellite service providers to include hardware origin and launch concerns.
manifests.subject: Prohibition of satellite service contracts based on foreign design, manufacture, or launch
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts for satellite services
manifests.conditions: Secretary reasonably believes satellite services use satellites designed, manufactured, or (with exceptions) launched in a covered foreign country or by associated entities, except as provided by exceptions or waivers
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment of this Act (December 12, 2017)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 225.772-2(b); DFARS 252.225-7051; contracting officer procedures and certifications for launch and hardware sources.
open_questions: Are there process checks in place to verify the design/manufacture/launch origin for each contract and ensure compliance?

manifests.description: Establishes specific exceptions to the new launch/manufacture prohibition, allowing: (A) launches occurring prior to December 31, 2022; and (B) contracts for launch services fully paid for or subject to legally binding commitments made within 180 days after enactment of this subsection.
manifests.subject: Exceptions to prohibition for certain launches and pre-existing launch service contracts
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts for satellite services subject to launch/manufacture prohibition
manifests.conditions: Launch before December 31, 2022; or contract/commitment made and paid for within 180 days after enactment of this section
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment of this Act (December 12, 2017)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 225.772-2(b)(2); include criteria for applying and documenting exceptions.
open_questions: Is there guidance for how contractors or contracting officers certify and track the timing and payment/commitment status to ensure valid exceptions are applied?

manifests.description: Updates and clarifies the statutory definition of 'covered foreign country' to include any country listed in NDAA FY2013 section 1261(c)(2) (China, North Korea, or any state sponsor of terrorism), and adds the Russian Federation by name.
manifests.subject: Update definition of 'covered foreign country'
manifests.applies_to: All of section 2279 and related contract actions
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment of this Act (December 12, 2017)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update definition in DFARS 225.772-1 and any cross-referenced provisions or clauses.

manifests.description: Defines 'cybersecurity risk' for satellite contracts as threats to and vulnerabilities of information or information systems and related consequences from unauthorized activity, including terrorism.
manifests.subject: Definition of 'cybersecurity risk' for satellite service contract actions
manifests.applies_to: All of section 2279 and related contract actions
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment of this Act (December 12, 2017)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure this precise definition is reflected in DFARS 225.772-1 and related contract clauses.

manifests.description: Updates section heading, cross-references, and table of sections in Title 10 to reflect changes to structure, new prohibitions, and expanded content, ensuring statutory and regulatory cross-references align.
manifests.subject: Conforming and clerical amendments to headings, cross-references, and table of sections
manifests.applies_to: 10 U.S.C. 2279 and table of sections for chapter 135
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment of this Act (December 12, 2017)
amends: Table of sections for chapter 135 of title 10
