manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Check clauses, DFARS prescriptions, and references to ensure all cite the correct subsections and heading.

manifests.description: Clarifies that, except as otherwise specifically provided, all amendments made by this section apply to contracts for satellite services awarded by DoD on or after the date of enactment of this Act.
manifests.subject: Application and effective date of amendments
manifests.applies_to: Contracts for satellite services awarded by DoD
manifests.conditions: Unless otherwise specifically provided
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment of this Act (December 12, 2017)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 225.772-2 and related implementation sections should clarify this effective date for new/renewed contracts.
open_questions: If contract award cycles straddle the enactment date, should the obligation be tied to signature, funding, or other point?

sections.section_heading: ELIMINATION OF REPORTING REQUIREMENTS TERMINATED AFTER NOVEMBER 25, 2017, PURSUANT TO SECTION 1080 OF THE NATIONAL DEFENSE AUTHORIZATION ACT FOR FISCAL YEAR 2016.

manifests.description: Eliminates the Reserve Forces Policy Board's required report from the Secretary of Defense's annual report to Congress.
manifests.subject: Reserve Forces Policy Board report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Strikes the requirement for the Total Force Management report from the annual reports by the Secretary of Defense.
manifests.subject: Total force management report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Removes the sunset date restriction ('during fiscal years 2013 through 2017') from the reporting requirement related to military leadership diversity, making the reporting ongoing.
manifests.subject: Diversity in military leadership report
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the biennial report on Defense Industrial Security expenditures and activities.
manifests.subject: Defense industrial security report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Removes the report to Congress on military musical units' acceptance of private donations.
manifests.subject: Military musical units gift report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary concerned
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the requirement for an annual report on the Force Health Protection Quality Assurance Program.
manifests.subject: Health protection quality report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Removes the requirement for a master plan and related reports for reductions in civilian positions and strikes outdated cross-references.
manifests.subject: Master plans for reductions in civilian positions
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the required annual report on the operation of the Acquisition Workforce Development Fund.
manifests.subject: Acquisition workforce development fund report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the reporting requirement for information on special requirements for civilian employees in the acquisition field.
manifests.subject: Acquisition corps report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the annual family readiness report from the requirements of Department of Defense military family programs.
manifests.subject: Military family readiness report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Repeals the requirement for the Professional Military Education report under 10 U.S.C. 2157.
manifests.subject: Professional military education report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the annual report regarding Department of Defense conferences.
manifests.subject: DoD conferences fee-collection report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the annual report on United States contributions to NATO common-funded budgets.
manifests.subject: NATO contributions report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the requirement for an annual report on foreign counter-space programs.
manifests.subject: Foreign counter-space programs report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Alters the content and frequency of the multiyear contract reporting requirement, limiting it to only those reports specified in statute.
manifests.subject: Use of multiyear contracts report
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense acquisition officials
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 217.1

manifests.description: Eliminates the annual report on burden sharing contributions by designated countries and regional organizations.
manifests.subject: Burden sharing contributions report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the annual report to Congress on waivers for contracts involving entities that comply with the secondary Arab boycott of Israel.
manifests.subject: Contract prohibition waiver report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Redefines the meaning of 'Strategic Sourcing Plan of Action' and eliminates the annual submission requirement, to require only internal tracking.
manifests.subject: Strategic sourcing plan of action definition and notification
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Moves the statement of purpose for Department of Defense industrial base policy guidance to its own subsection and eliminates the related annual reporting requirement.
manifests.subject: Technology and industrial base policy guidance report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the annual report on foreign-controlled contractors.
manifests.subject: Foreign-controlled contractors report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the requirement to report on Department of Defense support to certain sporting events.
manifests.subject: Support for sporting events report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the annual report to Congress on operation, maintenance, and repair costs of general and flag officer quarters.
manifests.subject: General and flag officer quarters report
manifests.applies_to: DoD housing officials
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the requirement for an annual report describing DoD military installation vulnerability assessments.
manifests.subject: Military installations vulnerability assessment reports
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the requirement to annually report on military construction projects under the industrial facility investment program.
manifests.subject: Industrial facility investment program construction report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the requirement for a statement of amounts available for water conservation at military installations in DoD budget submissions.
manifests.subject: Statement of amounts available for water conservation
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the reporting requirement for pilot projects for acquisition or construction of military unaccompanied housing.
manifests.subject: Acquisition or construction of military unaccompanied housing pilot projects report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of the Navy/Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the requirement to include in DoD budget materials a statement about funds available for obligation for energy cost savings.
manifests.subject: Statement of amounts available from energy cost savings
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Repeals the Army training report requirement.
manifests.subject: Army training report
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of the Army
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

manifests.description: Eliminates the 'State of the Army Reserve,' 'State of the Marine Corps Reserve,' and 'State of the Air Force Reserve' annual reports and associated requirements.
manifests.subject: Reserve component state reports
manifests.applies_to: Chiefs of Army Reserve, Marine Forces Reserve, Air Force Reserve
manifests.effective_date: The later of date of enactment or November 25, 2017

sections.section_heading: PROCUREMENT OF AVIATION CRITICAL SAFETY ITEMS.

manifests.description: Expands the prohibition on using reverse auctions or lowest price technically acceptable (LPTA) contracting methods for Department of Defense procurement to also cover aviation critical safety items, not just personal protective equipment. This ensures that cost-minimization approaches are avoided where failures could cause catastrophic aviation consequences.
manifests.subject: Prohibition of LPTA and reverse auctions for aviation critical safety items
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officials procuring aviation critical safety items
manifests.conditions: Procurement of any item defined as an aviation critical safety item in 10 U.S.C. 2319(g)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Amend DFARS language for LPTA/reverse auction prohibitions (currently for personal protective equipment) to also include aviation critical safety items, e.g., in 246.370, 209.270-5, and associated clauses/provisions.
open_questions: Are there other 'critical safety item' categories in DFARS that should be reviewed for similar coverage?
Are additional conforming amendments to DFARS definitions or guidance required for consistency?

manifests.description: Extends the required preference for 'best value' contracting methods to cover not only personal protective equipment, but also aviation critical safety items, and clarifies the terminology to cover both equipment and items. This further ensures that safety and technical merit are prioritized over lowest cost in these critical procurements.
manifests.subject: Best value preference for aviation critical safety items
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officials procuring aviation critical safety items
manifests.conditions: Procurement of any item defined as an aviation critical safety item in 10 U.S.C. 2319(g)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Modify DFARS provisions relating to preference for best value acquisition methods in procurement of personal protective equipment to also apply to aviation critical safety items and clarify 'equipment or item'.
open_questions: Does 'item' include non-physical products such as aviation-related software?
Do any additional DFARS sections defining personal protective equipment or critical safety items need update for terminology consistency?

sections.section_heading: NONCOMMERCIAL COMPUTER SOFTWARE ACQUISITION CONSIDERATIONS.

manifests.description: Requires the Department of Defense, during any negotiation for the acquisition of noncommercial computer software, to consider acquiring all software and related materials necessary to reproduce, build, recompile, test, and deploy the software at an appropriate time during the software's life cycle. This ensures the DoD can fully support the software’s use and sustainment.
manifests.subject: Consideration of comprehensive software needs during acquisition
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting and acquisition personnel negotiating for noncommercial computer software
manifests.conditions: Applies at the point of negotiation for noncommercial computer software acquisitions; triggered by any such negotiation
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS subpart 227.7203 (especially 227.7203-2) should be updated to embed mandatory consideration of acquiring all materials needed to build, test, and deploy noncommercial software.
open_questions: How should 'to the maximum extent practicable' be defined and documented in acquisition files?
What standards, if any, should guide the identification of 'all software and related materials necessary' in practice?

manifests.description: Establishes requirements for delivery of noncommercial computer software and related materials to ensure the government receives them in a usable, digital format, without reliance on excluded external software or data unless specifically included, and with sufficient documentation for maintenance and understanding if all dependent components are not deliverable.
manifests.subject: Delivery requirements for noncommercial computer software and materials
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and contractors delivering noncommercial computer software or related materials that must be provided as a result of an acquisition
manifests.conditions: Triggered by the delivery of noncommercial computer software or related materials required as a result of acquisition consideration
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 227.7203-2 and related DFARS 227.7200 series provisions should be updated to specify these deliverable requirements for noncommercial computer software.
open_questions: What exactly constitutes a 'usable, digital format' for different types of software deliverables?
What is considered 'sufficient documentation' to support maintenance and understanding, and should standards be referenced or defined?

manifests.description: Directs the Secretary of Defense to issue, within 180 days after enactment, updated guidance implementing the new statutory requirements for the consideration and delivery of noncommercial computer software and related materials.
manifests.subject: Issuance of implementation guidance
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and subordinate acquisition organizations
manifests.conditions: Within 180 days after December 12, 2017 (date of enactment)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Reference or update may be needed in DFARS Procedures, Guidance, and Information (PGI) or internal DoD acquisition guidance/documentation.
open_questions: Will the required guidance be publicly available and will it include compliance mechanisms?
How will any new guidance interact with or amend existing DFARS requirements and DoD internal procedures?

sections.section_heading: REVIEW OF REGULATIONS ON COMMERCIAL ITEMS.

manifests.description: The Secretary of Defense must review all past Federal Acquisition Regulatory Council determinations made under 41 U.S.C. 1906(b)(2), 1906(c)(3), or 1907(a)(2) that decided not to exempt Department of Defense contracts and subcontracts for commercial items and commercially available off-the-shelf (COTS) items from laws they would otherwise be exempt from. The goal is to determine if those non-exemptions remain appropriate.
manifests.subject: Review of non-exempt contracts and subcontracts for commercial and COTS items
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; Department of Defense contracts and subcontracts involving commercial items and COTS items
manifests.conditions: To be completed within one year after enactment of this Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS assignment for comprehensive review of relevant FAR Council determinations affecting DoD contracts and subcontracts for commercial and COTS items.
open_questions: Is there a complete, accessible record of all previous FAR Council determinations relevant to the scope?

manifests.description: The Secretary of Defense must propose changes to DFARS to newly exempt commercial item and COTS contracts and subcontracts from the laws considered in the above review, unless the Secretary documents a specific reason to continue each non-exemption. This establishes a presumption in favor of exemptions unless justified otherwise.
manifests.subject: DFARS exemption proposals for commercial and COTS contracts and subcontracts
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; DFARS treatment of commercial and COTS contracts and subcontracts
manifests.conditions: Triggered after the review in Manifest 1; must be completed within one year after enactment
amends: DFARS (Part 212 and relevant provisions as affected)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS to add/expand exemptions for commercial and COTS contracts and subcontracts, unless a specific, documented rationale for non-exemption is presented.
open_questions: What qualifies as a 'specific reason' justifying continued non-exemption for each law/regulation?

manifests.description: The Secretary of Defense must review all DFARS regulations requiring specific contract clauses in contracts using commercial item acquisition procedures under FAR Part 12, except those required by law or Executive order, to determine whether those requirements are still necessary.
manifests.subject: DFARS review of clause requirements for commercial item contracts
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; DFARS regulations for commercial item contracts under FAR Part 12
manifests.conditions: To be completed within one year after enactment; applies to clauses not required by law or Executive order
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Initiate a review process of all non-mandatory (non-statutory and non-E.O.) DFARS contract clauses applicable to commercial item contracts (FAR 12 procedures).
open_questions: Is there a central list of clauses clearly identified by their origin (law/Executive order/other) to facilitate this review?

manifests.description: After the review, the Secretary of Defense must propose DFARS revisions to eliminate any regulation requiring specific contract clauses in commercial item contracts that is not required by law or Executive order, unless the Secretary determines case-by-case that there is a specific reason not to eliminate the regulation.
manifests.subject: DFARS elimination proposals for non-mandatory clauses in commercial item contracts
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; DFARS regulations for commercial item contracts under FAR Part 12
manifests.conditions: Triggered after the review in Manifest 3; applies to non-statutory, non-E.O. derived clauses
amends: DFARS (Part 212 clause requirements for commercial item contracts)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Initiate DFARS rulemaking to eliminate all optional/non-mandated contract clauses for commercial item (FAR 12) contracts unless individually justified and documented.
open_questions: How should the DoD document or publish each decision to retain a clause for transparency and traceability?

manifests.description: The Secretary of Defense must review all DFARS regulations that require a prime contractor to include specific contract clauses in subcontracts for commercially available off-the-shelf (COTS) items, unless those clauses are required by law or Executive order.
manifests.subject: DFARS review of COTS clause flow-down requirements
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; DFARS regulations for COTS item subcontracts
manifests.conditions: To be completed within one year after enactment; applies to clauses not required by law or Executive order
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS review of clause flow-downs to COTS subcontracts; identify and inventory all requirements for non-statutory/non-E.O. clause inclusion.
open_questions: Are existing flow-down requirements for COTS subcontracts clearly tagged with their statutory/E.O. basis in existing DFARS text?

manifests.description: After the review, the Secretary of Defense must propose DFARS revisions to eliminate any regulation requiring clause flow-down to COTS subcontracts that is not required by law or Executive order, unless the Secretary determines on a case-by-case basis that there is a specific reason not to eliminate the regulation.
manifests.subject: DFARS elimination proposals for non-mandatory clause flow-downs in COTS subcontracts
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; DFARS regulations for COTS item subcontracts
manifests.conditions: Triggered after the review in Manifest 5; applies to flow-down requirements not required by law or Executive order
amends: DFARS (clause flow-down requirements for COTS subcontracts)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS rulemaking to remove flow-down clause requirements for COTS subcontracts unless individually justified and documented.
open_questions: Will each decision to retain a clause-flow-down be accompanied by published justification for public and industry review?

sections.section_heading: COMMERCIAL ITEM DETERMINATIONS.

manifests.description: Establishes that when an item was previously acquired under a contract using commercial item acquisition procedures (FAR Part 12), that contract serves as a standing determination that the item is a commercial item for all future Department of Defense procurements, unless a senior procurement executive provides written determination that it is no longer appropriate. This change is meant to reduce repeated commercial item determinations and ensure consistency.
manifests.subject: Presumption of commercial item status for previously acquired items
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense procurement of items
manifests.conditions: Applies unless a senior procurement executive of the relevant military department or DoD determines in writing that commercial procedures are no longer appropriate for the item.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 212.102 and PGI 212.102; clarify presumption for commerciality and process for written determination override.
open_questions: What process documentation or database should be used to record senior procurement executive written determinations that revoke commercial item status?

manifests.description: Prohibits the Department of Defense from using appropriated funds to buy under FAR Part 15 procedures any item that was previously acquired under a contract using commercial item acquisition procedures (FAR Part 12), unless (1) the head of contracting activity determines in writing that the prior use of commercial procedures was improper, or (2) a senior procurement executive determines in writing that it is no longer appropriate to use commercial procedures. The intent is to ensure commercial item purchasing is not reversed without proper justification.
manifests.subject: Limitation on use of FAR Part 15 for items previously bought as commercial items
manifests.applies_to: Procurements by the Department of Defense for items previously acquired as commercial items
manifests.conditions: Limitation may be waived only with a written determination by the head of contracting activity (under 10 U.S.C. 2306a(b)(4)(B)) or by the relevant senior procurement executive.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 212.102; prohibit use of FAR Part 15 procedures for items previously procured as commercial unless written exception by officials as specified.
open_questions: What oversight or appeal process is available if there are repeated reversions or disputes regarding these written determinations?
How should contracts be handled when items developed commercially are later modified enough to potentially lose commercial status?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2337a note.>> SHOULD-COST MANAGEMENT.

manifests.description: Directs the Department of Defense to update the DFARS within 180 days to provide detailed regulations for the use of the should-cost review process on major weapon system acquisitions, ensuring the process is transparent, objective, and supports efficiency in acquisition.
manifests.subject: Should-cost review process regulations for major weapon systems
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense acquisition officials conducting major weapon systems acquisitions
manifests.conditions: Within 180 days of enactment of the Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add to or create a new section in DFARS Subpart 234 specifically addressing should-cost reviews for major weapon system acquisitions.
open_questions: Should any minimum program size or dollar threshold apply, or is this for all major weapon systems by default?

manifests.description: Requires the regulations to include a description of the specific features of the should-cost review process for major weapon systems.
manifests.subject: Features of should-cost review process
manifests.applies_to: Major weapon system acquisition programs using should-cost reviews
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Include guidance outlining the key features/elements of should-cost reviews in the new DFARS section.
open_questions: Should the features be standardized across all programs, or can components customize them?

manifests.description: Mandates a process for communicating with the prime contractor about the specific elements of a proposed should-cost review, increasing transparency and contractor awareness.
manifests.subject: Prime contractor communication process
manifests.applies_to: Prime contractors and DoD officials involved in should-cost reviews
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Require notification and consultative communications with prime contractors in the should-cost review procedure.
open_questions: Is there a required form or minimal content for such communications? Should there be a minimum notice period?

manifests.description: Requires a method to ensure that should-cost savings opportunities are based on accurate, complete, and current information, and that they can be quantitatively tracked.
manifests.subject: Data quality and tracking for should-cost opportunities
manifests.applies_to: All should-cost reviews identifying savings opportunities
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Include process requirements on information validation and mechanisms for quantifying and tracking savings opportunities.
open_questions: Should this process reference existing DoD data verification standards or require new ones?

manifests.description: Requires a description in the regulations of the training, skills, and experience necessary for both DoD and contractor officials carrying out should-cost reviews.
manifests.subject: Training and qualifications for should-cost reviews
manifests.applies_to: DoD and contractor officials conducting should-cost reviews
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Specify required or recommended qualifications, skills, or training for personnel involved in should-cost reviews, potentially referencing DAWIA or equivalent certifications.
open_questions: Should DFARS list specific training programs or leave determination to individual agencies?

manifests.description: Requires a method for ensuring appropriate collaboration with the contractor throughout the review process to support transparency and mutual understanding.
manifests.subject: Ongoing contractor collaboration requirement
manifests.applies_to: DoD and contractors engaged in should-cost reviews on major weapon systems
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Mandate documentation and periodic demonstration of contractor engagement throughout the review cycle.
open_questions: How should DFARS define 'appropriate collaboration' and what recourse exists if collaboration breaks down?

manifests.description: Mandates that the regulations include requirements for thorough analysis during should-cost reviews while ensuring that review processes minimize impact on program schedules.
manifests.subject: Balancing thorough analysis and program schedule
manifests.applies_to: Should-cost review teams conducting reviews on major weapon system programs
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Specify timelines, scheduling requirements, and expected analysis standards for reviews to minimize adverse schedule impacts.
open_questions: Should there be a defined maximum allowable delay for programs undergoing should-cost reviews?

sections.section_heading: REQUIREMENT TO EMPHASIZE RELIABILITY AND MAINTAINABILITY IN WEAPON SYSTEM DESIGN.

manifests.description: Requires that the Secretary of Defense ensure the defense acquisition system emphasizes sustainment factors, especially those impacted mainly by system design, during weapon system development. The change aims to give greater priority to long-term reliability, maintainability, and support costs starting from initial design.
manifests.subject: Emphasis on sustainment factors in weapon system development
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and all weapon system acquisition programs
manifests.effective_date: December 12, 2017
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Create a new DFARS subpart implementing policy from 10 U.S.C. 2443 emphasizing early and ongoing sustainment in defense acquisition programs.
open_questions: Are there standardized sustainment design attributes or metrics the Department is expected to treat as benchmarks?
Should this be enforced through specific acquisition reviews or a new documentary requirement?

manifests.description: Requires that reliability and maintainability performance attributes be included in the sustainment key performance parameter (KPP) during capability requirements development for weapon systems, ensuring they are prioritized as essential goals from the start.
manifests.subject: Reliability and maintainability in performance attributes for sustainment
manifests.applies_to: Capability requirements for weapon systems (requirements writers, acquisition planners)
manifests.effective_date: December 12, 2017
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Mandate inclusion of reliability and maintainability as sustainment KPP performance attributes in requirements documentation for new acquisition programs.
open_questions: Which office or official verifies that KPPs meet this requirement?
Does this require updates to JCIDS or internal DOD guidance?

manifests.description: Requires solicitations and covered contracts for engineering/manufacturing development or production of weapon systems to include clear and measurable requirements for engineering and design specifications addressing reliability and maintainability.
manifests.subject: Reliability/maintainability requirements in solicitations and contracts
manifests.applies_to: Program managers and contracting officers for covered contracts
manifests.conditions: Only for contracts defined as 'covered' under the statute
manifests.effective_date: For covered contract solicitations issued on or after December 12, 2018 (one year after enactment)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Prescribe new contract clauses in DFARS for covered contracts to require reliability/maintainability engineering performance requirements.
open_questions: What constitutes 'measurable' and should this reference specific standards like MIL-STDs or other technical criteria?

manifests.description: If a program manager decides not to include reliability or maintainability requirements in a covered contract or solicitation, the decision must be justified in writing.
manifests.subject: Documentation of decisions to omit reliability/maintainability
manifests.applies_to: Program managers for covered weapon system contracts
manifests.conditions: Applies when reliability/maintainability requirements are omitted
manifests.effective_date: For covered contract solicitations issued on or after December 12, 2018 (one year after enactment)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add procedural requirement for file documentation and, potentially, a review or approval process for written justifications.
open_questions: Should these justifications be subject to higher-level review, and if so, by whom?
Where and how should they be retained for audit purposes?

manifests.description: Requires that sustainment, reliability, and maintainability receive substantial consideration in source selection for covered contracts; encourages use of objective criteria in proposal evaluation.
manifests.subject: Sustainment as source selection evaluation factor
manifests.applies_to: Source selection authorities for covered weapon system contracts
manifests.conditions: Applies to covered contracts as defined in the law
manifests.effective_date: For covered contract solicitations issued on or after December 12, 2018 (one year after enactment)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add/strengthen source selection requirements in DFARS to give greater weight to measurable sustainment, reliability, and maintainability factors for relevant acquisitions.
open_questions: How should 'ample emphasis' be operationalized and measured in the source selection process?
Does this require minimum weighting or thresholds?

manifests.description: Requires DoD to use best practices to respond to positive and negative contractor performance regarding sustainment requirements in covered contracts, and encourages the use of incentive fees and penalties, where appropriate, to drive achievement.
manifests.subject: Incentives and penalties for contractor sustainment performance
manifests.applies_to: All covered contracts for weapon systems
manifests.conditions: As appropriate and authorized (by the Secretary; see paragraph (2))
manifests.effective_date: For covered contract solicitations issued on or after December 12, 2018 (one year after enactment)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Incorporate language in DFARS supporting use of positive and negative financial incentives tied to sustainment performance on covered contracts.
open_questions: How are appropriate incentives or penalties determined—are ranges or limits to be specified?
Are there standard DoD models for sustainment incentives?

manifests.description: Authorizes the inclusion of incentive fee or penalty provisions in covered contracts for meeting or failing to meet reliability and maintainability design specifications, and requires the existence of such incentives/penalties to be disclosed in solicitations.
manifests.subject: Authority to include incentive/penalty fees in contracts, and notice thereof
manifests.applies_to: Contracting officers for covered weapon system contracts
manifests.conditions: As determined appropriate under the statute
manifests.effective_date: For covered contract solicitations issued on or after December 12, 2018 (one year after enactment)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Standardize contract clauses for incentive/penalty disclosure and administration in DFARS for qualifying contracts.
open_questions: Should a model incentive/penalty clause be developed for use across DoD?

manifests.description: Requires that program managers base assessments of contractor reliability and maintainability performance on program-collected data, with detailed metrics included in contracts, and, to the extent practicable, these data should be shared with appropriate contractor and government organizations.
manifests.subject: Data requirements for performance measurement
manifests.applies_to: Program managers for covered weapon systems
manifests.conditions: For newly solicited/awarded covered contracts after the effective date
manifests.effective_date: For covered contract solicitations issued on or after December 12, 2018 (one year after enactment)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Specify in DFARS and contract Data Rights/Delivery Requirements reliance on real-time and periodic data for sustainment evaluation and sharing procedures.
open_questions: How should proprietary data be addressed for sharing?
What standards are required for metric selection and reporting?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to notify the congressional defense committees when a covered contract is awarded that includes the authorized incentives or penalties for reliability/maintainability.
manifests.subject: Congressional notification of contracts with incentive/penalty provisions
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and relevant DoD contracting offices
manifests.conditions: Trigger: Contract award of a covered contract with incentive/penalty provisions
manifests.effective_date: For covered contract solicitations issued on or after December 12, 2018 (one year after enactment)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Implement notification mechanisms to ensure compliance with timely reporting to Congress for such contract actions.
open_questions: What information must be included in notifications and what is the timeline for submission?

manifests.description: Defines 'covered contract' for this section as a contract for engineering/manufacturing development or for production of a weapon system, including embedded software.
manifests.subject: Definition of 'covered contract'
manifests.applies_to: All applicability throughout 10 U.S.C. 2443
manifests.effective_date: December 12, 2017
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Incorporate this definition in relevant DFARS subparts and procedures for clarity.

manifests.description: Specifies that the new requirements regarding solicitations, contracts, and performance (10 U.S.C. 2443(c) and (d)) only apply to covered contract solicitations issued one year after the date of enactment (i.e., after December 12, 2018), providing a phase-in period.
manifests.subject: Delayed effective date for solicitation and performance requirements
manifests.applies_to: DoD acquisition and contracting workforce
manifests.conditions: Covers solicitations issued after December 12, 2018
manifests.effective_date: December 12, 2018
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Clarify applicability in implementing regulatory text.

manifests.description: Authorizes the Secretary of Defense, subject to appropriations, to fund engineering changes during development or production of a weapon system to improve reliability or maintainability and reduce projected operating/support costs.
manifests.subject: Authority to fund mid-program engineering changes for sustainment improvement
manifests.applies_to: DoD program offices and decision authorities managing ongoing acquisition programs
manifests.conditions: Subject to availability of appropriations
manifests.effective_date: December 12, 2017
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Reference this authority in context of change management and EMD/production funding guidance.
open_questions: Will a reporting requirement or guidance for evaluating such changes be issued?

sections.section_heading: PROHIBITION ON USE OF LOWEST PRICE TECHNICALLY ACCEPTABLE SOURCE SELECTION PROCESS FOR MAJOR DEFENSE ACQUISITION PROGRAMS.

manifests.description: Prohibits the Department of Defense from using a Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process for engineering and manufacturing development (EMD) contracts within major defense acquisition programs (MDAPs). The intention is to ensure that source selection for critical developmental efforts emphasizes technical merit and best value over lowest price alone, thereby reducing risks in complex defense acquisitions.
manifests.subject: Ban on LPTA for EMD contracts in MDAPs
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officials awarding EMD contracts for MDAPs
manifests.conditions: Applies only to contracts for engineering and manufacturing development of major defense acquisition programs as defined at 10 U.S.C. 2430; LPTA as defined in FAR Part 15
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Create a new DFARS section (e.g., 215.101 or update 215.300/235.006) explicitly stating LPTA cannot be used for EMD MDAP contracts, referencing 10 U.S.C. 2442. Include definitions appropriately.
open_questions: Should DFARS or PGI clarify if the prohibition applies to incremental or follow-on EMD contracts for MDAPs initiated before FY19?
Should DFARS cross-reference or restate the statutory definitions of EMD contract and MDAP, or simply cite 10 U.S.C. 2430 and new 2442?

manifests.description: Limits the scope of the prohibition so that it applies only to major defense acquisition programs with budget requests beginning in Fiscal Year 2019 or later, thereby exempting previously authorized programs. This is to allow a smooth transition for ongoing programs.
manifests.subject: Applicability date for LPTA prohibition
manifests.applies_to: Major defense acquisition programs with budget authority requested for FY19 or later
manifests.conditions: Not applicable to MDAPs with budget authority requested before FY19
manifests.effective_date: Applies to MDAPs for which budget authority is requested for FY2019 or a subsequent fiscal year
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: In the new or revised DFARS section, specify the effective date and applicability language to ensure the prohibition only covers MDAP EMD contracts with budget requests in FY19 or later.
open_questions: Does this require the DFARS to explicitly address how to treat contract modifications or additional EMD phases for MDAPs that began before FY19?
Should guidance be issued for edge cases such as re-baselined programs?

sections.section_heading: CONTRACT CLOSEOUT AUTHORITY.

manifests.description: Expands eligibility for Department of Defense special contract closeout authority by allowing any contract entered into at least 17 fiscal years before the current fiscal year (rather than only those entered into before fiscal year 2000) to qualify. This switches from a fixed cutoff date to a rolling window, enabling more contracts to be closed out under streamlined procedures as time passes.
manifests.subject: Rolling eligibility for contract closeout authority
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts eligible for special closeout authority
manifests.conditions: Contracts must be at least 17 fiscal years old, have no remaining deliverables, and meet further criteria in section 836(b) of NDAA FY17
amends: Section 836(b)(1), National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2017 (10 U.S.C. 2302 note)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 204.804 and related procedures should reference the rolling 17-year threshold for eligibility if implementing special contract closeout authority criteria.
open_questions: Should the 17-year period always be calculated from the precise contract award date, and how should situations with indefinite delivery or multi-phase awards be handled?
Is further DFARS guidance needed to ensure annual update of the rolling eligibility threshold and notification to contracting officers?

sections.section_heading: EXEMPTION FROM DESIGN-BUILD SELECTION PROCEDURES.

manifests.description: Expands and clarifies the exceptions to the 5-offeror cap for phase two of design-build construction contracts over $4 million. Now, contracting officers may set a higher maximum number of offerors if the solicitation is for an indefinite delivery-indefinite quantity (IDIQ) design-build contract, or if the head of the contracting activity (delegable no lower than the senior contracting official) approves a justification and the contracting officer documents that this is consistent with two-phase procedures' goals.
manifests.subject: Exceptions to maximum number of phase-two offerors in design-build two-phase selection procedures
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers conducting two-phase design-build selections for contracts over $4M
manifests.conditions: Contract value exceeds $4,000,000; applies to two-phase selection procedures for design-build construction per 10 U.S.C. 2305a(c).
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update or supplement DFARS Part 236 to clarify the expanded exceptions to the 5-offeror cap in two-phase design-build solicitation procedures.

sections.section_heading: USE OF LOWEST PRICE TECHNICALLY ACCEPTABLE SOURCE SELECTION PROCESS.

manifests.description: Further restricts when the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process can be used by adding two new required determinations: (1) that using a different source selection method would not provide significant innovation or future technological advantage, and (2) for contracts for goods, those goods must be predominantly expendable, nontechnical, or have a short life or shelf life. This ensures LPTA is used only when appropriate and not at the expense of innovation or for durable/technical items.
manifests.subject: New restrictions on LPTA use
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers using LPTA source selection
manifests.conditions: When considering LPTA for a DoD contract solicitation issued on or after 120 days after FY17 NDAA enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise DFARS 215.3 to explicitly add these two determinations as prerequisites to using LPTA.
open_questions: Are there exceptions for certain categories of goods that could create ambiguity about what is 'expendable', 'nontechnical', or has 'short life expectancy'?

manifests.description: Lowers the contract value threshold triggering mandatory Comptroller General reporting on LPTA contract awards from $10,000,000 to $5,000,000, significantly increasing oversight by covering more contracts.
manifests.subject: Expanded LPTA reporting requirement
manifests.applies_to: Comptroller General's annual report on DoD LPTA contract awards
manifests.conditions: For qualifying contracts awarded after the effective date, and applicable to specified reporting periods
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS coverage that references the reporting threshold for LPTA awards.
open_questions: Will the DoD need to adjust contract data reporting mechanisms to ensure the expanded dataset is captured accurately?

manifests.description: Specifies that the new $5 million threshold for LPTA contract reporting applies retroactively to the second, third, and fourth annual Comptroller General reports under FY17 NDAA Section 813(d), not just prospectively.
manifests.subject: Retroactive application of new LPTA reporting threshold
manifests.applies_to: Comptroller General's second, third, and fourth LPTA reports
manifests.conditions: Applies to report submissions for periods after the first report
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Issue DFARS Procedures, Guidance, and Information (PGI) or implementation memo clarifying the retroactive application to past reporting periods.
open_questions: Are supplemental instructions needed to retrieve historical contract data for the $5M to $10M range for past periods?

sections.section_heading: AMENDMENT RELATING TO APPLICABILITY OF INFLATION ADJUSTMENTS.

manifests.description: Ensures that inflation-adjusted dollar thresholds for acquisition-related requirements apply not only to new contracts and subcontracts, but also to any contract and any subcontract at any tier that are still in effect on the published date of the adjustment, regardless of when the contract or subcontract was originally awarded. This change aims to guarantee that updates to thresholds are uniformly applied across all active procurement instruments, preventing outdated thresholds from persisting in older agreements.
manifests.subject: Applicability of inflation adjustments to existing contracts and subcontracts
manifests.applies_to: All federal contracts and any subcontracts at any tier in effect on the date inflation-adjusted thresholds are published
manifests.conditions: Applies regardless of the date of award of the contract or subcontract
manifests.effective_date: Upon publication of the adjusted thresholds in the Federal Register
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Review and revise DFARS threshold references to clarify that inflation adjustments apply to all active contracts and subcontracts on the publication date, and not just those awarded after that date.
open_questions: How should agencies handle contracts whose terms may conflict with the retroactive application of new thresholds?
Are there specific contract types or categories that require further DFARS/FAR guidance for implementing this retroactive adjustment?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2305 note.>> ENHANCED POST-AWARD DEBRIEFING RIGHTS.

manifests.description: Requires DoD to revise DFARS so that all post-award debriefings for contract awards over $100 million must include disclosure of the agency’s written source selection award determination (with necessary redactions for confidential/proprietary information). For awards between $10 million and $100 million to a small business or nontraditional contractor, such disclosure is provided upon request.
manifests.subject: Mandatory source selection disclosure in debriefings
manifests.applies_to: All DoD contract awards and debriefings, and small businesses/nontraditional contractors for awards above $10M
manifests.conditions: Award above $100M triggers mandatory disclosure; awards above $10M, but ≤$100M, trigger disclosure only at request for small businesses or nontraditional contractors
manifests.effective_date: No later than 180 days after enactment (by mid-2018)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 215.506
open_questions: How is 'nontraditional contractor' defined for this purpose?
Are FOIA or procurement integrity protections adequately addressed in the redaction process?

manifests.description: Requires that all DoD contract awards, task orders, and delivery orders valued at $10 million or more include a right to a written or oral debriefing for offerors.
manifests.subject: Universal right to debriefing at $10M+
manifests.applies_to: All DoD contract, task, and delivery order awards $10 million and above
manifests.conditions: Applies for all awards/orders ≥$10M
manifests.effective_date: No later than 180 days after enactment (by mid-2018)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 215.506
open_questions: Does this override any more restrictive dollar thresholds previously in DFARS?

manifests.description: Requires both unsuccessful and winning offerors to receive the disclosures and debriefing rights described in manifests 1 and 2.
manifests.subject: Debriefing rights for both successful and unsuccessful offerors
manifests.applies_to: All offerors (winning and losing) for covered DoD awards
manifests.conditions: As specified for manifests 1 and 2
manifests.effective_date: No later than 180 days after enactment (by mid-2018)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 215.506
open_questions: Are there debriefing exceptions for winning offerors in certain award configurations?

manifests.description: Requires robust procedures, consistent with 10 U.S.C. 2305(b)(5)(D) and applicable FAR/DFARS procedures, to protect confidential and proprietary information of offerors during post-award debriefings.
manifests.subject: Protection of confidential/proprietary information in debriefings
manifests.applies_to: All offerors during DoD post-award debriefings
manifests.effective_date: No later than 180 days after enactment (by mid-2018)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 215.506; cross-reference to DFARS 203.104-4

manifests.description: Updates 10 U.S.C. 2305(b)(5) to allow disappointed offerors to submit additional questions within 2 business days after receiving a post-award debriefing, and requires the agency to provide written answers within 5 business days; the debriefing is not considered concluded until responses are delivered.
manifests.subject: Follow-up questions process for disappointed offerors
manifests.applies_to: Disappointed offerors in DoD procurements
manifests.conditions: Follows a post-award debriefing; additional questions submitted within 2 business days
manifests.effective_date: On amendment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 215.506; add procedures for follow-up questions and written responses
open_questions: Is there a cap on the number or scope of allowed questions?
What if the agency fails to respond within the deadline?

manifests.description: Amends 31 U.S.C. 3553(d)(4) so that for DoD procurements, the 5-day period for submitting a GAO protest does not start until written responses to post-debriefing questions are delivered to the disappointed offeror.
manifests.subject: Delayed commencement of GAO protest window for DoD post-award debriefings
manifests.applies_to: Disappointed offerors intending to protest DoD procurements
manifests.conditions: Only for DoD procurements; starts when agency provides written answers to debriefing follow-up questions, if any
manifests.effective_date: On amendment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 233.171 or similar protest procedures referencing 31 U.S.C. 3553
open_questions: Should notice of this delayed protest window be integrated into debriefing communication templates?

sections.section_heading: LIMITATION ON UNILATERAL DEFINITIZATION.

manifests.description: Creates a new requirement that, for undefinitized contractual actions (UCAs) valued at more than $50 million, if the government and contractor cannot agree on contract terms, specifications, and price within the statutory period, the contracting officer cannot unilaterally finalize (definitize) those terms, specifications, or price over the contractor’s objection unless (1) the service acquisition executive or the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment (depending on which entity awarded the contract) gives written approval for the action, (2) the written approval is provided to the contractor, and (3) a 30-day waiting period after such notice has elapsed.
manifests.subject: Limitation on Unilateral Definitization for High-Value UCAs
manifests.applies_to: Contracting officers handling DoD undefinitized contractual actions over $50 million
manifests.conditions: If contractual agreement is not reached within the period specified in 10 U.S.C. 2326(b)(1)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise/Add to DFARS Subpart 217.74 (especially 217.7404), adding procedures for executive-level approval and contractor notification with a 30-day waiting period before unilateral definitization over $50 million.
open_questions: How does the 30-day waiting period affect urgent operational requirements or contracting timelines?
If multiple unilateral attempts are made, does each require new approval and waiting period?

manifests.description: Updates an outdated statutory cross-reference in 10 U.S.C. 2326(b)(3), changing 'subsection (g)' to 'subsection (h)' to reflect the addition/renumbering of subsections. This ensures proper reference to the waiver provision for foreign military sales.
manifests.subject: Technical Amendment – Cross-Reference Update
manifests.applies_to: Statutory references in 10 U.S.C. 2326
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: No direct change necessary to DFARS unless statutory references appear and require updating for consistency.

manifests.description: Directs the Secretary of Defense to update the Defense Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement (DFARS) within 120 days after enactment to implement the amended 10 U.S.C. 2326, specifically including the new limitation on unilateral definitization for high-value UCAs.
manifests.subject: DFARS Update Implementation Directive
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense—DFARS/FAR drafters
manifests.conditions: Not later than 120 days after the enactment of this Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Mandate a DFARS case revising Subpart 217.74 procedures and any related policies on UCAs to incorporate the new approval/notice/wait requirements.

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2302 note.>> STATEMENTS OF PURPOSE FOR DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE ACQUISITION.

manifests.description: Requires that the Defense Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement (DFARS) be revised to include a statement that the defense acquisition system exists to manage U.S. investments in technologies, programs, and product support needed to achieve the national security strategy set by the President and to support U.S. Armed Forces.
manifests.subject: Statement of purpose: managing investments for national security
manifests.applies_to: Defense acquisition system as defined in 10 U.S.C. 2545; DFARS applicability
manifests.conditions: DFARS revision due within 180 days of enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add a new section at the start of DFARS, e.g., Part 201, to codify these purpose statements and coordinate with DoDD 5000.01/DoDI 5000.02.
open_questions: Is this a policy preamble only, or should it guide regulatory interpretation and acquisition decisions?
How does this interact with existing policy language in DoDD 5000.01 and DoDI 5000.02 already referenced in DFARS?

manifests.description: Mandates a statement in DFARS that the Department of Defense's investment strategy should support both current and future U.S. Armed Forces, not just present needs.
manifests.subject: Statement of purpose: investment strategy for current and future forces
manifests.applies_to: DFARS policy context for defense acquisition strategy
manifests.conditions: DFARS revision due within 180 days of enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Incorporate as part of the same new policy section, emphasizing long-term force support.
open_questions: Should this affect acquisition timelines or budgeting, or is it a statement of intent?

manifests.description: Requires DFARS to state that the primary objective of DoD acquisition is to acquire quality products that satisfy user needs, improve mission capability and operational support measurably, are delivered promptly, and are reasonably priced.
manifests.subject: Statement of purpose: objectives for quality, value, timeliness in acquisition
manifests.applies_to: DFARS guidance for DoD acquisition processes
manifests.conditions: DFARS revision due within 180 days of enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Standardize as an opening statement guiding interpretation of performance, value, and process requirements in DFARS.
open_questions: Should individual acquisition rules require explicit findings under this objective?
Does this overlap with existing DoD policy or DFARS text?

sections.section_heading: EXPANSION OF DEFINITION OF COMPETITIVE PROCEDURES TO INCLUDE COMPETITIVE SELECTION FOR AWARD OF SCIENCE AND TECHNOLOGY PROPOSALS.

manifests.description: Broadens the definition of 'competitive procedures' in defense acquisition law by replacing 'basic research' with 'science and technology.' This change allows competitive selection for a wider range of research and technology proposals (not limited to basic research) via general solicitation and peer/scientific review to qualify as having used competitive procedures. The purpose is to give DoD greater flexibility to use streamlined competitive awards for a broader array of science and technology activities.
manifests.subject: Expansion of competitive procedures definition to include science and technology proposals
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and DoD contracting officers applying the definition of 'competitive procedures' under procurement law
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Review DFARS 202.101 (Definitions) and any other references to the definition of 'competitive procedures' to ensure 'science and technology' language is adopted in place of or in addition to 'basic research.'
open_questions: Are there other DFARS clauses or guidance that specifically reference 'basic research' under this authority and need conforming updates to reflect 'science and technology'?
Does DoD plan to issue further guidance specifying what efforts qualify as 'science and technology proposals' under this expanded definition (e.g., applied research, advanced technology development)?

sections.section_heading: AMENDMENTS TO THE MENTOR-PROTEGE PROGRAM OF THE DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE.

manifests.description: Redefines the eligibility requirements for mentor firms in the DoD Mentor-Protégé Program, requiring them to be eligible for federal contracts, demonstrate meaningful qualifications and financial health, and either have at least $100,000,000 in DoD contracts/subcontracts in the prior fiscal year or receive direct approval for their development capability from the Secretary of Defense. Tightens and clarifies mentor selection to promote higher-quality assistance.
manifests.subject: Mentor firm eligibility criteria
manifests.applies_to: All firms seeking to participate as mentors in the DoD Mentor-Protégé Program
manifests.conditions: Mentor firm must seek to enter an agreement under the DoD Mentor-Protégé Program
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS Appendix I, section I-102 and relevant DFARS 219.7102/219.7103-1 provisions to reflect new mentor eligibility criteria.
open_questions: Is the $100,000,000 contract/subcontract threshold fixed or subject to inflation adjustment over time?

manifests.description: Prohibits mentor firms from entering into an agreement with a protégé if the Small Business Administration (SBA) has found affiliation between them, and requires the Secretary of Defense to request an SBA determination if there is reason to suspect affiliation. Ensures independence and proper alignment with small business program goals.
manifests.subject: Prohibition on mentor-protégé affiliation
manifests.applies_to: Proposed mentor-protégé pairs in the DoD Mentor-Protégé Program
manifests.conditions: SBA has made or is requested to make a determination of affiliation
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise DFARS Appendix I for mandatory SBA affiliation checks prior to agreement approval; update DFARS 219.7103-1 process for mentor-protégé approvals.
open_questions: What procedure governs if the SBA does not respond to a request for an affiliation determination in a timely manner?

manifests.description: Defines 'affiliation' for the Mentor-Protégé Program as having the same meaning as in section 121.103 of title 13, Code of Federal Regulations (or any successor regulation), ensuring regulatory alignment with SBA practices.
manifests.subject: Definition of affiliation
manifests.applies_to: All mentor-protégé program participants and program administration
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update all DFARS and Appendix I references to 'affiliation' to explicitly reference 13 CFR 121.103 definition.

manifests.description: Expands eligible recipients of mentor developmental assistance to include Women's Business Centers as described in 15 U.S.C. 656, alongside current categories like procurement technical assistance centers and small business development centers.
manifests.subject: Expansion of eligible protégé types
manifests.applies_to: Mentor firms, protégé applicants, Women's Business Centers
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Expressly add Women's Business Centers to the list of eligible entities in DFARS Appendix I, section I-102, and DFARS 219.7102/7103.
open_questions: Do Women's Business Centers follow identical participation requirements as other listed protégé types or do unique rules apply?

sections.section_heading: IMPROVING CONTRACTOR COMPLIANCE.

manifests.description: Requires Offices of Small and Disadvantaged Business Utilization in federal agencies to assist small businesses that have won contracts or subcontracts with finding resources for education and training on compliance with contracting regulations (including the Federal Acquisition Regulation) after award. The goal is to ensure small businesses have support and access to compliance information post-award.
manifests.subject: Post-award compliance assistance by offices of Small and Disadvantaged Business Utilization
manifests.applies_to: Offices of Small and Disadvantaged Business Utilization in federal agencies
manifests.conditions: Applies after small business is awarded a contract or subcontract under the Small Business Act, Title 10, or Title 41.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add requirement for OSDBUs to provide post-award compliance resource assistance. Consider inclusion in DFARS PGI for OSDBU procedures.
open_questions: Is assistance limited to providing information, or does it include training directly provided by OSDBUs?
Is there any baseline for resource quality to be provided?

manifests.description: Requires that the DoD Mentor-Protégé program mentor-protégé agreement include specific detail on how the mentor will assist the protégé with understanding and complying with federal and Defense contract regulations (including FAR/DFARS) after a subcontract award. This change formalizes compliance as a core part of the developmental support mentors give their protégés.
manifests.subject: Mentor obligation for compliance assistance under DoD Mentor-Protégé Program
manifests.applies_to: Mentor firms (and protégés) under the Department of Defense Mentor-Protégé Program
manifests.conditions: Applies to mentor-protégé agreements where a subcontract is to be awarded, if applicable.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 219.71 and Mentor-Protégé Program guidance should require mentor-protégé agreements to address post-award regulatory compliance.
open_questions: Does DoD require mentors to pre-identify specific resources/training or just state intent to assist?
Is evidence of assistance delivery required, or is inclusion in the agreement sufficient?

manifests.description: Directs the SBA Administrator to make available—through small business development centers, procurement technical assistance centers, and the SBA website—a comprehensive, accessible list of resources aimed at small businesses seeking education and assistance on post-award compliance with federal contracting regulations, including the FAR. This is intended to ensure no small business is left unaware of compliance obligations and resources.
manifests.subject: SBA to publish post-award compliance resource list for small businesses
manifests.applies_to: Small Business Administration (Administrator), small business development centers, Procurement Technical Assistance Program entities, and small businesses
manifests.conditions: For small business concerns seeking post-award compliance assistance.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS PGI can direct contracting officers/OSDBUs to refer awardees to the SBA's compliance resource list.
open_questions: Does the SBA have to vet the quality of listed resources?
How often must the lists be updated to remain current?

manifests.description: Adds to the duties of Procurement Center Representatives the responsibility to assist small businesses who have received contracts or subcontracts in finding resources for education and training on compliance with contracting regulations, after award. This ensures post-award guidance is a core, codified part of their role.
manifests.subject: Post-award compliance assistance by Procurement Center Representatives
manifests.applies_to: Procurement Center Representatives
manifests.conditions: After award of a contract or subcontract to a small business concern.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Include this duty in DFARS/DFARS PGI for PCR roles; consider updating standard PCR job description and related materials.
open_questions: Does this create a need for additional PCR resources or is it a clarification of existing duties?
Can PCRs provide custom guidance or only refer to public materials?

manifests.description: Requires that any agreement under the SBA Mentor-Protégé Program now explicitly include the types of assistance a mentor firm will provide to help the protégé firm comply with contracting requirements after contract or subcontract award. This change aligns the program to ensure continued support post-award for compliance, not just for winning contracts.
manifests.subject: Mentor obligation for compliance assistance under SBA Mentor-Protégé Program
manifests.applies_to: Mentor and protégé firms in the SBA Mentor-Protégé Program
manifests.conditions: Applies to all mentor-protégé agreements under the program.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS may reference this SBA requirement as a best practice for DoD mentor-protégé relationships when overlaps arise.
open_questions: How will compliance overlaps be managed where a protégé participates in both the SBA and DoD programs?
Does 'assistance' require formal training, guidance, or merely listing available resources?

sections.section_heading: SCOPE OF REVIEW BY PROCUREMENT CENTER REPRESENTATIVES.

manifests.description: Prohibits the Administrator of the Small Business Administration (SBA) from limiting the scope of review for any procurement center representative (PCR) on any solicitation for a contract or task order, regardless of whether it is set aside for small businesses, reserved for small businesses under a multiple award contract, or results in a bundled or consolidated contract or task order.
manifests.subject: Scope of PCR review – no limitation permitted by SBA Administrator
manifests.applies_to: Small Business Administration Administrator; Procurement Center Representatives
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 219.201 could be updated to reflect the broad scope of PCR review authority.

manifests.description: Requires the SBA Administrator to limit the scope of a PCR's review of solicitations by or for the Department of Defense, unless the contracting agency requests a review, when the solicitation is for: (1) contracts under the Arms Export Control Act sec. 22 (foreign military sales); (2) humanitarian operations as defined by 10 U.S.C. 401(e); (3) contingency operations as defined by 10 U.S.C. 101(a)(13); (4) contracts awarded pursuant to an agreement with a foreign government where U.S. forces are deployed; or (5) awards where both the place of award and place of performance are outside the United States and its territories.
manifests.subject: Forced scope limitation for certain DoD PCR reviews; exceptions listed
manifests.applies_to: Small Business Administration Administrator; Procurement Center Representatives
manifests.conditions: Solicitations by/for DoD under specified circumstances, unless the contracting agency requests a review.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 219.201 and related subparts should codify the exceptions and process for agency-requested reviews.
open_questions: Should the process for a contracting agency to request a review be standardized or documented explicitly?

manifests.description: Excludes contracts that are not subject to PCR review under the new DoD exceptions from being considered in the calculation of agency small business goals. In other words, these contracts will not count toward an agency’s small business goals.
manifests.subject: Exclusion of certain contracts from small business goal calculations
manifests.applies_to: Federal agencies establishing small business contracting goals
manifests.conditions: Contracts excluded from review under 15 U.S.C. 644(l)(9)(B)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 219.201 or related subparts on small business goaling should clarify exclusion of these contracts.

sections.section_heading: MAINTENANCE OF PROHIBITION ON PROCUREMENT BY DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE OF PEOPLE'S REPUBLIC OF CHINA-ORIGIN ITEMS THAT MEET THE DEFINITION OF GOODS AND SERVICES CONTROLLED AS MUNITIONS ITEMS WHEN MOVED TO THE ``600 SERIES'' OF THE COMMERCE CONTROL LIST.

manifests.description: Expands the scope of the Department of Defense's procurement prohibition to include not just items on the United States Munitions List (USML/ITAR), but also items listed in the '600 series' of the Commerce Control List under the Export Administration Regulations, when those items are of Chinese origin and meet the definition of munitions items. The intent is to ensure that munitions items that were moved from ITAR to the Commerce Control List but are still sensitive cannot be procured from Chinese military companies.
manifests.subject: Extension of China-origin munitions procurement ban to 600 Series items
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense procurement contracting, including all subcontract tiers
manifests.conditions: Relevant only to items on the 600 series of the Commerce Control List that previously met ITAR munitions definitions; exceptions are for visits, testing, or intelligence as in prior law
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 225.770, 252.225-7007, and 225.003 definitions; ensure explicit coverage of 600 series items

manifests.description: Adds a statutory definition for the '600 series of the control list of the Export Administration Regulations' to mean the 600 series of the Commerce Control List in Supplement No. 1 to part 774 of subtitle B of title 15 of the Code of Federal Regulations. This provides a clear, authoritative definition to prevent ambiguity about which items are covered.
manifests.subject: Definition of '600 series' for purposes of the procurement prohibition
manifests.applies_to: Interpretation of terms in the procurement prohibition
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Insert or cross-reference this statutory definition into DFARS 225.003 and related sections

manifests.description: Makes technical corrections to Section 1211 of NDAA FY06 by replacing both instances of 'Trafficking' with 'Traffic' in the reference to the International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR), to accurately reflect the title of the regulation.
manifests.subject: Correction of ITAR reference (from 'Trafficking' to 'Traffic')
manifests.applies_to: Legal text and all future references to ITAR in this context
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS references to reflect the correct name, 'International Traffic in Arms Regulations.'

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2241 note.>> PROHIBITION ON USE OF FUNDS FOR CERTAIN PROGRAMS AND PROJECTS OF THE DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE IN AFGHANISTAN THAT CANNOT BE SAFELY ACCESSED BY UNITED STATES GOVERNMENT PERSONNEL.

manifests.description: Prohibits the Department of Defense from obligating or spending funds on any construction or infrastructure program or project in Afghanistan if United States Government personnel or authorized representatives cannot safely access the project site to conduct oversight. This applies only to programs or projects initiated on or after the enactment date of this Act.
manifests.subject: Prohibition on funding inaccessible Afghanistan infrastructure projects
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense funds for infrastructure/construction programs or projects in Afghanistan
manifests.conditions: Applies only if U.S. Government personnel cannot safely access the project for oversight; only for projects initiated on or after enactment of this Act
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment of NDAA FY2017
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Consider new DFARS Subpart regarding project oversight access requirements for Afghanistan infrastructure contracts
open_questions: How is 'safe access' determined or assessed in the field?
Does this prohibition apply to contracts that are modified after the enactment date, or only entirely new projects?

manifests.description: Establishes a waiver process for the above prohibition, allowing exceptions if a written determination is made that the project meets specified conditions. The approval authority varies by project lifecycle cost: less than $1 million (contracting officer), $1M–<$20M (Commander of Combined Security Transition Command-Afghanistan), $20M–<$40M (Commander of U.S. Forces-Afghanistan), and $40M or more (Secretary of Defense). Determinations must find the project meets seven criteria related to U.S. interests, Afghan government needs, coordination, security, safeguards, sustainment, and results metrics.
manifests.subject: Waiver authorities and criteria for Afghanistan project oversight prohibition
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense officials with authority over Afghanistan infrastructure/construction programs or projects
manifests.conditions: Waiver must be based on a positive determination of all seven statutory criteria
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment of NDAA FY2017
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS coverage for documentation and approval/waiver process by dollar threshold for Afghanistan infrastructure projects
open_questions: Are all criteria (A)-(G) weighed equally, or can some be overridden if others are especially strong?
Is a negative finding on any single criterion an absolute bar to waiver eligibility?

manifests.description: Requires that the Secretary of Defense notify Congress within 15 days when issuing a waiver for any Afghanistan infrastructure program or project with an estimated lifecycle cost of $40 million or more.
manifests.subject: Congressional notification for high-value waivers
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense granting waivers for Afghanistan infrastructure projects ≥ $40 million
manifests.conditions: Waiver issued for program or project as described
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment of NDAA FY2017
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS/PGI guidance to notify Congress as part of the waiver approval workflow when applicable
open_questions: Does the notification requirement include any specific information that must be reported to Congress?
Should the notification requirement be cross-referenced to other reporting requirements for Afghanistan-related programs?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2331 note.>> SELECTION OF SERVICE PROVIDERS FOR AUDITING SERVICES AND AUDIT READINESS SERVICES.

manifests.description: The Department of Defense must select service providers for auditing services and audit readiness services based on 'best value' to the Department, as determined by the resource sponsor for the auditing contract. Selection may no longer be made using the 'lowest price technically acceptable' (LPTA) method. This ensures that quality and value are prioritized over simply choosing the cheapest technically acceptable offer, to improve the effectiveness and reliability of audit outcomes.
manifests.subject: Source selection for audit and audit readiness services
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officials and acquisition personnel procuring auditing and audit readiness services
