manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 237.270 (Acquisition of audit services) is the most appropriate place to implement this; clarify that selection for these contracts must use best value and prohibit use of LPTA.
open_questions: Should a specific definition of 'resource sponsor' be provided, or is existing usage clear across DoD contracting activities?
Are there exceptions (e.g., urgent or temporary audits) where LPTA might still be permitted, or is this intended as a blanket prohibition?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2305 note.>> REQUIREMENT AND REVIEW RELATING TO USE OF BRAND NAMES OR BRAND- NAME OR EQUIVALENT DESCRIPTIONS IN SOLICITATIONS.

manifests.description: Mandates that competition in Department of Defense contracts cannot be limited by the use of brand names, brand-name equivalents, or proprietary specifications or standards in solicitations unless a proper justification is provided and approved per procedures outlined in 10 U.S.C. 2304(f). This is intended to reduce anti-competitive practices and ensure fair competition.
manifests.subject: Limiting anti-competitive use of brand names/proprietary specifications in solicitations
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officials and offices issuing solicitations
manifests.conditions: Specification of brand names or proprietary material in solicitations unless justified and approved as required
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 211.105, 211.272, 211.273, and related procedures should be updated to require justification and approval for use of brand name or proprietary specifications in DoD solicitations, with cross-reference to the approval standards in 10 U.S.C. 2304(f).
open_questions: Does this apply to solicitations already in process at the time of enactment or only to new solicitations?

manifests.description: Requires the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics to review current policy, guidance, regulations, and training related to specifications used in information technology acquisitions to ensure elimination of unjustified anti-competitive requirements, with a focus on brand names and proprietary specifications. The review must also include current acquisition training curricula.
manifests.subject: Review of anti-competitive specifications in IT acquisitions
manifests.applies_to: Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics
manifests.conditions: Within 180 days of the enactment of this Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Review results to inform updates to DFARS 239 (Acquisition of Information Technology) and related guidance; possible revision of solicitation standards and training materials.
open_questions: Should findings from the review result in immediate regulatory changes, or only recommendations for future action?

manifests.description: Requires the Under Secretary to brief the Committees on Armed Services of the Senate and the House of Representatives on the results of the review of IT acquisition specifications within 270 days of enactment.
manifests.subject: Reporting results of IT specification review to Congress
manifests.applies_to: Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics
manifests.conditions: Within 270 days after enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure reporting/briefing requirement is tracked by the Under Secretary’s office and communication with congressional committees is established.

manifests.description: Requires the Under Secretary, within one year of enactment, to revise policies, guidance, and training to implement appropriate recommendations resulting from the review of information technology solicitation practices.
manifests.subject: Implementation of recommendations from IT specification review
manifests.applies_to: Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics; any relevant policy/training offices
manifests.conditions: Within one year after enactment; as appropriate based on review recommendations
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Coordinate DFARS 239 and related policy/training manual updates with the recommendations from the IT acquisition specifications review.
open_questions: What process should determine which recommendations from the review must be implemented?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2501 note.>> GREATER INTEGRATION OF THE NATIONAL TECHNOLOGY AND INDUSTRIAL BASE.

manifests.description: The Secretary of Defense is required to develop, by January 1, 2018, a comprehensive plan to reduce barriers and enable seamless integration among the U.S., UK, Australian, and Canadian elements of the national technology and industrial base (NTIB). The plan must specifically address: (1) describing NTIB’s components; (2) identifying barriers to integration of knowledge, goods, and services within the NTIB; (3) identifying existing authorities to further integration and a plan to maximize them; (4) identifying needed changes in export rules and laws aligned to civil-military integration; (5) recommending changes to treaties, agreements, and implementation, including relevant defense trade cooperation treaties and export exemptions.
manifests.subject: NTIB Integration Plan Requirement
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and Department of Defense organizational elements responsible for industrial base policy
manifests.conditions: Plan must be developed by January 1, 2018
manifests.effective_date: No later than January 1, 2018
source_authorities: Section 38 of the Arms Export Control Act
U.S.-Australia and U.S.-UK Defense Trade Cooperation Treaties
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 225.79, 225.7902, PGI for NTIB integration strategy and procedures
open_questions: What constitutes 'seamless integration' in practice and how will success be measured?
Will the resulting plan be made public, or remain a classified/FOUO strategy?

manifests.description: The definition of 'national technology and industrial base' in 10 U.S.C. 2500(1) is amended to explicitly include 'the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, Australia,' after 'United States,'—expanding statutory recognition for inclusion of these allied countries in the base.
manifests.subject: NTIB Definition Expansion
manifests.applies_to: Definition of NTIB as cited in Title 10 and all related DoD industrial base policy
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment (December 23, 2016)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS and PGI references affecting NTIB eligibility, access, and processes to reflect explicit inclusion of UK and Australia
open_questions: Are there defense programs or clauses where this revised definition directly impacts eligibility, source determination, or exemptions?
Are implementing countries recognized elsewhere (statute, treaties) in a manner inconsistent with this new definition?

manifests.description: The Secretary of Defense must report in the annual report required under 10 U.S.C. 2504 on the progress of implementing the NTIB integration plan (mandated in section 881(a)), ensuring congressional notification of actions and results.
manifests.subject: NTIB Integration Plan Annual Reporting
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and Defense Industrial Base policy offices
manifests.conditions: Report must be included in the annual report required by 10 U.S.C. 2504
manifests.effective_date: Next annual reporting cycle after enactment (due by March 1 each year)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Reference this reporting requirement in regulatory guidance for annual reporting (DFARS, and procedures for inputs to 10 U.S.C. 2504 report); consider cross-referencing DoDI process for industrial base reports.
open_questions: Is there a specific template or structure for this portion of the report, or will it rely on DoD’s discretion?
Should this be made a standing DFARS reporting template for future years?

manifests.description: The Secretary’s integration plan must recommend opportunities for updating, amending, or improving implementation of: (a) Section 126.5 of 22 CFR (ITAR Canada exemption), (b) the U.S.-Australia and U.S.-UK Defense Trade Cooperation Treaties, (c) related agreements among NTIB countries, with the goal of enhancing allied interoperability and NTIB integration in the supply of defense articles.
manifests.subject: Recommendations for Treaty and Export Control Improvements
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense plan contents and recommendations for U.S. and allied authorities
manifests.conditions: Plan must include these recommendations as feasible
manifests.effective_date: No later than January 1, 2018
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Cross-reference ongoing treaty and ITAR-related exemption implementation in DFARS 225.7902 and attach/align with guidance on integrated acquisition and interoperability initiatives, especially when proposals for legal or regulatory change result from the plan.
open_questions: Is DoD also responsible for implementing or only recommending changes to the international agreements and treaties?
How will recommendations be coordinated with State Department/export control authorities?

sections.section_heading: TREATMENT OF SERVICES PROVIDED BY NONTRADITIONAL CONTRACTORS AS COMMERCIAL ITEMS.

manifests.description: Expands the treatment of services provided by nontraditional defense contractors, requiring that such services will be treated as commercial items for DoD procurement purposes if (1) they are provided by a business unit qualifying as a nontraditional defense contractor, (2) use the same pool of employees as for commercial customers, and (3) are priced using a methodology similar to commercial pricing. This enables DoD to use streamlined commercial item procurement procedures for these services, even if they do not otherwise meet the standard definition of 'commercial item,' thereby lowering barriers for new supplier entry.
manifests.subject: Treatment of certain nontraditional contractor services as commercial items
manifests.applies_to: Services provided by business units that are nontraditional defense contractors
manifests.conditions: Applies only if the services use the same employees as for commercial customers and similar commercial pricing methodologies.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS should clarify that qualifying services from nontraditional defense contractors (same workforce and pricing as commercial customers) must be treated as commercial items, regardless of 10 U.S.C. 2376(1) or FAR 2.101. Update DFARS 212.102 and 252.215-7013. Establish documentation requirements for contracting officers.
open_questions: What constitutes 'the same pool of employees' and what evidence is sufficient to document this?
How similar must pricing methodologies be? Is contractor self-certification sufficient, or is additional supporting evidence required?
Does this eliminate any market research or written determination requirements under FAR or DFARS for these cases?

manifests.description: Updates the heading of section 2380A, and its identification in the table of sections, from 'Treatment of goods and services provided by nontraditional defense contractors as commercial items' to 'Treatment of certain items as commercial items' to reflect the broadened content. This is a technical conforming change for clarity and accuracy.
manifests.subject: Section heading and table of sections update
manifests.applies_to: 10 U.S.C. 2380A (now 2380a), Table of sections for chapter 140
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: No policy update required; make conforming edits in internal references and training materials to match the new section heading and citation.

sections.section_heading: TREATMENT OF COMMINGLED ITEMS PURCHASED BY CONTRACTORS AS COMMERCIAL ITEMS.

manifests.description: Requires the Department of Defense to treat items valued at less than $10,000 that are purchased by a contractor for use in the performance of multiple contracts with DoD and other parties, and that cannot be identified to any particular contract, as 'commercial items' for purposes of Chapter 140. This overrides the usual statutory definition of 'commercial item' for these cases to reduce administrative burden for small, commingled inventory items.
manifests.subject: Commingled small-value contractor purchases treated as commercial items
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts involving contractors who purchase and commingle small-value items for multiple contracts
manifests.conditions: Item value is less than $10,000; purchased for use in multiple contracts (with DoD and others); not identifiable to any particular contract
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 212.1, 244.402, and related commercial item acquisition policies should be updated to expressly recognize the treatment of commingled items as commercial items in accordance with 10 U.S.C. 2380B.
open_questions: Is further clarification needed on what constitutes 'not identifiable to any particular contract' (e.g., minimum documentation or inventory practices)?
Should regulations prescribe specific procedures or controls for contractor documentation of such commingled items?

manifests.description: Directs the insertion of a new entry for section 2380B in the table of sections for Chapter 140, confirming the addition of this statutory section.
manifests.subject: Clerical addition to table of sections for Chapter 140
manifests.applies_to: Table of sections for 10 U.S.C. Chapter 140 (Procurement of Commercial Items)
amends: Table of sections for 10 U.S.C. Chapter 140

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2305 note.>> USE OF COMMERCIAL OR NON- GOVERNMENT STANDARDS IN LIEU OF MILITARY SPECIFICATIONS AND STANDARDS.

manifests.description: Requires the Department of Defense to use commercial or non-Government specifications and standards instead of military ones in all acquisitions, unless no practical alternative exists to meet user needs.
manifests.subject: Preference for commercial/non-Government standards
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense acquisition activities and officials
manifests.conditions: Unless no practical alternative exists to meet user needs
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 211.273 should be revised to clarify and emphasize this preference for all acquisition categories.
open_questions: What is the process for determining and documenting that 'no practical alternative' exists?

manifests.description: Limits the use of military specifications in procurement to cases where only an exact design solution is needed and there is no acceptable commercial or non-Government standard, or using a commercial or non-Government standard is not cost effective.
manifests.subject: Limitation on use of military specifications
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense acquisition activities and officials
manifests.conditions: When no acceptable commercial or non-Government standard exists or use of a commercial or non-Government standard is not cost effective
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 211.273 and related PGI to clarify these limitations and require justification for use of military specifications.
open_questions: Who decides what is 'cost effective' or 'acceptable,' and what criteria must be used?

manifests.description: Requires that waivers allowing use of military specifications when otherwise restricted must be approved by a senior acquisition official: milestone decision authority, service acquisition executive, or the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics.
manifests.subject: Waiver process for military specifications
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense acquisition activities and officials
manifests.conditions: When military specifications are to be used under the limited exceptions
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add to DFARS 211.273 and/or PGI a waiver process specifying required senior officials as approvers.
open_questions: Are there documentation, reporting, or tracking requirements for these waivers?

manifests.description: Directs the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics to revise DFARS within 180 days to encourage contractors to propose commercial or non-Government standards and industry-wide practices that meet the intent of military specifications and standards.
manifests.subject: Revision of DFARS to encourage contractor proposals of commercial standards
manifests.applies_to: Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics; contractors
manifests.conditions: DFARS revision must occur no later than 180 days after enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Explicitly update DFARS 211.273 and 252.211-7005 and related guidance to encourage such proposals.
open_questions: How directly must DFARS encourage contractors?
Are contractors required to justify not proposing commercial standards?

manifests.description: Requires the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics to form partnerships with appropriate industry associations to develop commercial or non-Government standards to replace military specifications wherever practicable.
manifests.subject: Partnerships to develop commercial standards
manifests.applies_to: Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics
manifests.conditions: Where practicable
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add PGI guidance related to DoD engagement with industry standards bodies.
open_questions: What criteria define when replacement is 'practicable'?
Is there a timeline for developing and implementing such replacement standards?

manifests.description: Requires updating training, education, and guidance programs throughout DoD to incorporate reforms on the use of specifications and standards, emphasizing commercial/non-Government standards.
manifests.subject: Reform of training, education, and guidance programs
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense educational/training organizations
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Coordinate with DAU and integrate updates into relevant PGI or training references.
open_questions: What is the expected timeline for updating all relevant training and guidance?
Who is responsible for overseeing and certifying the updates are implemented?

manifests.description: Requires the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics to negotiate department-wide licenses for standards and to maintain an inventory of such licenses accessible to DoD organizations.
manifests.subject: Centralized licensing and inventory for standards
manifests.applies_to: Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Reference a central DFARS or PGI location for access to the license inventory and guidance on acquisition of standards.
open_questions: How must the inventory be maintained and how often updated?
What defines adequate 'accessibility' for DoD organizations?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2334 note.>> REPEAL OF MAJOR AUTOMATED INFORMATION SYSTEMS PROVISIONS.

manifests.description: Repeals the entirety of Chapter 144A of Title 10, U.S. Code, which created and governed 'major automated information system programs' (MAIS) within the Department of Defense, ending all requirements, reporting, and oversight structures unique to MAIS programs.
manifests.subject: Repeal of all statutory provisions for major automated information system programs (MAIS)
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense; any programs previously considered major automated information system programs
manifests.conditions: Effective September 30, 2017
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove or obsolete any DFARS coverage, reporting requirements, or internal processes in the DFARS that reference or implement 10 U.S.C. Ch. 144A or 'major automated information system program' provisions.
open_questions: Are there DoD policies or regulatory actions that rely solely on Ch. 144A and now require rescinding or revision?
How will programs previously managed as MAIS now be tracked, reported, or managed under DFARS or other DoD systems?

manifests.description: Removes the listing for Chapter 144A from the tables of chapters in Title 10 (at both the beginning of Subtitle A and at the beginning of Part IV of Subtitle A), conforming the organizational tables to the substantive repeal.
manifests.subject: Conforming amendment—removal from statutory tables
manifests.applies_to: U.S. Code structural tables and users indexing Title 10 chapters
manifests.conditions: Effective September 30, 2017
amends: 10 U.S.C. Subtitle A Table of Chapters
10 U.S.C. Subtitle A Part IV Table of Chapters
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Generally not required in DFARS unless DFARS references these tables or chapter locations explicitly.

manifests.description: Amends 10 U.S.C. § 2334(a)(2) to remove references to 'major automated information system programs' under the repealed Chapter 144A, narrowing Department of Defense cost estimation responsibilities to only major defense acquisition programs or major subprograms under Chapter 144. This consolidates oversight and reporting responsibilities.
manifests.subject: Narrowing scope of cost estimation and analysis responsibilities
manifests.applies_to: Director of Cost Assessment and Program Evaluation, Office of the Secretary of Defense
manifests.conditions: Effective September 30, 2017
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Review and revise any DFARS requirements for cost estimation, analysis, or review related to 'major automated information system programs' to conform with the narrowed statutory scope.
open_questions: Is there existing DFARS or DoD guidance that references MAIS specifically for cost estimation and needs harmonizing?
What process will be used to determine which programs previously classified as MAIS now require cost estimation under MDAP rules?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2302 note.>> CONTRACT CLOSEOUT AUTHORITY.

manifests.description: Authorizes the Secretary of Defense to close out certain very old contracts (entered before FY2000, with no deliverables due, and unreconcilable due to lost/destroyed records or cost-prohibitive reconciliation) without completing a reconciliation audit or other corrective action. Enables closeout by contract modification.
manifests.subject: Contract closeout authority for unreconcilable old contracts
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts entered into before fiscal year 2000 that are unreconcilable
manifests.conditions: Contract must have no remaining deliverables and be unreconcilable due to records being lost/destroyed or reconciliation cost being disproportionate
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add a special authority in the DFARS for contract closeout addressing contracts meeting these criteria, explicitly permitting closeout by administrative modification without reconciliation audit or corrective action.
open_questions: How to document the criteria used for determining contracts are not reconcilable?
What is the minimum required documentation prior to closeout?

manifests.description: Permits remaining balances in contract line items to be offset against one another within a contract, regardless of appropriation year, type, or status (including closed appropriations), to facilitate closeout as authorized.
manifests.subject: Offsets among contract line items for closeout
manifests.applies_to: Contracts covered under this authority as determined by the Secretary of Defense
manifests.conditions: Only to accomplish closeout of such contracts
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Implement an explicit exception in DFARS allowing offsets among CLINs regardless of fiscal/accounting distinctions when closing contracts under this authority.
open_questions: Does this raise any appropriations law issues not explicitly addressed?

manifests.description: Permits remaining balances to be offset across separate contracts with the same contractor, regardless of appropriation year, type, or status (including closed appropriations), solely for the purpose of contract closeout under this authority.
manifests.subject: Offsets across contracts for closeout
manifests.applies_to: Contracts and contract groups covered under this authority as determined by the Secretary of Defense
manifests.conditions: Only to accomplish closeout of such contracts
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Specify in DFARS that for qualifying contract closeouts, DoD may net obligations and deobligations across multiple contracts with the same contractor.
open_questions: Are there administrative controls needed to track inter-contract offsets for audit?

manifests.description: Allows the Secretary of Defense to close out qualifying contracts or groups of contracts by negotiated settlement with the contractor, rather than requiring precise financial reconciliation.
manifests.subject: Negotiated settlement for contract closeout
manifests.applies_to: Contracts and contract groups covered under this authority
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Authorize negotiated settlements in closeout procedures in lieu of exact audits for these cases.
open_questions: Are there parameters for what constitutes a fair negotiated settlement under this authority?

manifests.description: Empowers the Secretary of Defense to waive any provision of acquisition law or regulation as needed to close out contracts under this authority, with a requirement to notify congressional defense committees within 10 days of such waiver (and to specify each law or regulation waived).
manifests.subject: Waiver authority for contract closeout
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense actions on contracts eligible under this authority
manifests.conditions: Waivers must be reported to Congress within 10 days of exercise
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Implement a clause permitting waivers at the Secretary level for contract closeout, with DFARS guidance on documentation and notification to Congress.
open_questions: What level of detail is required in the congressional notification?
What is the process if Congress objects?

manifests.description: Directly authorizes the payment and accounting offices to adjust and close any open finance and accounting records related to contracts closed out under this authority.
manifests.subject: Adjustment and closure of contract-related records
manifests.applies_to: Payment and accounting offices processing contracts closed out under this authority
manifests.conditions: After contract is closed out using this authority
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS to allow accounting offices to make necessary record changes as part of closeout procedure.
open_questions: What documentation is required for such record adjustments?

manifests.description: Provides that no liability shall attach to any accounting, certifying, or payment official, or any contracting officer, for adjustments or closeouts executed under this authority.
manifests.subject: Liability protection for officials closing contracts under this authority
manifests.applies_to: Accounting, certifying, or payment officials, or contracting officers acting under this authority
manifests.conditions: Applies to adjustments or closeouts made pursuant to this authority
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS should reference liability immunity for officials acting in good faith under this authority.
open_questions: Does this bar only administrative liability, or also criminal/civil penalties if good faith is absent?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to prescribe regulations to carry out the authorities provided in this section.
manifests.subject: Regulation for administration of contract closeout authority
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS should be amended to conform to Secretary-issued regulations for this temporary authority.
open_questions: Will the regulations be implemented in DFARS directly or as a separate DoD issuance?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2762 note.>> REQUIREMENT TO USE FIRM FIXED-PRICE CONTRACTS FOR FOREIGN MILITARY SALES.

manifests.description: Requires the Department of Defense, within 180 days of enactment, to establish regulations mandating that firm fixed-price contracts (FFP) are to be used as the default contract type for most Foreign Military Sales (FMS) contracts. The intent is to promote predictability and limit cost risk for the U.S. government/foreign partners in FMS transactions.
manifests.subject: Mandate firm fixed-price (FFP) contracts for FMS
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and all FMS contracts unless covered by an exception or waiver
manifests.conditions: Must be implemented through regulations within 180 days of enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Create a new prescription in DFARS Subpart 225.73 making FFP mandatory for FMS, with references to enumerated exceptions and waiver.
open_questions: Should exceptions be made for contract size/complexity or other thresholds?

manifests.description: Requires that the FFP-for-FMS regulation include specific exceptions: (1) if the foreign country provides a written preference for a different contract type, or (2) if the foreign country requests in writing to use a different contract type for a specific FMS sale. These exceptions allow flexibility in responding to partner nation contract-type preferences.
manifests.subject: Exception to FFP requirement for FMS on foreign partner written preference/request
manifests.applies_to: FMS contracts where the foreign partner makes a written request as described
manifests.conditions: Foreign country must provide written preference or written request for specific sale
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Enumerate these as allowable exceptions to the FFP requirement in new/updated DFARS section.
open_questions: Is further approval or documentation required to invoke these exceptions?
Is a standing written preference sufficient, or must exceptions be evaluated each time?

manifests.description: Authorizes the Secretary of Defense or his designee to waive the requirement for FFP contract type on FMS if, on a case-by-case basis, it is determined to be in the best interest of the United States and American taxpayers. This gives senior leadership the ability to approve alternative contract types in exceptional circumstances.
manifests.subject: Waiver authority for FFP requirement in FMS
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense or designee; affects FMS contracts
manifests.conditions: Written determination must show use of a different contract type is in best U.S. interest; must be on a case-by-case basis
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Create or amend DFARS section in 225.73 subpart to delegate/define waiver authority, required justification, and administrative procedures.
open_questions: What is the minimum required documentation for the waiver?
Must waivers be publicly disclosed or reported?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to establish a pilot program (sunsetting January 1, 2020) to reform and accelerate the contracting and pricing process for up to 10 full-rate production major weapon system FMS contracts. The pilot must: (A) determine price reasonableness based on actual DoD cost/pricing data for the same product, and (B) reduce the cost/pricing data submission requirements in accordance with 10 U.S.C. 2306a for these contracts. The goal is to test and evaluate streamlined pricing for major FMS sales.
manifests.subject: Pilot program to streamline FMS major weapon systems contracting
manifests.applies_to: No more than 10 FMS contracts for full-rate production of major weapon systems
manifests.conditions: Pilot program authority expires January 1, 2020
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add temporary DFARS prescription for the pilot program under 225.73 and/or 215.4 series, specifying alternative price reasonableness methods and reduced submission of cost/pricing data.
open_questions: How will the specific contracts be selected for pilot participation?
What types of cost/pricing data reductions are permissible under the pilot?

manifests.description: Terminates the authority to operate the pilot program established in Manifest 4 as of January 1, 2020; after this date, the pilot cannot be used.
manifests.subject: Sunset of FMS price streamlining pilot program
manifests.applies_to: Pilot program authority and related DFARS provisions
manifests.conditions: Pilot expires January 1, 2020
manifests.effective_date: January 1, 2020
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Include expiration date/sunset language in the implementing DFARS text; ensure related provisions are removed or inapplicable after that date.
open_questions: Are FMS contracts initiated under the pilot before expiration still covered by pilot authorities after Jan 1, 2020?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2306 note.>> PREFERENCE FOR FIXED-PRICE CONTRACTS.

manifests.description: Requires DFARS to be updated within 180 days to establish an explicit preference for fixed-price contracts, including fixed-price incentive fee contracts, when selecting contract type. This aims to make fixed-price contracts the default or preferred option, reducing cost and schedule risks for the government.
manifests.subject: Preference for fixed-price contracts
manifests.applies_to: All Department of Defense contracting officers making contract type determinations
manifests.conditions: None; DFARS revision must take place within 180 days of enactment
amends: DFARS (new policy requirement for preference in contract type determination)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS Subpart 216.1 (Selecting Contract Types); possible reference to 216.403 for fixed-price incentive contracts
open_questions: Does the preference for fixed-price contracts apply to all acquisitions, or will there be stated exceptions/waivers?
Should there be policies for documenting justification when a cost-type contract is chosen over a fixed-price type?

manifests.description: Mandates that contracting officers cannot enter into a cost-type contract above $50 million (for contracts signed between October 1, 2018, and September 30, 2019) or above $25 million (for contracts signed on or after October 1, 2019), unless approved by a senior acquisition official (service acquisition executive, agency head, combatant command commander, or USD for AT&L). This is to limit and oversee large cost-type contracts and encourage the use of fixed-price contracts.
manifests.subject: Senior approval required for large cost-type contracts
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers seeking to enter into cost-type contracts above stated thresholds
manifests.conditions: Threshold is $50M for contracts entered into on or after Oct 1, 2018 and before Oct 1, 2019; $25M for contracts entered into on or after Oct 1, 2019
amends: DFARS (new approval requirement for cost-type contracts above thresholds)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS Subpart 216.3 (Cost-Reimbursement Contracts); cross-reference in 216.1 and 216.104 for contract type selection and approval procedures
open_questions: What process or documentation is required for approval by senior officials?
Are there exceptions for urgent or classified programs, or is this absolute for all contracts above thresholds?

sections.section_heading: TREATMENT OF INDEPENDENT RESEARCH AND DEVELOPMENT COSTS ON CERTAIN CONTRACTS.

manifests.description: Completely revises the treatment of independent research and development (IR&D) costs under 10 U.S.C. 2372. DoD regulations must ensure IR&D costs are allowable, reasonable indirect expenses, require separate reporting from other indirect costs, and may only impose certain controls or reporting obligations on contractors. Regulations cannot limit contractor independence to choose IR&D projects as long as the CEO deems these support DoD's advanced technology needs.
manifests.subject: Treatment of IR&D costs as allowable indirect costs and regulatory limitations
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and all defense contractors incurring IR&D costs
manifests.conditions: Applies to indirect costs incurred on or after October 1, 2017
manifests.effective_date: October 1, 2017
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 242.771 to match revised statutory language including mandatory allowability, reporting, and contractor independence requirements.
open_questions: How must a contractor's CEO determination be documented or validated for regulatory compliance?
Is further regulatory definition needed for 'advance the needs' of the Department under the new law?

manifests.description: Creates a new section, 10 U.S.C. 2372a, establishing specific rules for bid and proposal (B&P) costs on covered contracts. B&P costs are to be reported separately, are allowable as indirect expenses if reasonable and otherwise allowable, and an annual cap is set targeting total reimbursable B&P costs at not more than 1% of aggregate DoD industry sales. If the cap is exceeded, requires DoD to establish an expert panel to examine and recommend changes to laws, regulations, and practices contributing to high B&P costs.
manifests.subject: Treatment and limitation of bid and proposal (B&P) costs
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and contractors incurring B&P costs on covered contracts
manifests.conditions: Applies to indirect costs incurred on or after October 1, 2017; panel required if annual B&P costs exceed 1% target
manifests.effective_date: October 1, 2017
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Create new DFARS section paralleling 242.771/231.205 for B&P costs; implement reporting, allowability, and cost limitation goal, with provisions for triggering expert panel review.
open_questions: What is the exact process for DoD to calculate and enforce the 1% B&P costs goal and track industry sales numbers?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to contract with an independent entity to study laws, regulations, and practices related to contractor bid and proposal (B&P) expenses, and to report findings and recommendations for cost reductions to DoD and Congress within 180 days of contract award.
manifests.subject: Independent study and report on drivers of B&P costs
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense (for commissioning study); report applies to DoD and congressional defense committees
manifests.conditions: Report due 180 days after receipt of contract by study entity
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Reference forthcoming study under B&P policy; no immediate regulatory codification required.
open_questions: How will the findings of the independent study be disseminated to DoD contracting officials and acquisition workforce?

manifests.description: Modifies the required contents of the Defense Contract Audit Agency (DCAA) annual report to the congressional defense committees. Beginning FY 2019, the report must include annual summaries by dollar amount and percentage of: (1) indirect costs for contractors' IR&D and (2) indirect costs for contractors' B&P costs.
manifests.subject: DCAA annual reporting on IR&D and B&P indirect costs
manifests.applies_to: Defense Contract Audit Agency (DCAA) annual reporting requirements
manifests.conditions: Applies to annual reports for fiscal years beginning October 1, 2018
manifests.effective_date: October 1, 2018
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 242.771-3 and/or 232.006-5 to reflect new DCAA indirect cost reporting requirements.
open_questions: Are definitions for reportable IR&D and B&P indirect costs standardized for consistent reporting?
Will DCAA coordinate with DCMA and OUSD(AT&L) to ensure proper implementation and data collection?

sections.section_heading: ENHANCED COMPETITION REQUIREMENTS.

manifests.description: This change requires that certified cost or pricing data must be obtained for a prime contract entered into using procedures other than sealed-bid if that contract is only expected to receive one bid. Previously, the requirement did not expressly account for cases where only one bid was anticipated under non-sealed-bid procedures, potentially allowing some single-offer awards to avoid submitting this data. The amendment ensures cost or pricing data transparency in cases without meaningful competition.
manifests.subject: Certified cost/pricing data requirement for single-bid competitive procurements
manifests.applies_to: Offerors for prime contracts awarded with procedures other than sealed-bid where only one bid is expected
manifests.conditions: Relevant only when contract is to be awarded via other-than-sealed-bid procedures and is expected to receive just one bid
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS coverage for 215.403-1 and related procedures to require certified cost or pricing data whenever only one offer is anticipated, even in competitive acquisitions.

manifests.description: The standard for an exception from the requirement to submit certified cost or pricing data is tightened. Where previously the exception could apply when 'price competition' was present, now the exception is only allowed where there is competition resulting in at least two or more responsive and viable competing bids. This ensures that exceptions are based on actual, substantial competition, not just the competitive process.
manifests.subject: Narrowing competition exception for cost/pricing data
manifests.applies_to: Contracts, subcontracts, or modifications thereof where pricing was previously exempt from cost or pricing data submission based on competition
manifests.conditions: Exception now applies only when at least two or more responsive and viable bids are actually received
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Clarify in DFARS 215.403-1 and related guidance that the competition exception applies only with at least two responsive and viable offers received.
open_questions: Is further DFARS or PGI guidance needed to define 'viable' or document how offer viability is determined?

manifests.description: The law explicitly makes prime contractors responsible for determining whether a subcontract under a prime contract qualifies for an exception from the requirement to submit certified cost or pricing data. This change clarifies the flow-down authority and accountability, ensuring prime contractors make the determination rather than the government or lower-tier subcontractors, potentially reducing ambiguity in the acquisition process.
manifests.subject: Prime contractor responsibility for subcontract cost/pricing data exception determinations
manifests.applies_to: Prime contractors required to submit certified cost or pricing data for a prime contract
manifests.conditions: Applies when a prime contractor is required to submit certified data for the prime contract and must consider whether any subcontracts are excepted
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add to DFARS/PGI, and flow-down clauses, that prime contractors are responsible for making and documenting exception determinations for subcontracts regarding cost/pricing data.
open_questions: What level of documentation is required for these determinations? Is government notification or approval ever required, and how will this be audited?

sections.section_heading: INCREASED MICRO-PURCHASE THRESHOLD APPLICABLE TO DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE PROCUREMENTS.

manifests.description: Creates a new statutory section establishing a $5,000 micro-purchase threshold specifically for Department of Defense procurements. This overrides the general federal micro-purchase threshold set forth in 41 U.S.C. 1902(a) for DoD, streamlining the process for small purchases by military organizations.
manifests.subject: Department of Defense micro-purchase threshold set at $5,000
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense purchases subject to the micro-purchase threshold
manifests.conditions: Overrides subsection (a) of 41 U.S.C. 1902; applies only to DoD
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update references to the micro-purchase threshold for DoD in DFARS 213.2, 213.201, 213.306, and related sections to reflect $5,000.
open_questions: If the general FAR threshold changes in the future, should DFARS continue to explicitly reference $5,000 for DoD, or cross-reference the U.S. Code instead?
Should contingency or emergency operation exceptions for higher micro-purchase thresholds in FAR/DFARS be clarified for DoD under this new section?

manifests.description: Adds a new item '2338. Micro-purchase threshold.' to the table of sections at the start of Chapter 137 of title 10, ensuring the organizational structure of the code reflects the new section.
manifests.subject: Table of sections clerical update
manifests.applies_to: Table of sections for Chapter 137 of title 10, United States Code

sections.section_heading: COMPLIANCE WITH DOMESTIC SOURCE REQUIREMENTS FOR FOOTWEAR FURNISHED TO ENLISTED MEMBERS OF THE ARMED FORCES UPON THEIR INITIAL ENTRY INTO THE ARMED FORCES.

manifests.description: Requires the Department of Defense to provide required athletic footwear directly to recruits entering the Army, Navy, Air Force, or Marine Corps upon initial entry, rather than giving them a cash allowance to buy their own shoes. The purpose is to ensure control and compliance over sourcing and quality, rather than allowing decentralized individual purchase.
manifests.subject: Direct provision of athletic footwear to recruits
manifests.applies_to: Recruits entering the Army, Navy, Air Force, or Marine Corps upon initial entry
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 225.7002 or create a related instruction to require in-kind provision for initial-issue athletic footwear, not cash allowance.
open_questions: How are logistical and size/fit concerns for direct-issue athletic footwear to be managed?
How does this align with service-specific clothing allowance practices for other uniform items?

manifests.description: Mandates that all athletic footwear procured for these recruits must comply with the Berry Amendment's domestic source requirements, even for purchases below the simplified acquisition threshold or those otherwise exempt by law. The intent is to ensure all such shoes are made in the United States.
manifests.subject: Berry Amendment compliance for recruit athletic footwear
manifests.applies_to: Athletic footwear procured for new Army, Navy, Air Force, or Marine Corps recruits
manifests.conditions: Regardless of simplified acquisition threshold or other law
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Clarify or strengthen DFARS 225.7002 to explicitly include all initial-issue athletic footwear for recruits, regardless of dollar threshold.
open_questions: Is a stand-alone DFARS provision needed, or should 225.7002 be amended?
How should compliance be documented, especially for small purchases?

manifests.description: For two years following enactment (December 23, 2016), requires the Department of Defense to procure a variety of additional athletic footwear models to provide recruits with enough choices to minimize athletic injuries and reduce unnecessary harm during initial training.
manifests.subject: Increased variety of athletic footwear for recruits during transition period
manifests.applies_to: Army, Navy, Air Force, or Marine Corps recruits during initial entry
manifests.conditions: Temporary provision applies for 2 years after December 23, 2016
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Issue temporary DFARS/PGI guidance or a procurement policy memo requiring a range of Berry-compliant athletic footwear offerings for initial entry recruits during the two-year period.
open_questions: What standards or criteria satisfy “sufficient choices” to minimize injury?
Do existing contracts count if they already offer a variety, or are new ones required?

manifests.description: Allows an exception so the Department of Defense may provide a cash allowance for athletic footwear if a recruit has a unique medical requirement that cannot be met by available Berry-compliant shoes. This ensures medical needs are prioritized when appropriate footwear is unavailable.
manifests.subject: Medical exception for cash allowance
manifests.applies_to: Recruits with unique physiological needs requiring non-standard footwear
manifests.conditions: Only if footwear is medically required and cannot be met by Berry-compliant shoes
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add DFARS or PGI guidance outlining the approval and documentation process for this medical exception.
open_questions: What is the process and required documentation for determining ‘unique physiological needs’?
Who certifies medical necessity and authorizes the cash allowance?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2302 note.>> PROCUREMENT OF PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT.

manifests.description: Prohibits the Department of Defense from using reverse auctions or lowest price technically acceptable (LPTA) contracting methods for procuring personal protective equipment if the failure or poor quality of the item could result in combat casualties. This is intended to ensure that acquisition methods do not compromise the protection or safety of service members.
manifests.subject: Ban on reverse auction/LPTA methods for PPE
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers and acquisition officials
manifests.conditions: Applies to procurement of personal protective equipment where quality level or failure could result in combat casualties.
manifests.effective_date: Not later than 90 days after enactment of the Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add new language or section in DFARS, potentially in Part 213 or Part 215, prohibiting reverse auctions and LPTA for qualifying personal protective equipment.
open_questions: What specific standards or processes will be used to determine when the PPE qualifies as being high enough risk that this section applies?
Are any waivers or exceptions to be allowed, and if so, by what authority?

manifests.description: Establishes a preference for using 'best value' contracting methods for the procurement of personal protective equipment. The goal is to ensure quality and safety are prioritized in these acquisitions.
manifests.subject: Preference for best value contracting methods for PPE
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers and acquisition officials
manifests.conditions: Applies to procurement of personal protective equipment.
manifests.effective_date: Not later than 90 days after enactment of the Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add a preference for best value procurement for personal protective equipment, likely in DFARS Part 215 or a new dedicated subpart.
open_questions: Does this preference allow for exceptions based on mission requirements or cost issues?
How is 'preference' defined—must contracting officers justify not using best value, or is this guidance only?

manifests.description: Repeals section 884 of the FY16 NDAA, which previously required DoD to use technical qualifications as the predominant source selection criteria for PPE and critical safety items when failures could result in severe harm or death. The intent is to remove possibly conflicting or redundant requirements and ensure regulation aligns with the new standards.
manifests.subject: Repeal of prior PPE procurement requirements
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense (as pertains to PPE and critical safety item procurement regulations)
manifests.conditions: None
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment of this Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Rescind DFARS text implementing FY16 NDAA sec. 884 as it relates to PPE, and ensure consistency with new requirements.
open_questions: Does this deletion impact requirements for other 'critical safety items' besides PPE? Should any provisions regarding those items remain?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2305 note.>> USE OF LOWEST PRICE TECHNICALLY ACCEPTABLE SOURCE SELECTION PROCESS.

manifests.description: Establishes a new Department of Defense policy to avoid using 'lowest price technically acceptable' (LPTA) source selection criteria in contract award situations where using LPTA would prevent the department from gaining the potential benefits of cost and technical trade-offs.
manifests.subject: Department of Defense LPTA policy
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense acquisition officials
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment of this Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Should be codified as a policy statement at the beginning of DFARS Subpart 215.3 Source Selection.

manifests.description: Requires revision of the DFARS to only allow LPTA source selection criteria for solicitations issued 120 days after enactment (and after) when all of the following are true: (1) Clear definition of minimum performance requirements in measurable terms; (2) little to no value is gained from exceeding the minimum requirements; (3) technical proposals require minimal subjective judgment to evaluate; (4) the source selection authority is confident no higher value would emerge from higher-priced proposals; (5) a justification for LPTA is documented in the contract file; (6) lowest price reflects full life-cycle costs.
manifests.subject: LPTA usage restrictions and required conditions
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers and source selection authorities
manifests.conditions: For solicitations issued on or after 120 days after enactment
manifests.effective_date: 120 days after the date of enactment of this Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Incorporate as mandatory conditions for use of LPTA in DFARS 215.3, with contract file justification requirement.
open_questions: How should contracting officers assess and document life-cycle costs and the subjective value of higher offers?

manifests.description: Mandates that DOD avoid, to the maximum extent practicable, using LPTA source selection criteria for procurements that primarily involve: information technology or cybersecurity services, systems engineering/technical assistance, advanced electronic testing, audit/audit readiness, and similar knowledge-based professional services; personal protective equipment; or knowledge-based training or logistics for contingency or overseas operations, including Afghanistan or Iraq.
manifests.subject: Avoidance of LPTA in specific procurement types
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense acquisition and contracting officials
manifests.conditions: To the maximum extent practicable; applies to specified service and product categories
manifests.effective_date: Date of enactment of this Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add explicit restrictions within DFARS 215.3 against the use of LPTA in the specified categories unless justified per subsection (b).

manifests.description: Establishes a requirement for the Comptroller General to submit annual reports (beginning December 1, 2017, and annually for three years) to the congressional defense committees on the number of contracts over $10 million awarded using LPTA criteria, including explanations of how the six conditions in subsection (b) were considered.
manifests.subject: Annual reporting on LPTA use over $10 million
manifests.applies_to: Comptroller General of the United States; indirectly affects DOD contract reporting practices
manifests.conditions: Contracts exceeding $10,000,000
manifests.effective_date: December 1, 2017 (for three years)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS should refer to reporting requirement and may need to specify recordkeeping to support GAO review.
open_questions: What systems or processes will DOD use to track LPTA usage and support Comptroller General reporting?

sections.section_heading: AMENDMENTS RELATING TO INVENTORY AND TRACKING OF PURCHASES OF SERVICES.

manifests.description: Raises the data collection threshold for Department of Defense service purchases from those above the simplified acquisition threshold to only those exceeding $3,000,000. This narrows reporting obligations, focusing oversight on higher-value contracts.
manifests.subject: Threshold for service acquisition data collection
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense components acquiring services
manifests.conditions: Purchases of services in excess of $3,000,000
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS and PGI thresholds that refer to inventory and tracking of service acquisitions, ensuring $3 million aligns across policy.

manifests.description: Narrows the scope of service acquisitions tracked to four named 'service acquisition portfolio groups': logistics management services, equipment related services, knowledge-based services, and electronics and communications services. This focuses tracking and oversight on activities within these categories.
manifests.subject: Scope of services tracked
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense components acquiring services
manifests.conditions: Applicability tied to the defined service acquisition portfolio groups
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS and PGI should specify service portfolio groups for application of reporting obligations, citing DoDI 5000.74.
open_questions: Are services outside the four named portfolio groups excluded from all reporting, or tracked elsewhere?

manifests.description: Changes the requirement from submitting an annual inventory of all service activities to Congress to preparing an annual inventory and submitting only a summary of activities performed under staff augmentation contracts in the prior fiscal year. This streamlines congressional reporting and narrows its focus.
manifests.subject: Inventory reporting process
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense
manifests.conditions: Activities performed pursuant to staff augmentation contracts during the prior fiscal year
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update instructions to require only a summary to Congress, and limit coverage to staff augmentation contracts.
open_questions: Should the internal inventory itself (not the summary) include non-staff augmentation services?

manifests.description: Eliminates requirements for public posting of inventories, certain Inspector General and Comptroller General reporting, and possibly other obsolete compliance mechanisms by striking subsections (d), (g), and (h). This simplifies reporting and administration.
manifests.subject: Repeal of obsolete compliance/reporting subsections
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove DFARS/PGI references to repealed inventory public posting/IG/GAO reporting obligations.
open_questions: Do any necessary notifications or public postings persist in other statutes or are they entirely repealed?

manifests.description: Adds a requirement that annual reviews of contract inventories pay particular attention to three high-risk product service codes: (A) special studies or analysis (not R&D), (B) information technology and telecommunications, and (C) support (professional, administrative, management). This is to enhance oversight in areas vulnerable to improper service contracting.
manifests.subject: Emphasis on high-risk service code review
manifests.applies_to: Secretaries of the military departments and heads of Defense Agencies
manifests.conditions: When reviewing contract inventories per 10 U.S.C. 2330a(d)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS/PGI guidance for service contract inventory review to include special focus on the named PSC/FSC groups.
open_questions: How are these 'high-risk' categories mapped to existing DoD or DFARS codes?

manifests.description: Requires the Comptroller General to submit a report to the congressional defense committees by March 31, 2018, assessing the Department of Defense’s implementation of service acquisition data collection and inventory review efforts. This adds external oversight of the statutory requirements’ implementation.
manifests.subject: Comptroller General report on implementation
manifests.applies_to: Comptroller General of the United States
manifests.conditions: Deadline March 31, 2018
manifests.effective_date: March 31, 2018 (report due)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: No DFARS change required—DFARS offices may provide data/support to GAO if requested.

manifests.description: Adds clear statutory definitions for 'service acquisition portfolio groups' (by reference to DODI 5000.74) and 'staff augmentation contracts' (services contracts for DoD workspace, including personal services contracts). This clarifies interpretation for future applications.
manifests.subject: Definitions for service acquisition portfolio groups and staff augmentation contracts
manifests.applies_to: Interpretation of 10 U.S.C. 2330a
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure these definitions are reflected or referenced in DFARS/PGI implementations and in training.
open_questions: Does this definition of staff augmentation conflict with or fit other definitions in FAR/DFARS?

sections.section_heading: INCREASED MICRO-PURCHASE THRESHOLD FOR RESEARCH PROGRAMS AND ENTITIES.

manifests.description: Establishes a new statutory provision making the micro-purchase threshold $10,000 for basic research programs and activities at Department of Defense science and technology reinvention laboratories. This allows DoD labs and their research programs to make micro-purchases up to $10,000, notwithstanding the usual lower limit that applies to most other government purchases.
manifests.subject: Higher micro-purchase threshold for DoD research labs
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense basic research programs and science and technology reinvention laboratories
manifests.conditions: Applies to basic research programs and qualifying laboratory activities under the Department of Defense
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 213 and associated guidance to specify a $10,000 micro-purchase threshold for DoD basic research programs and science/technology reinvention labs. Include cross-reference to 10 U.S.C. 2339.
open_questions: Is there a DoD-official list or definition of 'science and technology reinvention laboratories' for contracting staff?

manifests.description: Raises the micro-purchase threshold in 41 U.S.C. 1902 for procurement activities carried out by higher education institutions, related nonprofit entities, nonprofit research organizations, and independent research institutes to $10,000. Additionally, it allows the head of an executive agency to set a higher threshold if justified by audit results, risk assessments, or state law. This change is intended to streamline purchases for research entities and reduce administrative burden.
manifests.subject: Higher micro-purchase threshold for universities and research organizations
manifests.applies_to: Universities, affiliated nonprofit entities, nonprofit research organizations, independent research institutes with procurement activities under 31 U.S.C. 6303-6305
manifests.conditions: Procurement activities as defined, and subject to audit, risk assessment, or state law if exceeding $10,000
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise DFARS 213.201 and related grant/cooperative agreement procedures to allow for this higher threshold, with instructions for applying the agency head override criteria.
open_questions: What internal documentation or procedures are required when an agency head authorizes a threshold higher than $10,000?

manifests.description: Revises 41 U.S.C. 1902(d) and 1902(e) by replacing the specific dollar amount ('$3,000') with a reference to the 'micro-purchase threshold.' This ensures the law always reflects the current statutory threshold, even if changed in the future.
manifests.subject: Technical update to match threshold terminology
manifests.applies_to: All executive agency micro-purchases
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Replace explicit $3,000 references with 'micro-purchase threshold' in DFARS provisions on competitive quotation and equitable supplier distribution for micro-purchases.

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2304 note.>> COMPETITION FOR RELIGIOUS SERVICES CONTRACTS.

manifests.description: The Department of Defense is newly prohibited from preventing non-profit organizations from competing for contracts for religious-related services on U.S. military installations. This change is intended to ensure that non-profits are not categorically excluded from these opportunities and to promote open and fair competition among potential service providers.
manifests.subject: Competition for Religious Services Contracts by Non-Profits
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting for religious-related services on U.S. military installations
manifests.conditions: Applies to all competitions for contracts for religious-related services on military installations; affects non-profit organizations seeking to compete
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS parts on competition (DFARS 215), eligibility (DFARS 219), and any sections governing contracting for religious services should be updated to clearly state that non-profit organizations may not be precluded from competing for these contracts.
open_questions: What is the intended definition of a 'non-profit organization' for these competitions (e.g., IRS 501(c)(3) status or another standard)?
Are there current policies or DFARS subparts that specifically or indirectly preclude non-profits from competing that must be revised?
Does this requirement apply only to prime contracts, or should it also extend to subcontracts for religious-related services?

sections.section_heading: AMENDMENTS CONCERNING DETECTION AND AVOIDANCE OF COUNTERFEIT ELECTRONIC PARTS.

manifests.description: Narrows the contractor's responsibility to explicitly refer to 'counterfeit electronic parts' rather than just 'counterfeit parts,' ensuring policy and requirements are focused specifically on electronic components.
manifests.subject: Scope of counterfeit part requirements
manifests.applies_to: Covered defense contractors handling electronic parts
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise 246.870, 252.246-7007, and related DFARS text to specify 'counterfeit electronic parts' as the focus.
open_questions: Is there any remaining ambiguity in the regulatory or clause definitions of 'electronic part' that needs to be resolved for consistency?

manifests.description: Makes allowance for reimbursement of costs associated with counterfeit electronic parts or suspect counterfeit electronic parts only if the parts are 'covered' and were obtained in accordance with official regulations on trusted sources for electronic parts, thereby encouraging compliance with proper sourcing protocols.
manifests.subject: Allowable costs for counterfeit parts events
manifests.applies_to: Covered defense contractors seeking reimbursement under DoD contracts
manifests.conditions: Costs are only allowable if the parts are covered and were obtained in accordance with relevant sourcing regulations.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 231.205-71 and ensure coordination with DFARS 252.246-7008 on sources of electronic parts.
open_questions: Is there a clear working definition or accessible listing of 'covered' parts for contractor use?
Are there standardized procedures for contractors to document and prove compliance with the sourcing regulations?

manifests.description: Clarifies that contractors only gain the cost allowability exception if they 'discover' (not just possess) counterfeit or suspect counterfeit electronic parts and provide notification, reinforcing that contractors must have active systems to detect and report such parts.
manifests.subject: Requirement for contractor discovery in reporting and cost allowability
manifests.applies_to: Covered defense contractors providing cost submissions on relevant contracts
manifests.conditions: Allowability/relief only applies if the contractor discovers and reports counterfeit parts.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Clarify in DFARS 231.205-71 and associated guidance that discovery (not solely possession) triggers reporting and related allowability.
open_questions: How should 'discovery' be defined or proven for regulatory and enforcement purposes? Does this align with GIDEP and DFARS notification deadlines?

manifests.description: Requires that the selection of additional trusted suppliers (beyond the original manufacturer or authorized dealers) by contractors is subject to not only review and audit, but also explicit approval by designated DoD officials, increasing government oversight of the trusted supplier approval process.
manifests.subject: DoD approval of additional trusted suppliers
manifests.applies_to: Contractors and subcontractors selecting trusted electronic part suppliers
manifests.conditions: Applicable when not sourcing from original manufacturer or authorized dealer
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 246.870-2 and relevant guidance to require formal DoD approval of additional trusted suppliers.
open_questions: What is the process and timeline for obtaining DoD approval, and does this extend to previously approved suppliers or only new selections?

sections.section_heading: REPORTING RELATED TO FAILURE OF CONTRACTORS TO MEET GOALS UNDER NEGOTIATED COMPREHENSIVE SMALL BUSINESS SUBCONTRACTING PLANS.

manifests.description: Removes the existing penalty that made contractors ineligible to negotiate a new comprehensive small business subcontracting plan if they failed to meet prior year goals, and instead requires the Secretary of Defense to report to Congress on any negotiated comprehensive subcontracting plan that did not meet its goals for the prior fiscal year. The purpose is to shift from a direct penalty to increased Congressional oversight and transparency.
manifests.subject: Reporting to Congress on failures to meet small business subcontracting goals
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense and contractors with negotiated comprehensive small business subcontracting plans
manifests.conditions: When the Secretary determines a contractor did not meet negotiated subcontracting goals in the prior fiscal year
amends: 15 U.S.C. 637 note (Section 834(d)(2) of the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Years 1990 and 1991)
source_authorities: Section 821(d)(2) of the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2015
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Consider new or amended DFARS coverage (possibly in 219.7 or a related reporting subpart) to implement this requirement for DoD acquisition offices to report contractor failures to Congress.
open_questions: Should DFARS specify the process, format, and deadline for submitting the required report to Congress?
Are there to be any remaining consequences for contractors who fail to meet goals, or is reporting now the only required action?

sections.section_heading: AMENDMENT TO MENTOR-PROTEGE PROGRAM.

manifests.description: Broadens the objectives of the DoD Mentor-Protégé Program so that, in addition to helping disadvantaged small businesses perform as subcontractors, the program also seeks to increase their participation as subcontractors and suppliers on all Department of Defense, other federal, and commercial contracts. This expands the purpose and potential benefit of the program.
manifests.subject: Expanded Program Goals
manifests.applies_to: Mentor-Protégé Program participants
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment of NDAA FY16 for new agreements
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 219.7101 and Appendix I to reflect expanded program goals.

manifests.description: Limits a protege concern to only one concurrent mentor-protege agreement and restricts the total period for entering into such agreements to five years from the date of the first agreement. This is intended to limit indefinite program participation by a single firm.
manifests.subject: Limitations on Protégé Agreements
manifests.applies_to: Protégé firms and agreements
manifests.conditions: Applies to new agreements entered after date of enactment
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment of NDAA FY16 for new agreements
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise DFARS Appendix I (I-104, I-105) to include 5-year cap and bar on concurrent agreements.
open_questions: Are pre-existing agreements grandfathered, or only new agreements affected?

manifests.description: Adds more stringent requirements for mentor firms: they cannot be affiliated with their protégés prior to the program, must prove qualifications to provide valuable developmental assistance, must be financially healthy and not debarred, and must show applicable defense contracting or business experience.
manifests.subject: Mentor Eligibility and Value
manifests.applies_to: Prospective mentor firms
manifests.conditions: For agreements entered after enactment
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment of NDAA FY16 for new agreements
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Tighten DFARS Appendix I requirements and screening for mentors.
open_questions: What process or documentation will be used to demonstrate lack of affiliation or adequate experience/value?

manifests.description: Specifies that mentor-protégé agreements must include detailed developmental plans with measurable progress factors, descriptions of benefits to DoD, and goals for the protégé to win awards outside the program. This creates stronger accountability for actual developmental outcomes.
manifests.subject: Developmental Plan Requirements
manifests.applies_to: All mentor-protégé agreements
manifests.conditions: For agreements entered after enactment
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment of NDAA FY16 for new agreements
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS Appendix I to require these plan elements in all new agreements.
open_questions: What constitutes adequate evidence for projected benefits or measurable goals?

manifests.description: Removes business development expenses from reimbursable costs for mentors and clarifies that DoD will not reimburse mentor fees for services to protégés as described, or such expenses incurred while in a joint venture with the protégé.
manifests.subject: Prohibited and Limited Mentor Reimbursements
manifests.applies_to: Mentor firms seeking reimbursement
manifests.conditions: As specified for new and existing agreements as relevant
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment of NDAA FY16 for new agreements
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update reimbursement policies and guidance in Appendix I to ensure compliance.
open_questions: Are any clarifications needed regarding which joint venture costs are non-reimbursable?

manifests.description: Updates and clarifies cross-references to the Small Business Act and related statutory language throughout the program's governing statute for added clarity.
manifests.subject: Statutory Reference Conformance
manifests.applies_to: Relevant provisions in mentor-protégé program statute and implementing regulation
manifests.effective_date: Immediate upon enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Conform references in DFARS to latest statutory citations.

manifests.description: Extends sunset dates for entering into new mentor-protégé agreements (to September 30, 2018) and for the program's end date (to September 30, 2021), allowing continued participation and administrative wind-down.
manifests.subject: Extension of Program and Sunset
manifests.applies_to: Mentor-Protégé Program administrators and participants
manifests.effective_date: As of amended deadlines
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update expiration/sunset references in DFARS Appendix I to align with new dates.

manifests.description: Requires that mentor firms submit an annual report to the Secretary of Defense detailing the technical/management assistance, subcontracts, payments, joint ventures, loans, and qualitative results of their support to the protégé firm. This report ensures oversight and measurement of program outcomes.
manifests.subject: Annual Mentor Reporting Requirement
manifests.applies_to: Mentor firms with active mentor-protégé agreements
manifests.conditions: For all agreements (see retroactivity manifest)
manifests.effective_date: Applies to all agreements regardless of when entered
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add or amend DFARS Appendix I, section I-112, to clarify the specifics of annual mentor reporting.
open_questions: Standardized report template and process for submission?

manifests.description: Requires DoD's Office of Small Business Programs to review the annual mentor reports and, if the agreement does not further program purposes, to deny continuation of the agreement. This provides a clear continuation/termination process based on performance.
manifests.subject: DoD Review and Authority to Deny Continuation
manifests.applies_to: All mentor-protégé agreements
manifests.conditions: Based on review of annual reports
manifests.effective_date: Applies to all agreements regardless of when entered
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Appendix I should describe the process for DoD review and procedures for non-continuation where criteria are not met.
open_questions: What appeals/recourse does a terminated agreement have?

manifests.description: Expands the definition of eligible protégé firms to include nontraditional defense contractors and small businesses critical to the defense supplier base. Clarifies size, ownership, and other criteria, broadening the types of small businesses that can participate.
manifests.subject: Expanded and Clarified Protégé Eligibility
manifests.applies_to: Applicants for protégé status
manifests.conditions: Eligibility requirements as amended
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment of NDAA FY16 for new agreements
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 219.7102 and Appendix I eligibility sections.
open_questions: What evidence or documentation must an applicant provide to prove nontraditional or critical supplier status?

manifests.description: Establishes an explicit and detailed definition of 'affiliated' between mentor and protégé firms for program eligibility and compliance, delineating types of relationships and past joint behavior that will constitute affiliation.
manifests.subject: Definition of 'Affiliated'
manifests.applies_to: Mentor and protégé applicant firms and reviewers
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment for new agreements
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add explicit definition to DFARS 219.7102 or Appendix I; use to assess qualifications and eligibility.
open_questions: What is the process and evidence required to determine 'affiliated' status? Who within DoD/SBA will enforce this?

manifests.description: Specifies that the new reporting requirements on mentor firms and DoD review/denial authority apply to all agreements, including those entered into before the FY16 Act. This gives retroactive effect to procedural mandates.
manifests.subject: Retroactive Application of Reporting and Review
manifests.applies_to: All active and new mentor-protégé agreements
manifests.conditions: Regardless of when agreement was entered
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment for all existing and future agreements
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Explicitly state in Appendix I and communications that reporting and review changes apply retroactively.
open_questions: How will compliance/reporting be enforced for agreements predating this amendment (e.g., notice, grace periods)?

sections.section_heading: TREATMENT OF GOODS AND SERVICES PROVIDED BY NONTRADITIONAL DEFENSE CONTRACTORS AS COMMERCIAL ITEMS.
