manifests.description: Allows the head of a defense agency to treat goods and services provided by nontraditional defense contractors as commercial items for acquisition purposes, even if they do not meet the standard statutory definition. This reduces barriers and regulatory burdens for nontraditional defense contractors to participate in defense procurement.
manifests.subject: Commercial item treatment for nontraditional defense contractors
manifests.applies_to: Heads of defense agencies and nontraditional defense contractors
manifests.conditions: Applies to items and services provided by nontraditional defense contractors as defined in 10 U.S.C. 2302(9)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add new provision and procedures to DFARS 212 and/or 244 allowing contracting officers to classify supplies/services from nontraditional defense contractors as commercial items. Provide guidance for identification and documentation.
open_questions: Are there any substantive limits on what kinds of items or services this authority applies to, or is it intended as a broad grant?
Should contracting officers generally default to this treatment, or only apply it when it is especially advantageous for the acquisition?

manifests.description: Updates the table of sections for chapter 140 of title 10 to include the new section 2380A on commercial item treatment for nontraditional defense contractors.
manifests.subject: Clerical update to table of sections
manifests.applies_to: Table of sections for 10 U.S.C. chapter 140
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure DFARS references to statutory authorities in chapter 140 include the new section 2380A where relevant.

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2377 note.>> LIMITATION ON CONVERSION OF PROCUREMENTS FROM COMMERCIAL ACQUISITION PROCEDURES.

manifests.description: Requires that before converting a procurement of commercial items or services valued over $1,000,000 from commercial acquisition procedures (FAR Part 12) to noncommercial acquisition procedures (FAR Part 15), the contracting officer must provide a written determination. The officer must state that the earlier use of commercial acquisition was in error or based on inadequate information, and that converting will provide a cost savings to DoD compared to continued commercial procedures.
manifests.subject: Written determination for conversion from commercial to noncommercial procedures
manifests.applies_to: Contracting officers handling DoD procurements of commercial items or services valued above $1,000,000
manifests.conditions: Applies only to conversions from FAR Part 12 to FAR Part 15 procedures for contracts over $1,000,000
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment of the NDAA for FY2016
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add a new requirement in DFARS 212.102 for written determinations prior to conversion of procurements over $1 million from commercial to noncommercial procedures.
open_questions: To what extent does 'inadequate information' require additional definition or regulatory guidance for implementation?

manifests.description: For conversions valued over $100,000,000, requires that the determination by the contracting officer must be approved by the head of the contracting activity and a copy of this approval must be provided to the Office of the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics before contract award.
manifests.subject: Additional approval and notification requirement for large conversions
manifests.applies_to: Contracting officers and heads of contracting activity for DoD procurements exceeding $100,000,000
manifests.conditions: Procurements over $100 million being converted from commercial (FAR 12) to noncommercial (FAR 15) procedures
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment of the NDAA for FY2016
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Implement as procedural steps in DFARS 212.102, specifying approval thresholds and notification process for actions over $100 million.
open_questions: How are organizational changes to the 'head of the contracting activity' or the Office of the Under Secretary to be handled over time?

manifests.description: Mandates that in making the required written determination for conversion, the official must consider: estimated future R&D costs by the existing contractor, transaction costs for DoD and the contractor in supporting the conversion, changes in purchase quantities, and cost impacts of potential procurement delays from the conversion.
manifests.subject: Factors to be considered in conversion determination
manifests.applies_to: Contracting officers making determinations to convert a procurement from commercial to noncommercial procedures
manifests.conditions: Applies when preparing the required written determination under subsection (a)
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment of the NDAA for FY2016
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add these factors to DFARS 212.102 or related guidance as requirements for the determination documentation.
open_questions: Is there a minimum standard for the analysis of these factors, or is documentation of consideration alone sufficient?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense, within 180 days of enactment, to develop procedures to track all such conversions of contracts and subcontracts for improved analysis and reporting, and to update the DFARS to reflect the conversion requirements.
manifests.subject: Tracking and DFARS revision for conversions
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; DoD acquisition enterprise
manifests.conditions: Conversion of contracts and subcontracts as described in subsection (a)
manifests.effective_date: 180 days after enactment of the NDAA for FY2016
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Require a data collection mechanism and update DFARS 212.102 and relevant reporting/recordkeeping procedures.
open_questions: What are the minimum details required in the new tracking and reporting procedures?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense, within one year after enactment, to submit a report to the congressional defense committees on the implementation of the conversion requirements, including a list of contracts procured as described.
manifests.subject: One-year reporting requirement on conversions
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense; congressional defense committees (for receiving the report)
manifests.conditions: One year after enactment of the NDAA for FY2016
manifests.effective_date: One year after enactment of the NDAA for FY2016
open_questions: Is further regulatory implementation needed for this one-time reporting requirement, or is it a direct statutory obligation?

manifests.description: Provides that all requirements of this section will expire 5 years after the date of enactment unless renewed by law.
manifests.subject: Sunset of conversion requirements
manifests.applies_to: All requirements established by this NDAA section
manifests.conditions: Five years after enactment of the NDAA for FY2016
manifests.effective_date: Five years after enactment of the NDAA for FY2016
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS text implementing these provisions should include a sunset clause or automatic expiration notice.
open_questions: Should DFARS implementing text be automatically withdrawn/archived upon sunset, or should a formal rulemaking action be required to remove it?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2377 note.>> MARKET RESEARCH AND PREFERENCE FOR COMMERCIAL ITEMS.

manifests.description: Require the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics to issue guidance within 90 days to ensure all DoD acquisition officials comply fully with statutory requirements concerning market research and preference for commercial items under 10 U.S.C. 2377.
manifests.subject: Issuance of DOD-wide market research guidance
manifests.applies_to: Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics; DoD acquisition officials
manifests.conditions: Guidance must be issued within 90 days of enactment
manifests.effective_date: 90 days after enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS Part 210—add requirement for policy issuance and oversight
open_questions: What mechanisms will be used to enforce and measure compliance with this guidance beyond issuance?

manifests.description: Mandate that heads of agencies cannot award contracts above the simplified acquisition threshold for information technology products or services that are not commercial items unless there is a written determination that no commercial item is suitable for the need, citing 10 U.S.C. 2377(c)(2).
manifests.subject: Restriction on non-commercial IT contracts
manifests.applies_to: Heads of DoD agencies
manifests.conditions: Contracts for IT products/services over the simplified acquisition threshold; applies unless written commercial item unsuitability determination is made
manifests.effective_date: Upon issuance of required guidance
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 210 or 212—add explicit written determination requirement for IT above threshold
open_questions: What is the required format, approval chain, or central reporting mechanism for these written determinations?

manifests.description: Require that market research conducted in accordance with 10 U.S.C. 2377(c) be used, where appropriate, to inform determinations of price reasonableness for acquisitions.
manifests.subject: Market research use in price reasonableness determinations
manifests.applies_to: DoD procurement officials
manifests.conditions: Where appropriate (not all market research may be relevant for price reasonableness)
manifests.effective_date: Upon issuance of required guidance
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS Parts 210 and/or 215—cross-reference market research use in price reasonableness findings
open_questions: How is 'where appropriate' defined—will criteria for applicability be specified in future guidance or left to discretion?

manifests.description: Require the Chairman and Vice Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, in consultation with the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics, to review and revise, within 180 days, all requirements development policies (e.g., CJCSI 3170.01, JCIDS Manual) to ensure processes enforce market research as required by 10 U.S.C. 2377(c) and FAR 10.001.
manifests.subject: Revision of requirements development process to ensure market research
manifests.applies_to: Joint Chiefs of Staff (Chairman, Vice Chairman), USDoD requirements/capability process owners
manifests.conditions: Review and revision must be completed within 180 days of enactment
manifests.effective_date: 180 days after enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Reference compliance linkage in DFARS 210, or requirements/tailoring section, cross-referencing current Joint Staff requirements policy updates
open_questions: Will DFARS reference specific Joint Staff policy documents, or set a general compliance mandate?

manifests.description: For purposes of implementation, define 'market research' as a review of existing systems, subsystems, capabilities, and technologies available or potentially available to meet DoD needs. The minimum standard includes contacting knowledgeable individuals in Government and industry, and use of any techniques in FAR 10.002(b)(2).
manifests.subject: Definition of market research
manifests.applies_to: DoD procurement and requirements officials
manifests.conditions: Applies in implementing all above requirements under this section
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 210—add or clarify definition of 'market research' meeting this standard, including required contact with knowledgeable individuals
open_questions: Should this definition be used for all market research in DFARS, or limited to commercial item-related acquisitions?

sections.section_heading: USE OF RECENT PRICES PAID BY THE GOVERNMENT IN THE DETERMINATION OF PRICE REASONABLENESS.

manifests.description: Requires contracting officers to consider evidence presented by an offeror of recent prices paid by the Government for the same or similar commercial items when determining if a price is reasonable in a new purchase. The officer must assess whether prior prices are still valid references after evaluating all relevant factors, including how much time has passed since the previous procurement, differences in quantities, and any changes to terms or conditions.
manifests.subject: Consideration of recent Government prices for establishing price reasonableness
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers
manifests.conditions: Applies when an offeror provides evidence of recent prices paid by the Government for the same or similar commercial items and the officer deems those prices to be valid references in light of relevant factors.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Implement in DFARS parts addressing fair and reasonable price determination for commercial items, such as updating procedures in DFARS 215.404-1 and any DFARS commercial item pricing guidance to require explicit consideration of recent government purchase prices as evidence, along with analysis of time elapsed, quantities, and terms.
open_questions: Should additional regulatory guidance clarify what is considered a 'recent' price and what documentation offerors are expected to provide?
Should regulations specify the relative weight given to past prices versus other factors that may affect current price reasonableness?

sections.section_heading: MODIFICATION TO INFORMATION REQUIRED TO BE SUBMITTED BY OFFEROR IN PROCUREMENT OF MAJOR WEAPON SYSTEMS AS COMMERCIAL ITEMS.

manifests.description: Removes the requirement that the offeror must submit sufficient information to evaluate, through price analysis, the reasonableness of the price when buying a major weapon system as a commercial item. Now, only the Secretary of Defense's determination and notification to Congress are prerequisites for such treatment.
manifests.subject: Major Weapon System Commercial Item Determination Requirements
manifests.applies_to: Procurements of major weapon systems as commercial items
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Modify DFARS 234.7002(a) to remove the requirement for offeror price analysis information for major weapon systems treated as commercial items.
open_questions: Should all associated DFARS/PGI references to offeror price analysis submission be removed or narrowed to maintain consistency?

manifests.description: Changes the criteria for treating subsystems of major weapon systems as commercial items. A subsystem is eligible if either: (1) its parent system is or has been procured as a commercial item; or (2) the contracting officer determines in writing that the subsystem is commercial. Removes the requirement for information submission by the offeror supporting price reasonableness.
manifests.subject: Subsystem Commercial Item Criteria
manifests.applies_to: DoD procurement of subsystems of major weapon systems
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise DFARS 234.7002(b) to match the new eligibility tests and remove information submission requirements for subsystems.

manifests.description: Changes the process for treating components and spare parts of major weapon systems as commercial items for price analysis/exemption purposes. A component or spare part may be treated as a commercial item if: (a) its parent weapon system or subsystem is being or has been procured as a commercial item, or (b) the contracting officer determines in writing that it is commercial. Removes the previous requirement for the offeror to submit price analysis info.
manifests.subject: Component/Spare Part Commercial Item Criteria
manifests.applies_to: DoD procurement of components and spare parts for major weapon systems
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 234.7002 to reflect new criteria for treating components and spare parts as commercial items and remove information submission requirement.

manifests.description: Substitutes a new process for the information required from offerors to determine price reasonableness. Now, the contracting officer must sequentially request: (A) prior paid prices for same or similar items under comparable terms; (B) if (A) is inadequate, additional price info (e.g., different terms, similar effort, alternative solutions, other relevant info); (C) if still inadequate, other relevant cost info (labor, material, overhead). Forbids requiring (C) for COTS items and only allows it for privately developed items if the head of the contracting activity makes a written determination of insufficiency in the prior steps.
manifests.subject: Information Required from Offerors for Price Reasonableness
manifests.applies_to: Offerors in procurements of major weapon systems, subsystems, and components claimed as commercial items
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise DFARS 234.7002 to spell out this tiered, sequential approach and its limitations, with references to documentation and approval requirements for exceptional cases.
open_questions: Should DFARS specify additional procedures for documenting decisions to request cost data for privately developed items?

manifests.description: Requires that if price information for the same or similar items is unavailable or insufficient to determine reasonableness under the Truth in Negotiations Act, the contracting officer shall require other relevant pricing data (including work effort, alternative solutions, and other relevant info) to support a fair and reasonable price.
manifests.subject: Additional Information Required under Truth in Negotiations Act
manifests.applies_to: Offerors and contracting officers under 10 U.S.C. 2306a
manifests.conditions: When price data for same/similar items is insufficient or unavailable
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Amend DFARS part 215 (or the implementing section for 2306a) to require and enumerate the further pricing data sources when previous price data is insufficient.

sections.section_heading: PROCUREMENT OF COMMERCIAL ITEMS.

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to establish and maintain a centralized office or team with expertise and resources for overseeing commercial item determinations in Department of Defense procurements. This is intended to ensure consistency, knowledgeable decision-making, and oversight of commercial item status across the Department.
manifests.subject: Centralized commercial item determination oversight
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense (all procurements involving commercial item determinations)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add new DFARS subpart establishing centralized entity and laying out its roles and responsibilities.
open_questions: What is the expected structure or minimum qualifications for this centralized capability? Can it be an existing office or must it be newly formed?

manifests.description: Requires the Department of Defense to provide public access to its commercial item determinations so that offerors and the general public can review prior decisions, supporting transparency and consistent application.
manifests.subject: Public access to commercial item determinations
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Include requirement for public posting of commercial item determinations in DFARS; address handling/redaction of sensitive or proprietary data.
open_questions: What level of detail must be included in public postings?
How will proprietary and source selection sensitive information be protected?

manifests.description: Allows contracting officers to presume that a prior commercial item determination by any DoD component applies to subsequent procurements of the same item, aiming to reduce redundant or conflicting determinations and streamline acquisition.
manifests.subject: Presumption of prior commercial item determination applicability
manifests.applies_to: DoD contracting officers
manifests.conditions: When a prior commercial item determination was made by a DoD military department, Defense Agency, or other component for the same item.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS 215.403-1 and related procedures to incorporate this presumption, referencing a centralized database.
open_questions: Does the age or context of the previous determination (e.g., different program or acquisition strategy) affect the presumption?

manifests.description: Requires that if a contracting officer chooses not to rely on a prior DoD commercial item determination and instead treats the item as noncommercial, the officer must request a review of that decision by the head of the contracting activity before proceeding.
manifests.subject: Review process for overriding prior commercial item status
manifests.applies_to: DoD contracting officers
manifests.conditions: When not presuming commercial item status despite a prior DoD determination for the same item.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Require head of contracting activity review in DFARS for decisions not to follow precedent commercial item determinations.
open_questions: What documentation is necessary for the request and for the review outcome?

manifests.description: Sets a 30-day deadline for the head of contracting activity to either confirm the appropriateness/applicability of the prior commercial item determination or issue a revised determination with written explanation if the prior finding is not upheld.
manifests.subject: Timeline for review of commercial item status
manifests.applies_to: Heads of contracting activities within DoD
manifests.conditions: When review is requested after a contracting officer does not apply the prior commercial item status.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Specify 30-day limit in DFARS for review and written determination.
open_questions: Are there consequences or appeal procedures if the timeline is exceeded?

manifests.description: Clarifies that nothing in this section or its amendments changes the meaning of 'commercial item' as defined for 10 U.S.C. 2464(a)(5), nor does it affect requirements of 2464(a)(3) or (c). This is a savings clause to prevent unintended changes to statutory definitions or core logistics capabilities requirements for maintenance and repair.
manifests.subject: Definition savings clause for commercial items
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense statutory and regulatory interpretations
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Annotate DFARS definition sections as needed to cross-reference this limitation.
open_questions: Could future changes to 2464(a)(5) or related sections cause inconsistent interpretation?

manifests.description: Requires that the DFARS be updated within 180 days of enactment to implement all requirements and amendments from this section, ensuring timely regulatory alignment and DoD compliance.
manifests.subject: DFARS regulations update deadline
manifests.applies_to: Defense Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement (DFARS) drafting authorities
manifests.conditions: Not later than 180 days after enactment of this Act.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Direct update to all relevant DFARS parts (especially Parts 212, 215, 227).

manifests.description: States that nothing in this section prohibits contracting officers from requiring a contractor to provide information sufficient to determine the reasonableness of price for a commercial item procurement, even if that information was not required for a prior buy. This preserves the contracting officer's discretion for price analysis.
manifests.subject: Preservation of price analysis authority for commercial items
manifests.applies_to: DoD contracting officers and contractors
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Cross-reference in DFARS 212, 215 and price analysis/policy guidance.
open_questions: Are there reasonable limits to what information may be requested under this authority, especially after prior determinations of commerciality?

sections.section_heading: AMENDMENTS TO OTHER TRANSACTION AUTHORITY.

manifests.description: Establishes a new statutory authority (10 U.S.C. 2371b) allowing DoD and designated officials to carry out prototype projects using Other Transaction Authority (OTA) for efforts directly relevant to military effectiveness, acquisition, or improvement of defense platforms, systems, components, or materials.
manifests.subject: Creation of prototype Other Transaction Authority (OTA)
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense, DARPA, military departments, and designated officials
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Create new DFARS subpart for prototype OTA, outlining scope, eligible officials, and project eligibility.
open_questions: Are there any additional required procedures for internal DoD implementation?

manifests.description: Sets required senior official approvals for high-dollar prototype OTA projects: $50M-$250M requires a senior procurement executive or director's approval; above $250M requires Under Secretary of Defense for AT&L's approval and advance congressional notification, with no further delegation permitted.
manifests.subject: Senior approval thresholds for OTA prototypes
manifests.applies_to: DoD prototype projects using OTA exceeding $50M
manifests.conditions: Varying written approvals and notifications required based on project value
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Define approval and notification procedures and documentation in DFARS chapter discussing prototype OTAs.
open_questions: Is there need for a standardized approval template or reporting process?

manifests.description: Mandates competitive procedures be used to the maximum extent practicable when entering OTA prototype agreements, but specifically exempts these projects from certain competitive requirements in 10 U.S.C. 2371(e).
manifests.subject: Competitive procedures for OTA prototypes
manifests.applies_to: DoD contracting officers entering prototype OTAs
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS should clarify competitive expectations for prototype OTAs, overriding older 2371(e) requirements.
open_questions: What exceptions are allowed to competition and how should these be documented?

manifests.description: Requires all prototype OTA agreements exceeding $5M to include a clause granting Comptroller General audit access, with specific exceptions based on contractor history and waivable by the contracting activity head upon notification to Congress and the Comptroller General.
manifests.subject: Comptroller General access to OTA prototype records
manifests.applies_to: Parties and entities to OTA prototype agreements over $5M
manifests.conditions: Exceptions for new entities and waiver with notification; time limit of 3 years after final payment
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Mandate inclusion of Comptroller General access clauses in OTA prototype agreements in DFARS.
open_questions: Should DoD develop a standard format for the required waiver notification?

manifests.description: Establishes conditions under which OTA prototype authority can be used, requiring participation by nontraditional defense contractors or small businesses, significant cost-sharing, or documented exceptional circumstances.
manifests.subject: Eligibility and cost-sharing criteria for prototype OTAs
manifests.applies_to: Officials entering DoD OTA prototype transactions
manifests.conditions: Must meet one of four criteria relating to participants or cost-sharing
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS should establish checklists and internal review requirements for compliance with eligibility and cost-sharing.
open_questions: How should agencies document their review of these requirements (forms, certifications)?

manifests.description: Modifies the statutory definition of 'nontraditional defense contractor' as an entity that has not performed any DoD contract/subcontract subject to full cost accounting standards for at least one year before solicitation.
manifests.subject: Update of definition: nontraditional defense contractor
manifests.applies_to: All parties seeking nontraditional status in DoD prototype OTA projects
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS section on definitions/representations to match the revised 10 U.S.C. 2302(9) text.
open_questions: Should offerors be required to certify to this eligibility in each proposal, and if so, what documentation is adequate?

manifests.description: Allows for an OTA prototype agreement to include provisions for noncompetitive follow-on production contracts or transactions, so long as the prototype was competitively awarded and successfully completed.
manifests.subject: Follow-on production contracting under prototype OTAs
manifests.applies_to: DoD and OTA agreement holders for eligible prototype projects
manifests.conditions: Prototype OTA must have used competition and be successfully completed
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add DFARS language authorizing and guiding non-competitive follow-on production awards from eligible OTAs.
open_questions: Is there a definition or standard for 'successfully completed' in this context? Who certifies this?

manifests.description: Authorizes OTA agreements or follow-on contracts to allow for prototypes or production items to be provided as Government-furnished equipment (GFE) to other contractors.
manifests.subject: Authority to transfer prototype/GFE items under OTA
manifests.applies_to: OTA prototype/follow-on agreement participants and DoD
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS should clarify when and how prototype and production items may become GFE under OTAs, with references to property accountability requirements.
open_questions: Are there additional controls needed on inventory, disposition, or risk of GFE provided under this authority?

manifests.description: Clarifies that OTA prototype agreements are deemed 'Federal agency procurements' for purposes of procurement integrity and ethics requirements in 41 U.S.C. chapter 21.
manifests.subject: Application of procurement ethics laws to OTA prototype agreements
manifests.applies_to: All parties to OTA prototype agreements under 10 U.S.C. 2371b
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS ethics and procurement integrity language should explicitly apply to OTA prototype actions.
open_questions: Does this require extra training or guidance for OTA agreement officers and participants?

manifests.description: Repeals Section 845 of NDAA 1994 (old prototype OTA authority), but provides a savings clause allowing existing agreements to be maintained and modified as needed to reflect current law.
manifests.subject: Repeal of obsolete Section 845 OTA authority
manifests.applies_to: Legacy OTA prototype agreements under Section 845
manifests.conditions: Agreements remain in force but may be modified to reflect current law
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove or update any DFARS references to Section 845; ensure guidance on transitioning existing agreements.
open_questions: How will DoD track and manage conversion of legacy agreements?

manifests.description: Makes conforming amendments to update the chapter 139 table of sections for the new 10 U.S.C. 2371b and to correct references in other laws and notes as needed.
manifests.subject: Clerical and conforming statutory amendments
manifests.applies_to: Legal references, agencies using ch. 139 authorities
manifests.effective_date: Upon enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure DFARS statutory references for OTA prototype authority are aligned.

manifests.description: Directs the Secretary of Defense to issue updated guidance implementing these OTA authority changes within 180 days.
manifests.subject: Requirement for updated implementing guidance
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense, all DoD acquisition and contracting officials
manifests.conditions: Deadline: within 180 days of the NDAA's enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Coordinate DFARS changes with the forthcoming DoD guidance.
open_questions: Will interim DFARS rules be necessary or will updates await DoD guidance?

manifests.description: Requires the Secretary of Defense to provide a report to Congress within 180 days assessing the risks/benefits of allowing not-for-profit contractors to participate in cost-sharing under OTA, and the implications of removing cost-sharing requirements.
manifests.subject: Congressional report on OTA cost-sharing and not-for-profit eligibility
manifests.applies_to: Secretary of Defense, congressional defense committees
manifests.conditions: Due within 180 days of enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: None immediately, but future rulemaking could be needed depending on report findings.

sections.section_heading: APPLICABILITY OF COST AND PRICING DATA AND CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENTS.

manifests.description: Add a new exception to the requirement for contractors, subcontractors, or offerors to submit certified cost or pricing data for Department of Defense contracts (Truth in Negotiations requirements). This new exception applies when the data relate to an offset agreement in connection with a contract for the sale of a weapon system or defense-related item to a foreign country or foreign firm, as long as the data does not pertain to a contract or subcontract under that offset agreement for work performed in the foreign country or by the foreign firm that is directly related to the item being purchased. The effect is to reduce the submission/certification burden for offset-related costs in international sales, except where the work is directly related and performed in/by the purchasing country or firm.
manifests.subject: Exception to certified cost or pricing data requirements for certain offset agreements in Foreign Military Sales
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracting officers, contractors, subcontractors, and offerors involved in contracts for sales to foreign countries or firms with offset agreements
manifests.conditions: Applies only to cost or pricing data relating to an offset agreement for weapon system/defense item sales to foreign parties, and only to the extent the data does not relate to work under the offset performed in/by the purchasing country/firm directly related to the purchased item.
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 215.403-1(b) – add explicit exception for cost or pricing data related to offset agreements on FMS contracts per amended 10 U.S.C. 2306a(b)(1)(D); ensure consistency with treatment of FMS/offsets in 225.7303-2.
open_questions: Should DoD clarify in DFARS or PGI the process for determining if cost or pricing data relate strictly to non-domestic work under an offset agreement?
Should the term 'offset agreement' be defined by reference to ITAR, and do contracting officers need further guidance on implementation?

sections.section_heading: TEMPORARY EXTENSION OF AND AMENDMENTS TO TEST PROGRAM FOR NEGOTIATION OF COMPREHENSIVE SMALL BUSINESS SUBCONTRACTING PLANS.

manifests.description: Extends the duration of the DoD test program for negotiation of comprehensive small business subcontracting plans from December 31, 2014, to December 31, 2017.
manifests.subject: Extension of Test Program
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense, contractors participating in test program
manifests.effective_date: December 31, 2014
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update expiration date in DFARS 219.702 and clause 252.219-7004.

manifests.description: Raises the minimum threshold for contracts covered by the comprehensive subcontracting plan reporting requirement from $5,000,000 to $100,000,000.
manifests.subject: Contract Value Threshold
manifests.applies_to: Contractors with comprehensive subcontracting plans
manifests.conditions: When contract is for maintenance, overhaul, repair, servicing, rehabilitation, salvage, modernization, or modification of supplies, systems, or equipment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise threshold language in DFARS 252.219-7004 and related guidance.

manifests.description: Expands and clarifies the reporting requirements under the test program, mandating semiannual submission of detailed reports regarding first-tier subcontract dollars, the total number of such subcontracts, and costs related to negotiating and managing the comprehensive subcontracting plans.
manifests.subject: Reporting Requirements
manifests.applies_to: Contractors with comprehensive subcontracting plans
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update reporting clauses and requirements in DFARS 252.219-7004 and PGI as appropriate.
open_questions: Can the eSRS system accommodate all newly required categories without update?

manifests.description: Failure to make a good faith effort to comply with the comprehensive subcontracting plan will now be considered as a factor in past performance evaluations for future contract awards.
manifests.subject: Consequences for Failure to Meet Goals
manifests.applies_to: Contractors under comprehensive subcontracting plan test program
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Cross-reference or expand DFARS 215.305 to make this explicit for contracts under the test program.
open_questions: Does the past performance factor apply only to DoD, or all federal agencies using comprehensive plans?

manifests.description: Repeals Section 402 of Public Law 101-574, which suspended the application of liquidated damages for failure to comply with comprehensive subcontracting plans.
manifests.subject: Repeal of Suspension of Damages
manifests.applies_to: Contractors under comprehensive subcontracting plan test program
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove DFARS 219.702(2) language exempting test program contractors from liquidated damages.
open_questions: Should damages now be imposed retroactively for failures during the suspension period?

manifests.description: Prohibits the Secretary of Defense from negotiating a comprehensive subcontracting plan with a contractor for a fiscal year if that contractor, in the prior year, did not meet its negotiated subcontracting goals.
manifests.subject: Eligibility Restriction for Test Program
manifests.applies_to: Contractors seeking comprehensive subcontracting plans
manifests.conditions: If contractor did not meet negotiated goals in prior fiscal year (starting FY16 forward)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add eligibility requirements to the guidance/provisions for 252.219-7004 and DFARS 219.702.
open_questions: What process and standard of proof is required for making this determination?

manifests.description: Requires the Comptroller General to submit a report on the results of the test program to the committees on Armed Services and on Small Business in both chambers by September 30, 2015.
manifests.subject: Congressional Reporting Requirement
manifests.applies_to: Comptroller General
manifests.effective_date: September 30, 2015

manifests.description: Expands the statutory definition of 'covered small business concern' for purposes of the test program to explicitly include VOSBs, SDVOSBs, HUBZone firms, SDBs, and WOSBs as defined in the Small Business Act.
manifests.subject: Definitions - Covered Small Business Concern
manifests.applies_to: All references to small business concerns in the test program
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise DFARS clause 252.219-7004 and related definitions sections.

manifests.description: Updates references throughout the test program to use the new term 'covered small business concerns' instead of 'small business concerns and small business concerns owned and controlled by socially and economically disadvantaged individuals.'
manifests.subject: Conforming Amendment - Terminology
manifests.applies_to: DoD comprehensive subcontracting plan test program
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update terminology in all relevant DFARS provisions and clauses.

sections.section_heading: LIMITATIONS ON SUBCONTRACTING.

manifests.description: Creates a new section (15 U.S.C. 657s) in the Small Business Act, which establishes unified, statutory limitations on how much of a set-aside small business contract may be subcontracted out by the awardee under all major small business contract programs.
manifests.subject: Codification of government-wide small business subcontracting limits
manifests.applies_to: Covered small business concerns awarded set-aside contracts (8(a), WOSB, HUBZone, SDVOSB, etc.)
manifests.conditions: Award of contract under specified small business programs
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Create a DFARS cross-reference to 15 U.S.C. 657s and update related parts (e.g., 219.704, 252.219-7003).

manifests.description: Standardizes the percentage limitations: covered small businesses may not subcontract out more than 50% of the value of the contract for services or supplies (less cost of materials, as appropriate). For certain supply contracts, the product provided must be from a domestic small business manufacturer unless waived by SBA. For mixed contracts, special rules clarify the applicable limitation.
manifests.subject: Unification/update of percentage limitations
manifests.applies_to: Covered small business concerns awarded contracts for services or supplies under small business programs
manifests.conditions: Thresholds apply as described; separate rules for service, supply, and mixed contracts
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise limitation on subcontracting clauses/DFARS 219.704/252.219-7003 and ensure all small business programs use unified thresholds.
open_questions: How should mixed contracts be handled in current DFARS implementation—should this formula be adopted as-is or adapted?

manifests.description: Excludes from the subcontracting limitation any contract amounts expended on a 'similarly situated entity' (i.e., subcontractors with the same program status as the prime). This allows small businesses to team without penalty for compliance.
manifests.subject: Exclusion for 'similarly situated entities'
manifests.applies_to: Covered small business concerns and their subcontractors
manifests.conditions: Subcontractor is a 'similarly situated entity' as defined
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Clarify in limitation on subcontracting clauses that expenditures to similarly situated entities are excluded from the 50% calculation.
open_questions: Does DFARS or FAR require additional compliance/verification procedures for 'similarly situated entity' status?

manifests.description: Authorizes the SBA Administrator, via rulemaking, to revise the percentage (e.g., the 50% limitation) for any designated program or industry category to reflect normal industry practice, based on public input and evidence.
manifests.subject: Regulatory authority to modify percentage thresholds
manifests.applies_to: Small business programs covered by subsection (a); SBA
manifests.conditions: Administrator publishes rule after notice and comment, and determines adjustment reflects industry practice
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Instruct DFARS to incorporate or trigger updates automatically when SBA changes the percentage by rule.
open_questions: What is the timeline for DoD/DFARS implementation after an SBA rule change?

manifests.description: Requires SBA to establish, by public rulemaking, analogous percentage-based limitations on subcontracting for construction contracts, specialty construction, and any other industry not specifically covered in the statute.
manifests.subject: Regulatory authority for construction and excluded industries
manifests.applies_to: SBA, covered small business contracts in construction/other categories
manifests.conditions: SBA must conduct rulemaking
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS should track SBA rules and ensure newly covered industries/contract types are added to requirements.
open_questions: Are there interim limitations for these categories before SBA regulations are finalized?

manifests.description: Adds statutory definitions for 'covered small business concern' (specifying what types of small businesses/ownership qualify under which programs) and for 'similarly situated entity' (what type of subcontractor qualifies for the subcontracting limitation exclusion).
manifests.subject: Definitions for compliance/enforcement
manifests.applies_to: All parties implementing small business set-aside programs
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add cross-reference and usage of new definitions in the corresponding DFARS parts/clauses.
open_questions: What verification process will DoD require to confirm 'similarly situated' entity status?

manifests.description: By enacting the new 15 U.S.C. 657s and establishing it as the government-wide standard for limitations on subcontracting, this section repeals or supplants the prior more scattered and inconsistent statutory limitations found in 15 U.S.C. 637(a)(14) and 15 U.S.C. 644(o).
manifests.subject: Repeal of previously codified limitations, unification under single standard
manifests.applies_to: All agencies and covered small business contractors
manifests.conditions: Supersession by statute
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Remove or update all references to 637(a)(14) and 644(o) in DFARS and use 15 U.S.C. 657s for all limitation on subcontracting requirements.
open_questions: Are contracts awarded under the prior statutes 'grandfathered,' or does the new standard apply retroactively to current contracts in performance?

sections.section_heading: <<NOTE: 10 USC 2306a note. Deadlines.>> GUIDANCE AND TRAINING RELATED TO EVALUATING REASONABLENESS OF PRICE.

manifests.description: Requires the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics to issue, within 180 days, guidance to the Department of Defense on using the authorities in 10 U.S.C. 2306a(d) and 10 U.S.C. 2379 to evaluate price reasonableness. The guidance must establish standards for when prior sales prices are adequate, how much uncertified cost information to require when price information is lacking, require that such data be provided in the form kept by the offeror, and prohibit additional cost info requests when sufficient non-Government sales exist.
manifests.subject: Issuance of price reasonableness guidance for commercial items
manifests.applies_to: Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics and the Department of Defense acquisition workforce
manifests.conditions: Action must be completed within 180 days after the date of enactment of this Act
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: DFARS 215.403-1(c)(3) and PGI 215.403-1 should be updated or supplemented to include new standards and rules on price reasonableness for commercial item acquisitions.
open_questions: What definition will be used for 'sufficient' non-Government sales?
How prescriptive should the standards for adequacy of previous sales price information be, given the variety of acquisitions across DoD?

manifests.description: Requires, within 270 days, the development and implementation of a plan to train the acquisition workforce in using the authorities in 10 U.S.C. 2306a(d) and 10 U.S.C. 2379 for evaluating price reasonableness in commercial item procurements. Also requires development of a cadre of experts to support and advise the acquisition workforce on these standards and their application.
manifests.subject: Training and development of expertise on commercial item pricing under 10 U.S.C. 2306a(d) and 2379
manifests.applies_to: Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology, and Logistics; acquisition workforce
manifests.conditions: Action must be developed and implementation begun within 270 days after enactment
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Cross-reference DFARS Subpart 212 and PGI 215.402; possibly new section for workforce training and expert support requirements.
open_questions: What will be the content, scope, and delivery methods for the training?
What criteria should be used to qualify the internal cadre of experts?

manifests.description: Requires all DoD requests for uncertified cost information to be fully documented in the contract file, including the justification for requesting additional cost information, a copy of the request, and any contractor response (including justification for not providing information, if applicable).
manifests.subject: Documentation of requests for uncertified cost data in price analysis
manifests.applies_to: DoD contracting officers and officials requesting cost information from contractors
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update PGI 215.403-3 to include mandatory contract file documentation checklist for these data requests.
open_questions: Are there any privacy, proprietary, or CUI restrictions that would affect recording contractor responses in the contract file?

manifests.description: Requires the Comptroller General to conduct a review of data collected under 10 U.S.C. 2306a(d) and 2379 during the two years following enactment, and to submit a report to the congressional defense committees within 180 days after that period. The report must address DoD's need for additional cost data, compliance with issued guidance, contractor refusals, and recommendations for improvement.
manifests.subject: Comptroller General review and congressional report on use of cost data for price reasonableness
manifests.applies_to: Comptroller General, Department of Defense, congressional defense committees
manifests.conditions: Covers two-year period post-enactment; report due 180 days after period ends
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: May require internal reporting/data collection mechanisms on cost/price analysis for commercial items in DFARS or PGI.
open_questions: Will recommendations in the Comptroller General’s report trigger further required DFARS or statutory changes?

sections.section_heading: RIGHTS IN TECHNICAL DATA AND VALIDATION OF PROPRIETARY DATA RESTRICTIONS.

manifests.description: Expands the circumstances under which the government may release technical data subject to restrictions—not just for emergency repair or foreign release, but also when necessary to segregate or reintegrate items or processes (or their equivalents) for broader procurement, maintenance, or engineering purposes.
manifests.subject: Government release/use of contractor technical data
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and defense contractors providing technical data
manifests.conditions: When technical data is necessary for segregation or reintegration of items or processes (or their equivalents)
manifests.effective_date: December 31, 2011
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update 252.227-7013 and related DFARS guidance to explicitly state these new grounds for compulsory or permitted release of technical data.

manifests.description: Sets a firm rule that the government receives government purpose rights in technical data developed with mixed funding unless the Secretary of Defense specifically determines, under set criteria, that negotiating different rights is in the best interest of the United States.
manifests.subject: Government purpose rights for mixed-funding technical data
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts with technical data funded both privately and with federal funds
manifests.conditions: Secretary of Defense must determine, based on established criteria, that different rights are in the best interest of the United States to negotiate otherwise.
manifests.effective_date: December 31, 2011
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure DFARS and contract clauses treat mixed-funding technical data as government purpose rights by default, unless exceptional negotiation criteria are met.
open_questions: What are the specific criteria for the Secretary's determination of best interest?

manifests.description: Streamlines the definition for 'Federal funds' as it affects the treatment of technical data rights, ensuring consistent interpretations for both private and federal expenditures.
manifests.subject: Definition of Federal funds for technical data rights
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and defense contractors/subcontractors
manifests.effective_date: January 7, 2011
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Review/align DFARS definitions and procedures concerning indirect costs, IR&D, bid/proposal costs, and what counts as 'Federal funds' for technical data rights.
open_questions: How does this affect current DoD practice on treatment of IR&D and similar costs?

manifests.description: Authorizes the US government to require delivery (at any time) of certain technical data generated or used under a contract—even if not originally specified—provided the government compensates only for reasonable conversion/delivery costs, if the data are needed for reprocurement, sustainment, modification, or upgrade of covered systems/items and meet other specified conditions.
manifests.subject: Right to require delivery of additional technical data
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and defense contractors/subcontractors with technical data generated or utilized under a contract
manifests.conditions: A determination that the data is needed for reprocurement, sustainment, modification, or upgrade of a covered system/item, and the data pertains to items/processes developed with federal funds or necessary for integration/segregation
manifests.effective_date: December 31, 2011
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Implement as a new rights/remedies clause or subprovision in 252.227-7013/DFARS 227.7103, specifying procedures, eligible uses, and compensation limits.
open_questions: How are 'reasonable costs' determined? What is the scope of 'generated or utilized'?

manifests.description: Specifies that the government does not lose the right to require delivery of technical data merely because it failed to challenge a contractor's restrictive marking within the statutory challenge period.
manifests.subject: Government right to require delivery of technical data after challenge period
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense acquisition officials
manifests.conditions: When enforcing delivery of technical data, even if restrictive markings were not challenged within six years
manifests.effective_date: December 31, 2011
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Add clarifying language in DFARS and contract clauses (252.227-7037) to ensure agencies are aware they retain this right.
open_questions: How does this interact with contractor remedies/compensation and disputes?

manifests.description: Extends from three to six years the period during which the government may challenge a contractor's asserted restriction on use or release of technical data delivered under a DoD contract.
manifests.subject: Validation/challenge window for technical data restrictions
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and defense contractors/subcontractors
manifests.conditions: Challenge period is now within 6 years of final payment or data delivery (whichever is later)
manifests.effective_date: December 31, 2011
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Revise 252.227-7037 validation clause and DFARS challenge procedures to allow for a six-year challenge window.
open_questions: How does this apply to contracts already in process or previously closed?

manifests.description: Adds 'fraudulently asserted restrictions' as an explicit ground for the government to challenge a technical data restriction at any time, even after the normal six-year window.
manifests.subject: Exception for challenging fraudulently marked technical data
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and defense contractors/subcontractors
manifests.conditions: If the asserted restriction was made fraudulently
manifests.effective_date: December 31, 2011
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure challenge guidance and contract language reflect this no time bar for fraud-based challenges.
open_questions: How is 'fraudulently asserted' defined and determined in practice?

manifests.description: Removes obsolete provision (earlier subparagraph (C)) in 10 U.S.C. 2321 that described an additional exception to the statutory limitation period for challenges, streamlining the structure and deferring to the new provisions.
manifests.subject: Deletion of obsolete exception provision
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense and defense contractors/subcontractors
manifests.effective_date: December 31, 2011
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Update DFARS to remove references to the deleted exception.

manifests.description: Applies the statutory amendments prospectively from the enactment date, with one technical provision relating to definitions taking effect on January 7, 2011, to coordinate with the prior NDAA.
manifests.subject: Effective dates for amendments
manifests.applies_to: Department of Defense contracts and acquisition personnel
manifests.effective_date: December 31, 2011 (general); January 7, 2011 for the amendment to 2320(a)(3)
manifests.dfars_implementation_hint: Ensure regulatory text and contract clause dates match effective dates and address transition issues for in-process contracts.
open_questions: How should contracts executed between January 7, 2011 and this section's enactment be treated regarding the technical amendment?
